verse.original: महाधनसेट्ठिपुत्तवत्थु अचरित्वा ब्रह्मचरियं, अलद्धा योब्बने धनं; जिण्णकोञ्चाव झायन्ति, खीणमच्छेव पल्लले।
verse.transliteration: Mahādhanaseṭṭhiputtavatthu Acaritvā brahmacariyaṁ, aladdhā yobbane dhanaṁ; Jiṇṇakoñcāva jhāyanti, khīṇamaccheva pallale.
verse.translation: When young they spurned the spiritual path and failed to earn any wealth. Now they brood like old cranes in a pond bereft of fish.

meaning.word_by_word: अचरित्वा (not having lived), ब्रह्मचरियं (spiritual life), अलद्धा (not having gained), योब्बने (in youth), धनं (wealth); जिण्णकोञ्चाव [जिण्ण-कोञ्चा-इव] (like old herons), झायन्ति (they pine away), खीणमत्स्ये [खीण-मच्छे] (depleted of fish), पल्लले (in a lake)
meaning.overall: When young they spurned the spiritual path and failed to earn any wealth. Now they brood like old cranes in a pond bereft of fish.

interpretation.traditional: In the Jarāvagga (Chapter on Old Age), the Buddha presents stark contemplations on aging, decay, and mortality (maranasati). The physical body, once beautiful and strong, is shown to be a fragile house of bones destined for destruction. Traditional commentary states that these teachings are meant to shatter physical vanity and push the practitioner to seek the deathless state (Nibbana) before it is too late.
interpretation.core_principle: Understanding karma, practicing empathy, confronting impermanence, and taming the self are the keys to peace.
interpretation.psychological: Denial of aging and death creates a subterranean anxiety that drives excessive consumerism and status-chasing. Intentionally confronting mortality (memento mori) reduces vanity, fosters gratitude for the present, and shifts values from superficiality to depth.

explanation.simple: The body is fragile and constantly aging. It will one day break down and return to the earth. Do not waste your life chasing empty pleasures; search instead for the lasting truth that never dies.
explanation.deep: Chapter 11 is a powerful call to face impermanence. We build our lives around physical beauty, sensory pleasures, and worldly achievements, ignoring the reality that the body is decaying every second. The Buddha compares the aging body to worn-out royal chariots, a nest of disease, and grey bones scattered in autumn. Recognizing this truth cuts off pride and fuels the search for the inner, undying truth.

logic.premise: Every action shapes character; inner discipline determines happiness and freedom.
logic.observation: The mind has a natural tendency to wander, but taming it leads to a secure refuge.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, we must actively cultivate virtue and mindfulness.

reasoning.flow: Awareness of Truth → Ethical Intent → Mindful Self-Regulation → Inner Peace → Liberation

tension.human_doubt: How do I stay committed to inner growth when outer demands are constant?
tension.resolution: View daily tasks as opportunities for mindfulness and kindness; there is no separate spiritual compartment.

contrast.ignorance: Chasing transient pleasure, ignoring decay, and neglecting self-taming.
contrast.wisdom: Guarding one's character, facing mortality directly, and being one's own refuge.

verses.analogy: Like an artisan carefully shaping their masterpiece from raw stone.
verses.real_life: A person who calmly accepts feedback, owns their errors, and works to improve their reactions daily.

actions: Observe your physical body today with gratitude for its current function, while recognizing its impermanence.
Look at a photo of yourself from years ago, reflecting on the constant river of change.
reflection_questions: How much of my time is spent maintaining a temporary physical image?
What is the 'house-builder' of my desires that I need to dismantle?

perspectives.spiritual: The spiritual path is a journey of gradual purification and self-awakening.
perspectives.philosophical: True autonomy is mastery of one's own desires and actions.
perspectives.practical: Set daily routines that support physical health and mental clarity.
perspectives.leadership: A mature leader guides with empathy and takes personal responsibility for failures.

similar_ideas.idea: There is no purifier in this world comparable to transcendental knowledge.
Wisdom is the principal thing; therefore get wisdom: and with all thy getting get understanding.

verses.insight: The body is a temporary guest-house; do not mistake it for your home.

verse.original: अचरित्वा ब्रह्मचरियं, अलद्धा योब्बने धनं; सेन्ति चापातिखीणाव, पुराणानि अनुत्थुनं। जरावग्गो एकादसमो।
verse.transliteration: Acaritvā brahmacariyaṁ, aladdhā yobbane dhanaṁ; Senti cāpātikhīṇāva, purāṇāni anutthunaṁ. Jarāvaggo ekādasamo.
verse.translation: When young they spurned the spiritual path and failed to earn any wealth. Now they lie like spent arrows, bemoaning over things past.

meaning.word_by_word: अचरित्वा (not having lived), ब्रह्मचरियं (spiritual life), अलद्धा (not having gained), योब्बने (in youth), धनं (wealth); सेन्ति (they lie), चापातिखीणाव [चापा अतो खीणा इव] (like worn-out arrows shot from a bow), सखायन्ति [सोचन्ति] (sighing/mourning), पुराणानि (over the past)
meaning.overall: When young they spurned the spiritual path and failed to earn any wealth. Now they lie like spent arrows, bemoaning over things past.

verse.original: खुद्दकनिकाय धम्मपद अत्तवग्ग बोधिराजकुमारवत्थु अत्तानञ्चे पियं जञ्ञा, रक्खेय्य नं सुरक्खितं; तिण्णं अञ्ञतरं यामं, पटिजग्गेय्य पण्डितो।
verse.transliteration: Khuddakanikāya Dhammapada Attavagga Bodhirājakumāravatthu Attānañce piyaṁ jaññā, rakkheyya naṁ surakkhitaṁ; Tiṇṇaṁ aññataraṁ yāmaṁ, paṭijaggeyya paṇḍito.
verse.translation: Minor Collection Sayings of the Dhamma 157–166 12. The Self If you knew your self as beloved, you’d look after it so well. In one of the night’s three watches, an astute person would remain alert.

meaning.word_by_word: अत्तानञ्-चे (oneself if), पियं (dear), जञ्ञा (one knows), रक्खेय्य (one should guard), नं (oneself), सुरक्खितं (diligently/well-guarded); तिण्णं (of the three), यामं (watches of the night), पटिजागेय्य (the wise should stay awake), पण्डितो (wise person)
meaning.overall: If you hold yourself dear, you should guard yourself diligently. During at least one of the three watches of the night, the wise should stay awake.

interpretation.traditional: Theravada commentary explains that 'guarding oneself' means guarding one's virtue and mind from defilements. The 'three watches of life' (youth, middle age, old age) are also implied: at least in one phase of life, one must awaken and cultivate the spiritual path.
interpretation.core_principle: True self-love is shown not through sensory indulgence, but through protecting one's character and virtue; we must devote our active energy to spiritual awakening.
interpretation.psychological: Guarding the self is self-regulation. True self-care is not pampering the ego, but protecting the mind from toxic thoughts, habits, and speech that cause long-term suffering.

explanation.simple: If you really care about yourself, you must guard your mind and actions carefully. During at least one part of your life or your night, stay awake and remain alert.
explanation.deep: Guarding oneself ('rakkheyya naṁ surakkhitaṁ') is the highest form of self-love. If we love ourselves, we do not feed ourselves poison (anger, greed, distraction). The 'three watches' (yāmaṁ) literally refer to the divisions of the night for meditation, but also symbolically represent the stages of life: youth, maturity, and old age. If you wasted your youth, awaken in middle age; if middle age is gone, do not delay in old age. The wise person ('paṇḍito') knows that time is limited.

logic.premise: Character and peace of mind are precious assets that can be easily corrupted by negative influences.
logic.observation: Guarding these assets requires active vigilance (staying awake/alert).
logic.conclusion: Therefore, if one truly loves oneself, one must practice continuous vigilance to protect one's moral and spiritual integrity.

reasoning.flow: Holding Self Dear → Understanding Real Welfare → Guarding Virtue → Awakening to Truth

tension.human_doubt: Isn't guarding myself selfish? Shouldn't I focus on guarding and helping others?
tension.resolution: You cannot protect others if you are unprotected. A person who is drowning cannot save another drowning person. Self-protection through virtue is the prerequisite for helping others.

contrast.ignorance: Equating self-love with self-indulgence (eating, drinking, and spending money), which actually harms health and character.
contrast.wisdom: Recognizing that your mind is your most precious possession, and guarding it from greed, anger, and delusion.

verses.analogy: Like a valuable treasure chest that must be locked and guarded against thieves.
verses.real_life: A person who decides to step away from a gossip session because they know it will poison their mind with judgment.

actions: Identify one negative habit that regularly drains your mental energy and put a guard against it today.
Dedicate the last 30 minutes of your day to silent reflection or reading, rather than screen time.
reflection_questions: Am I truly loving myself, or am I just indulging my impulses?
How can I stay more awake and vigilant in my life?

perspectives.spiritual: Guarding the doors of the senses (indriya-samvara) is a key stage of training.
perspectives.philosophical: The concept of 'care of the self' (cura sui) in Foucault and ancient philosophy.
perspectives.practical: Establish digital boundaries (like app blockers) to guard your attention from manipulation.
perspectives.leadership: A leader must guard their own integrity and energy; if they burn out or compromise their ethics, the whole team suffers.

similar_ideas.idea: One must elevate oneself by one's own mind, and not degrade oneself; for the mind is the friend of the self, and the mind is the enemy of the self.
Keep your heart with all vigilance, for from it flow the springs of life.

verses.insight: To love yourself is to keep your mind pure.

verse.original: उपनन्दसक्यपुत्तत्थेरवत्थु अत्तानमेव पठमं, पतिरूपे निवेसये; अथञ्ञमनुसासेय्य, न किलिस्सेय्य पण्डितो।
verse.transliteration: Upanandasakyaputtattheravatthu Attānameva paṭhamaṁ, patirūpe nivesaye; Athaññamanusāseyya, na kilisseyya paṇḍito.
verse.translation: The astute would avoid being corrupted by first grounding themselves in what is suitable, and then instructing others.

meaning.word_by_word: अत्तानम्-एव (oneself indeed), पठमं (first), पतिरूपे (in what is proper), निवेसये (should establish); अथञ्ञम्-अनुसासेय्य [अथ अञ्ञं अनुसासेय्य] (then others one should instruct), न (not), किलिस्सेय्य (should not be defiled/suffer), पण्डितो (the wise person)
meaning.overall: The astute would avoid being corrupted by first grounding themselves in what is suitable, and then instructing others.

interpretation.traditional: In the Attavagga (Chapter on the Self), the Buddha clarifies the true meaning of self-love and self-reliance (atta-sarana). True self-love is not ego-indulgence, but guarding oneself through virtue and wisdom. Traditional Theravada commentary stresses that each individual is responsible for their own purification and liberation; no external savior can perform the work of taming the mind.
interpretation.core_principle: Understanding karma, practicing empathy, confronting impermanence, and taming the self are the keys to peace.
interpretation.psychological: Taking full responsibility for our own mental state (internal locus of control) is the cornerstone of psychological maturity. It frees us from victimhood, blame, and dependency, allowing us to actively shape our character and experience.

explanation.simple: You are your own master and your own helper. Keep your mind and actions pure, because no one else can do that work for you. Train yourself, and you will find a rare, steady peace.
explanation.deep: Chapter 12 outlines the radical autonomy of the Buddhist path. It emphasizes that we are our own refuge and our own master ('attā hi attano nātho'). A wild, untamed mind is our greatest enemy, while a disciplined mind is our only secure refuge. The Buddha teaches that purity and impurity are entirely individual matters; no one can purify another. We must do the work of self-training.

logic.premise: Every action shapes character; inner discipline determines happiness and freedom.
logic.observation: The mind has a natural tendency to wander, but taming it leads to a secure refuge.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, we must actively cultivate virtue and mindfulness.

reasoning.flow: Awareness of Truth → Ethical Intent → Mindful Self-Regulation → Inner Peace → Liberation

tension.resolution: View daily tasks as opportunities for mindfulness and kindness; there is no separate spiritual compartment.

contrast.ignorance: Chasing transient pleasure, ignoring decay, and neglecting self-taming.
contrast.wisdom: Guarding one's character, facing mortality directly, and being one's own refuge.

verses.analogy: Like an artisan carefully shaping their masterpiece from raw stone.
verses.real_life: A person who calmly accepts feedback, owns their errors, and works to improve their reactions daily.

actions: Take full responsibility for your emotional reaction to a situation today, refusing to blame anyone else.
Establish a clear limit or boundary for your behavior today in the spirit of self-protection.
reflection_questions: Am I waiting for someone or something else to make me happy or peaceful?
How would my life change if I accepted that I am entirely responsible for my own mind?

perspectives.spiritual: The spiritual path is a journey of gradual purification and self-awakening.
perspectives.philosophical: True autonomy is mastery of one's own desires and actions.
perspectives.practical: Set daily routines that support physical health and mental clarity.
perspectives.leadership: A mature leader guides with empathy and takes personal responsibility for failures.

similar_ideas.idea: The mind is restless and hard to curb, but it can be controlled by practice.
Indeed, Allah will not change the condition of a people until they change what is in themselves.

verses.insight: You are the sculptor, and your life is the marble; carve it with care.

verse.original: पधानिकतिस्सत्थेरवत्थु अत्तानञ्चे तथा कयिरा, यथाञ्ञमनुसासति; सुदन्तो वत दमेथ, अत्ता हि किर दुद्दमो।
verse.transliteration: Padhānikatissattheravatthu Attānañce tathā kayirā, yathāññamanusāsati; Sudanto vata dametha, attā hi kira duddamo.
verse.translation: If one were to treat oneself as one instructs another, the well-tamed indeed would tame: for the self, it seems, is hard to tame.

meaning.word_by_word: अत्तानञ्-चे (oneself if), तथा (so), कयिरा (should do), यथा (just as), अञ्ञम्-अनुसासति (instructs others); सुदन्तो (well-tamed), वत (indeed), दमेथा (one should tame), अत्ता (oneself), हि (indeed), किर (they say), दुद्दमो (is hard to tame)
meaning.overall: If one were to treat oneself as one instructs another, the well-tamed indeed would tame: for the self, it seems, is hard to tame.

interpretation.traditional: In the Attavagga (Chapter on the Self), the Buddha clarifies the true meaning of self-love and self-reliance (atta-sarana). verse.original: कुमारकस्सपमातुत्थेरिवत्थु अत्ता हि अत्तनो नाथो, को हि नाथो परो सिया; अत्तना हि सुदन्तेन, नाथं लभति दुल्लभं।
verse.transliteration: Kumārakassapamātuttherivatthu Attā hi attano nātho, ko hi nātho paro siyā; Attanā hi sudantena, nāthaṁ labhati dullabhaṁ.
verse.translation: One is indeed the lord of oneself, for who else would be one’s lord? By means of a well-tamed self, one gains a lord that’s rare indeed.

meaning.word_by_word: अत्ता (self), हि (indeed), अत्तनो (of oneself), नाथो (lord/refuge), को (what), हि (indeed), नाथो (refuge), परो (other), सिया (could be); अत्तना (by oneself), हि (indeed), सुदन्तेन (well-tamed), नाथं (refuge), लभति (one obtains), दुल्लभं (difficult to obtain)
meaning.overall: Self is the refuge of self; what other refuge could there be? With a self well-tamed, one obtains a refuge that is hard to find.

interpretation.traditional: This is one of the most famous verses in the Dhammapada, establishing the core Buddhist principle of self-reliance (atta-sarana). No god, priest, or external savior can purify or liberate another person; salvation is entirely a matter of taming one's own mind.
interpretation.core_principle: The ultimate responsibility for our liberation and happiness lies within ourselves; a well-trained mind is our only true refuge.
interpretation.psychological: Developing an internal locus of control is essential for mental health. Relying on external circumstances, relationships, or authority figures for our sense of safety and value makes us fragile; inner mastery is the only stable foundation.

explanation.simple: You are your own master and your own refuge. Who else could be your refuge? If you train and control yourself well, you will find a rare and secure shelter that cannot be taken away.
explanation.deep: 'Attā hi attano nātho'—Self is indeed the refuge of self. The Buddha's teaching is radical. It cuts through the human tendency to look for a savior. We want someone else to carry our burdens. But the mind's defilements are ours, and the training must be ours. 'Sudantena' means well-tamed. When the mind is wild, it is our greatest enemy. When it is tamed, it becomes the ultimate refuge—a state of peace ('nāthaṁ dullabhaṁ') that no storm of the world can shake.

logic.premise: No external entity can transform your mind or experience for you.
logic.observation: External refuges are impermanent, vulnerable, and beyond your ultimate control.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, you must take full responsibility for taming your own mind, which is the only reliable and lasting refuge.

reasoning.flow: Search for External Refuge → Realization of its Limits → Turning Inward → Taming the Self → Finding the Ultimate Refuge

tension.human_doubt: Does this mean I should never ask for help or rely on friends, family, or teachers?
tension.resolution: Help, community, and teachers are valuable guides (as in Panditavagga). But they can only point the way; they cannot walk it for you. The food they offer cannot feed your hunger unless you chew and swallow it yourself.

contrast.ignorance: Hoping for a miracle, a savior, or a change in circumstances to solve your inner suffering.
contrast.wisdom: Taking full ownership of your reactions, emotions, and growth, knowing that you hold the key to your own freedom.

verses.analogy: Like a physician who can prescribe medicine, but the patient must take it to be cured.
verses.real_life: A person who stops blaming their childhood or their ex-partner for their current unhappiness and enters therapy with the attitude: 'I am here to change my own mind.'

actions: Identify one area where you are waiting for someone else to fix your problem, and take one action to address it yourself.
Sit quietly and feel the strength of your own presence as a refuge.
reflection_questions: Where am I still looking for an external 'saviour' in my life?
What would change if I lived with the absolute conviction that I am my own refuge?

perspectives.spiritual: The Buddha's final instruction: 'Be lamps unto yourselves, be refuges unto yourselves, take no external refuge.'
perspectives.philosophical: Existential self-reliance: we are condemned to be free and must create our own meaning (Sartre).
perspectives.practical: Take responsibility for your own skill development and career path rather than waiting for your employer to train you.
perspectives.leadership: A leader must be self-anchored and self-correcting, not dependent on the constant approval of their followers.

similar_ideas.idea: One must deliver oneself with the help of one's own mind, and not degrade oneself.
For each will have to bear his own load.

verses.insight: The door to your refuge opens from the inside.

verse.original: महाकालौपासकवत्थु अत्तना हि कतं पापं, अत्तजं अत्तसम्भवं; अभिमत्थति दुम्मेधं, वजिरंवस्ममयं मणिं।
verse.transliteration: Mahākālaupāsakavatthu Attanā hi kataṁ pāpaṁ, Attajaṁ attasambhavaṁ; Abhimatthati dummedhaṁ, Vajiraṁvasmamayaṁ maṇiṁ.
verse.translation: For the evil that is done by oneself, born and produced in oneself, grinds down a simpleton, as diamond grinds a lesser gem.

meaning.word_by_word: अत्तना (by oneself), व (indeed), कतं (done), पापं (evil), अत्तजं (self-born), अत्तसम्भवं (self-produced); अभिमन्थति (crushes), दुम्मेधं (the fool), वजिरं (diamond), व (like), अम्हमयं (stone/gem)
meaning.overall: For the evil that is done by oneself, born and produced in oneself, grinds down a simpleton, as diamond grinds a lesser gem.

verse.original: देवदत्तवत्थु यस्स अच्चन्तदुस्सिल्यं, मालुवा सालमिवोत्थतं; करोति सो तथत्तानं, यथा नं इच्छती दिसो।
verse.transliteration: Devadattavatthu Yassa accantadussilyaṁ, māluvā sālamivotthataṁ; Karoti so tathattānaṁ, yathā naṁ icchatī diso.
verse.translation: One choked by immorality, as a sal tree by a creeper, does to themselves what a foe only wishes.

meaning.word_by_word: यस्स (whose), अच्चन्त-दुस्सील्यं (extreme immorality), मालुवा (creeper), सालमिवोततं [सालम् इव ओततं] (spreads like a maluva creeper over a sala tree); करोति (does), सो (he), तथत्तानं (so to himself), यथा (just as), नं (him), इच्छति (wishes), दिसो (enemy)
meaning.overall: One choked by immorality, as a sal tree by a creeper, does to themselves what a foe only wishes.

interpretation.traditional: In the Attavagga (Chapter on the Self), the Buddha clarifies the true meaning of self-love and self-reliance (atta-sarana). True self-love is not ego-indulgence, but guarding oneself through virtue and wisdom. Traditional Theravada commentary stresses that each individual is responsible for their own purification and liberation; no external savior can perform the work of taming the mind.

verse.original: सङ्घभेदपरिसक्कनवत्थु सुकरानि असाधूनि, अत्तनो अहितानि च; यं वे हितञ्च साधुञ्च, तं वे परमदुक्करं।
verse.transliteration: Saṅghabhedaparisakkanavatthu Sukarāni asādhūni, attano ahitāni ca; Yaṁ ve hitañca sādhuñca, taṁ ve paramadukkaraṁ.
verse.translation: It’s easy to do bad things harmful to oneself, but good things that are helpful are the hardest things to do.

meaning.word_by_word: सुकरानि (easy to do), अहितानि (unbeneficial/bad things), अत्तनो (to oneself), च (and), दुक्खातानि (harmful/evil); यं (what), वे (indeed), हितञ्-च (beneficial and), साधुञ्-च (good and), तं (that), वे (indeed), परमदुद्दसं [परम-दुद्दसं] (is extremely difficult to do)
meaning.overall: It’s easy to do bad things harmful to oneself, but good things that are helpful are the hardest things to do.

verse.original: कालत्थेरवत्थु यो सासनं अरहतं, अरियानं धम्मजीविनं; पटिक्कोसति दुम्मेधो, दिट्ठिं निस्साय पापिकं; फलानि कट्ठकस्सेव, अत्तघाताय फल्लति।
verse.transliteration: Kālattheravatthu Yo sāsanaṁ arahataṁ, ariyānaṁ dhammajīvinaṁ; Paṭikkosati dummedho, diṭṭhiṁ nissāya pāpikaṁ; Phalāni kaṭṭhakasseva, attaghātāya phallati.
verse.translation: On account of wicked views— scorning the guidance of the perfected ones, the noble ones living righteously— the idiot begets their own self’s demise, like the bamboo bearing fruit.

meaning.word_by_word: यो (whoever), सासनं (teaching), अरहतं (of the Arahants), अरियानं (of the noble ones), धम्मजीविनं (living according to Dhamma); पटिक्कोसति (scoffs at/rejects), दुम्मेधो (the foolish one), दिट्ठिं (views), निस्साय (depending on), पापिकं (evil); फलन्ति (bears fruit), कट्ठकस्सेव [कट्ठकस्स इव] (like the bamboo), फल्लं (fruit), अत्तवधाय (for self-destruction)
meaning.overall: On account of wicked views— scorning the guidance of the perfected ones, the noble ones living righteously— the idiot begets their own self’s demise, like the bamboo bearing fruit.

verse.original: चूऌअकालौपासकवत्थु अत्तना हि कतं पापं, अत्तना सङ्किलिस्सति; अत्तना अकतं पापं, अत्तनाव विसुज्झति; सुद्धी असुद्धि पच्चत्तं, नाञ्ञो अञ्ञं विसोधये।
verse.transliteration: Cūḷakālaupāsakavatthu Attanā hi kataṁ pāpaṁ, attanā saṅkilissati; Attanā akataṁ pāpaṁ, attanāva visujjhati; Suddhī asuddhi paccattaṁ, nāñño aññaṁ visodhaye.
verse.translation: For it is by oneself that evil’s done, one is corrupted by oneself. It’s by oneself that evil’s not done, one is purified by oneself. Purity and impurity are personal matters, no one can purify another.

meaning.word_by_word: अत्तना (by oneself), व (indeed), कतं (done), पापं (evil), अत्तना (by oneself), सङ्किलिस्सति (one is defiled); अत्तना (by oneself), अकतं (undone), पापं (evil), अत्तना (by oneself), व (indeed), विस्सुज्झति (one is purified); सुद्धि (purity), असुद्धि (impurity), पच्चत्तं (is individual/personal), नाञ्ञो [न अञ्ञो] (not another), अञ्ञं (another), विसोधये (can purify)
meaning.overall: For it is by oneself that evil’s done, one is corrupted by oneself. It’s by oneself that evil’s not done, one is purified by oneself. Purity and impurity are personal matters, no one can purify another.

verse.original: अत्तदत्थत्थेरवत्थु अत्तदत्थं परत्थेन, बहुनापि न हापये; अत्तदत्थमभिञ्ञाय, सदत्थपसुतो सिया। अत्तवग्गो द्वादसमो।
verse.transliteration: Attadatthattheravatthu Attadatthaṁ paratthena, bahunāpi na hāpaye; Attadatthamabhiññāya, sadatthapasuto siyā. Attavaggo dvādasamo.
verse.translation: Never neglect what is good for yourself for the sake of another, however great. Knowing well what is good for yourself, be intent upon your heart’s goal.

meaning.word_by_word: अत्तदत्थं (one's own spiritual good), न (not), हापेय्य (neglect), परत्थेन (for another's good), बहुनापि (even if much); अत्तदत्थम्-अभिञ्ञाय (knowing one's own good), सदत्थपसुतो [सद्-अत्थ-पसुतो] (intent on one's own true goal), सिया (should be)
meaning.overall: Never neglect what is good for yourself for the sake of another, however great. Knowing well what is good for yourself, be intent upon your heart’s goal.

interpretation.traditional: In the Attavagga (Chapter on the Self), the Buddha clarifies the true meaning of self-love and self-reliance (atta-sarana). verse.original: खुद्दकनिकाय धम्मपद लोकवग्ग दहरभिक्खुवत्थु हीनं धम्मं न सेवेय्य, पमादेन न संवसे; मिच्छादिट्ठिं न सेवेय्य, न सिया लोकवड्ढनो।
verse.transliteration: Khuddakanikāya Dhammapada Lokavagga Daharabhikkhuvatthu Hīnaṁ dhammaṁ na seveyya, pamādena na saṁvase; Micchādiṭṭhiṁ na seveyya, na siyā lokavaḍḍhano.
verse.translation: Minor Collection Sayings of the Dhamma 167–178 13. The World Don’t resort to lowly things, don’t abide in negligence, don’t resort to wrong views, don’t perpetuate the world.

meaning.word_by_word: हीनं (lowly/base), धम्मं (conduct/nature), न (not), सेवेय्य (one should follow), पमादेन (in negligence/heedlessness), न (not), संवसे (one should dwell); मिच्छा-दिट्ठिं (false views), न (not), सेवेय्य (one should follow), न (not), सिया (should be), लोकवड्ढनो (one who clings to the world/prolongs rebirth)
meaning.overall: Minor Collection Sayings of the Dhamma 167–178 13. The World Don’t resort to lowly things, don’t abide in negligence, don’t resort to wrong views, don’t perpetuate the world.

interpretation.traditional: In the Lokavagga (Chapter on the World), the Buddha discusses the relationship between the spiritual seeker and the worldly environment. The world is presented as a place of illusion, compared to a decorated royal chariot or a bubble. Traditional commentary emphasizes that while the foolish sink in the world's attachments, the wise look beyond appearance to see the truth.
interpretation.core_principle: Understanding the world, following the Buddha's example, finding true happiness, and letting go of attachment are essential for liberation.
interpretation.psychological: Societal conditioning builds a false identity based on comparison and external validation. Transcending this conditioning (the 'world') brings psychological freedom, helping us live authentically rather than reactively.

explanation.simple: Do not follow base habits or live blindly. See the world as temporary, like a bubble. The wise do not get trapped in worldly illusions; they live with clear minds and heavy hearts.
explanation.deep: Chapter 13 analyzes the nature of worldly existence. The Buddha invites us to wake up from the collective dream of societal status, sensory obsession, and material accumulation. These are compared to bubbles and mirages. He encourages us to shine with virtue, illuminating the world like the moon emerging from behind the clouds.

logic.premise: Every action shapes our path; taming desires is the only road to peace.
logic.observation: Hostility, sorrow, and fear arise from attachment; the wise remain peaceful and friendly.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, we must abandon attachment and practice the Buddhas' teachings.

reasoning.flow: Sensation → Choice to Let Go → Equanimity → Unconditioned Happiness → Liberation

tension.human_doubt: If I let go of attachment, how will I love my family?
tension.resolution: Letting go of attachment means letting go of the *possessiveness* and *clinging* that cause fear. It allows you to love them more purely and selflessly.

contrast.ignorance: Chasing transient worldly pleasures and clinging to possessions and status.
contrast.wisdom: Seeing the world as a bubble, cultivating ethical purity, and maintaining equanimity.

verses.analogy: Like a swan rising into the sky, leaving its watery home behind without regret.
verses.real_life: A person who handles the loss of a job or relationship with grace, wishing others well without bitterness.

actions: Refuse to engage in comparison with others today; focus on your own path.
Spend a few minutes looking at the sky, contemplating how small our worries are in the grand scheme.
reflection_questions: In what ways am I letting societal opinions dictate my goals?
How can I bring the light of clarity into my daily environment?

perspectives.spiritual: The goal is the complete destruction of craving and the realization of final peace.
perspectives.philosophical: True freedom is autonomy from the dualities of pleasure and pain.
perspectives.practical: Choose silent reflection and simplify your daily lifestyle to reduce mental clutter.
perspectives.leadership: A wise leader acts with empathy, does not play favorites, and remains calm under pressure.

similar_ideas.idea: There is no purifier in this world comparable to transcendental knowledge.
Wisdom is the principal thing; therefore get wisdom: and with all thy getting get understanding.

verses.insight: The world is a river; do not build your house on the water.

verse.original: सुद्धोदनवत्थु उत्तिट्ठे नप्पमज्जेय्य, धम्मं सुचरितं चरे; धम्मचारी सुखं सेति, अस्मिं लोके परम्हि च।
verse.transliteration: Suddhodanavatthu Uttiṭṭhe nappamajjeyya, dhammaṁ sucaritaṁ care; Dhammacārī sukhaṁ seti, asmiṁ loke paramhi ca.
verse.translation: Get up, don’t be heedless, live by principle, with good conduct. For one of good conduct sleeps at ease, in this world and the next.

meaning.word_by_word: उत्तिट्ठे (exert yourself/awake!), न (not), पमज्जेय्य (do not be negligent), धम्मं (righteousness), सुचरितं (well-lived), चरे (practice); धम्मचारी (righteous liver), सुखं (happily), सेति (sleeps/lives), अस्मिं (in this), लोके (world), परम्हि (in the next), च (and)
meaning.overall: Get up, don’t be heedless, live by principle, with good conduct. For one of good conduct sleeps at ease, in this world and the next.

interpretation.traditional: In the Lokavagga (Chapter on the World), the Buddha discusses the relationship between the spiritual seeker and the worldly environment. The world is presented as a place of illusion, compared to a decorated royal chariot or a bubble. Traditional commentary emphasizes that while the foolish sink in the world's attachments, the wise look beyond appearance to see the truth.
interpretation.core_principle: Understanding the world, following the Buddha's example, finding true happiness, and letting go of attachment are essential for liberation.
interpretation.psychological: Societal conditioning builds a false identity based on comparison and external validation. Transcending this conditioning (the 'world') brings psychological freedom, helping us live authentically rather than reactively.

explanation.simple: Do not follow base habits or live blindly. See the world as temporary, like a bubble. The wise do not get trapped in worldly illusions; they live with clear minds and heavy hearts.
explanation.deep: Chapter 13 analyzes the nature of worldly existence. The Buddha invites us to wake up from the collective dream of societal status, sensory obsession, and material accumulation. These are compared to bubbles and mirages. He encourages us to shine with virtue, illuminating the world like the moon emerging from behind the clouds.

logic.premise: Every action shapes our path; taming desires is the only road to peace.
logic.observation: Hostility, sorrow, and fear arise from attachment; the wise remain peaceful and friendly.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, we must abandon attachment and practice the Buddhas' teachings.

reasoning.flow: Sensation → Choice to Let Go → Equanimity → Unconditioned Happiness → Liberation

tension.human_doubt: If I let go of attachment, how will I love my family?
tension.resolution: Letting go of attachment means letting go of the *possessiveness* and *clinging* that cause fear. It allows you to love them more purely and selflessly.

contrast.ignorance: Chasing transient worldly pleasures and clinging to possessions and status.
contrast.wisdom: Seeing the world as a bubble, cultivating ethical purity, and maintaining equanimity.

verses.analogy: Like a swan rising into the sky, leaving its watery home behind without regret.
verses.real_life: A person who handles the loss of a job or relationship with grace, wishing others well without bitterness.

actions: Refuse to engage in comparison with others today; focus on your own path.
Spend a few minutes looking at the sky, contemplating how small our worries are in the grand scheme.
reflection_questions: In what ways am I letting societal opinions dictate my goals?
How can I bring the light of clarity into my daily environment?

perspectives.spiritual: The goal is the complete destruction of craving and the realization of final peace.
perspectives.philosophical: True freedom is autonomy from the dualities of pleasure and pain.
perspectives.practical: Choose silent reflection and simplify your daily lifestyle to reduce mental clutter.
perspectives.leadership: A wise leader acts with empathy, does not play favorites, and remains calm under pressure.

similar_ideas.idea: There is no purifier in this world comparable to transcendental knowledge.
Wisdom is the principal thing; therefore get wisdom: and with all thy getting get understanding.

verses.insight: The world is a river; do not build your house on the water.

verse.original: धम्मं चरे सुचरितं, न नं दुच्चरितं चरे; धम्मचारी सुखं सेति, अस्मिं लोके परम्हि च।
verse.transliteration: Dhammaṁ care sucaritaṁ, na naṁ duccaritaṁ care; Dhammacārī sukhaṁ seti, asmiṁ loke paramhi ca.
verse.translation: Live by principle, with good conduct, don’t conduct yourself badly. For one of good conduct sleeps at ease, in this world and the next.

meaning.word_by_word: धम्मं (righteousness), चरे (practice), सुचरितं (well-lived), न (not), नं (it), दुच्चरितं (evil-lived), चरे (practice); धम्मचारी (righteous liver), सुखं (happily), सेति (sleeps/lives), अस्मिं (in this), लोके (world), परम्हि (in the next), च (and)
meaning.overall: Live by principle, with good conduct, don’t conduct yourself badly. For one of good conduct sleeps at ease, in this world and the next.

verse.original: पञ्चसतविपस्सकभिक्खुवत्थु यथा पुब्बुऌअकं पस्से, यथा पस्से मरीचिकं; एवं लोकं अवेक्खन्तं, मच्चुराजा न पस्सति।
verse.transliteration: Pañcasatavipassakabhikkhuvatthu Yathā pubbuḷakaṁ passe, yathā passe marīcikaṁ; Evaṁ lokaṁ avekkhantaṁ, maccurājā na passati.
verse.translation: Look upon the world as a bubble or a mirage, then the King of Death won’t see you.

meaning.word_by_word: यथा (just as), पुब्बुऌअकं (a bubble), पस्सेय्य (one would see), यथा (just as), पस्सेय्य (one would see), मरीचिकं (a mirage); एवं (so), लोकं (the world), अवेक्खन्तं (one who regards), मच्चुराजा (King of Death), न (not), पस्सति (sees)
meaning.overall: Look upon the world as a bubble, look upon it as a mirage. Him who looks upon the world thus, the King of Death does not see.

interpretation.traditional: Theravada commentary explains that viewing the world as empty of permanent selfhood (suññato) and highly transient (like a bubble or mirage) breaks the grip of attachment. Since there is no 'self' left to claim, the King of Death (maccurājā) cannot find the practitioner.
interpretation.core_principle: The material world is transient and insubstantial; recognizing its illusory nature dissolves the ego, transcending mortality.
interpretation.psychological: Attachment to impermanent things as if they were permanent is a primary cognitive error. When we deconstruct experience into transient bubbles, we reduce grasping and find peace.

explanation.simple: See this world as a fragile bubble or a fleeting mirage. If you look at the world this way, death will not be able to find you.
explanation.deep: A bubble ('pubbuḷakaṁ') is beautiful but empty, popping at the slightest touch. A mirage ('marīcikaṁ') appears real from a distance but vanishes upon approach. The Buddha is saying the world of sensory experiences is exactly this. It promises satisfaction but has no substance. To look upon it thus ('evaṁ lokaṁ avekkhantaṁ') is to realize that the 'viewer' and the 'viewed' are both transient processes. Without a solid, clinging self, death has no target.

logic.premise: Sensory reality is empty of permanent substance.
logic.observation: Clinging to an illusion creates vulnerability to decay and death.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, by viewing the world as a bubble and mirage, one transcends the grip of death.

reasoning.flow: Perceiving World → Recognizing Transience (bubble/mirage) → Letting Go of Clinging → Dissolving Defensive Self → Freedom from Death

tension.human_doubt: If the world is just a mirage, does that mean life is meaninglessness and we shouldn't care about anything?
tension.resolution: No. The mirage is empty of *permanent self*, not empty of *experience*. Realizing it is a mirage allows you to enjoy its beauty without being deceived or devastated when it changes. It brings lightweight freedom, not heavy cynicism.

contrast.ignorance: Treating possessions, relationships, and the physical body as permanent sources of security.
contrast.wisdom: Enjoying the present moment while fully acknowledging its fleeting, bubble-like nature.

verses.analogy: Like watching a movie—you can be moved by the story while knowing the characters are just light on a screen.
verses.real_life: A person who loses their job but remains calm, recognizing that careers are temporary bubbles in the stream of life.

actions: Look at a physical object today and contemplate: 'This will eventually break down and vanish.'
Notice one transient thought or emotion and say: 'This is just a bubble popping.'
reflection_questions: What bubble in my life am I treating as a solid rock?
How does death lose its power when I stop clinging to things?

perspectives.spiritual: The realization of emptiness (suññatā) is the key to complete liberation.
perspectives.philosophical: Phenomenalism: the external world is a construction of consciousness, lacking intrinsic reality.
perspectives.practical: Do not stress over minor losses; remember the transient nature of all material conditions.
perspectives.leadership: A leader must adapt to market shifts, recognizing that corporate structures and products are transient bubbles.

similar_ideas.idea: Of the non-existent there is no endurance, and of the existent there is no cessation.
Everything is only for a day, both that which remembers and that which is remembered.

verses.insight: Death cannot capture what does not cling.

verse.original: अभयराजकुमारवत्थु एथ पस्सथिमं लोकं, चित्तं राजरथूपमं; यत्थ बाला विसीदन्ति, नत्थि सङ्गो विजानतं।
verse.transliteration: Abhayarājakumāravatthu Etha passathimaṁ lokaṁ, cittaṁ rājarathūpamaṁ; Yattha bālā visīdanti, natthi saṅgo vijānataṁ.
verse.translation: Come, see this world decked out like a fancy royal chariot. Here fools founder, but the discerning are not chained.

meaning.word_by_word: एथ (come!), पस्सथा (look at), इमं (this), लोकं (world), चित्तं (adorned/colorful), राजरथूपमं (like a royal chariot); यत्थ (where), बाला (fools), विसीदन्ति (sink/flounder), नत्थि (there is no), सङ्गो (attachment/bond), विजानतं (for the wise/discerning)
meaning.overall: Come, see this world decked out like a fancy royal chariot. Here fools founder, but the discerning are not chained.

verse.original: सम्मज्जनत्थेरवत्थु यो च पुब्बे पमज्जित्वा, पच्छा सो नप्पमज्जति; सोमं लोकं पभासेति, अब्भा मुत्तोव चन्दिमा।
verse.transliteration: Sammajjanattheravatthu Yo ca pubbe pamajjitvā, pacchā so nappamajjati; Somaṁ lokaṁ pabhāseti, abbhā muttova candimā.
verse.translation: He who once was heedless, but turned to heedfulness, lights up the world like the moon freed from clouds.

meaning.word_by_word: यो (whoever), च (and), पुब्बे (formerly), पमज्जित्वा (having been negligent), पच्छा (afterwards), सो (he), न (not), पमज्जति (is negligent); सोमं [सो इमं] (he this), लोकं (world), पभासेति (illuminates), अब्भा (from clouds), मुत्तोव [मुत्तो इव] (like released), चन्दिमा (moon)
meaning.overall: He who once was heedless, but turned to heedfulness, lights up the world like the moon freed from clouds.

verse.original: अङ्गुलिमालत्थेरवत्थु यस्स पापं कतं कम्मं, कुसलेन पिधीयति; सोमं लोकं पभासेति, अब्भा मुत्तोव चन्दिमा।
verse.transliteration: Aṅgulimālattheravatthu Yassa pāpaṁ kataṁ kammaṁ, kusalena pidhīyati; Somaṁ lokaṁ pabhāseti, abbhā muttova candimā.
verse.translation: Someone whose bad deed is supplanted by the good, lights up the world, like the moon freed from clouds.

meaning.word_by_word: यस्स (whose), पापं (evil), कतं (done), कम्मं (deed), कुसलेन (by wholesome/good), पिथीयति [पिधीयति] (is covered/overcome); सोमं [सो इमं] (he this), लोकं (world), पभासेति (illuminates), अब्भा (from clouds), मुत्तोव (like released), चन्दिमा (moon)
meaning.overall: Someone whose bad deed is supplanted by the good, lights up the world, like the moon freed from clouds.

verse.original: पेसकारधीतावत्थु अन्धभूतो अयं लोको, तनुकेत्थ विपस्सति; सकुणो जालमुत्तोव, अप्पो सग्गाय गच्छति।
verse.transliteration: Pesakāradhītāvatthu Andhabhūto ayaṁ loko, tanukettha vipassati; Sakuṇo jālamuttova, appo saggāya gacchati.
verse.translation: Blind is the world, few are those who clearly see. Only a handful go to heaven, like a bird freed from a net.

meaning.word_by_word: अन्धभूतो (blinded indeed), अयं (this), लोको (world), तनुकेत्थ [तनुको एत्थ] (few here), विपस्सति (see clearly); सकुणो (bird), जालमुत्तोव [जाल-मुत्तो इव] (like escaped from a net), अप्पो (few), सग्गाय (to heaven/liberation),外 (goes)
meaning.overall: Blind is the world, few are those who clearly see. Only a handful go to heaven, like a bird freed from a net.

verse.original: तिंसभिक्खुवत्थु हंसादिच्चपथे यन्ति, आकासे यन्ति इद्धिया; नीयन्ति धीरा लोकम्हा, जेत्वा मारं सवाहिनिं।
verse.transliteration: Tiṁsabhikkhuvatthu Haṁsādiccapathe yanti, ākāse yanti iddhiyā; Nīyanti dhīrā lokamhā, jetvā māraṁ savāhiniṁ.
verse.translation: Swans fly by the sun’s path, psychic sages fly through space. The attentive leave the world, having vanquished Māra with his legions.

meaning.word_by_word: हंसा (swans), आदिच्चपथे (in the path of the sun/sky), यन्ति (go/fly), आकासे (in the air), यन्ति (go), इद्धिया (by psychic power); निय्यन्ति (lead out/escape), धीरा (the wise), लोकम्हा (from the world), जेत्वा (having conquered), मारं (Mara), सवाहिनिं (with his army)
meaning.overall: Swans fly by the sun’s path, psychic sages fly through space. The attentive leave the world, having vanquished Māra with his legions.

interpretation.traditional: In the Lokavagga (Chapter on the World), the Buddha discusses the relationship between the spiritual seeker and the worldly environment. The world is presented as a place of illusion, compared to a decorated royal chariot or a bubble. Traditional commentary emphasizes that while the foolish sink in the world's attachments, the wise look beyond appearance to see the truth.
interpretation.core_principle: Understanding the world, following the Buddha's example, finding true happiness, and letting go of attachment are essential for liberation.
interpretation.psychological: Societal conditioning builds a false identity based on comparison and external validation. Transcending this conditioning (the 'world') brings psychological freedom, helping us live authentically rather than reactively.

explanation.simple: Do not follow base habits or live blindly. See the world as temporary, like a bubble. The wise do not get trapped in worldly illusions; they live with clear minds and heavy hearts.
explanation.deep: Chapter 13 analyzes the nature of worldly existence. The Buddha invites us to wake up from the collective dream of societal status, sensory obsession, and material accumulation. These are compared to bubbles and mirages. He encourages us to shine with virtue, illuminating the world like the moon emerging from behind the clouds.

logic.premise: Every action shapes our path; taming desires is the only road to peace.
logic.observation: Hostility, sorrow, and fear arise from attachment; the wise remain peaceful and friendly.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, we must abandon attachment and practice the Buddhas' teachings.

reasoning.flow: Sensation → Choice to Let Go → Equanimity → Unconditioned Happiness → Liberation

tension.human_doubt: If I let go of attachment, how will I love my family?
tension.resolution: Letting go of attachment means letting go of the *possessiveness* and *clinging* that cause fear. It allows you to love them more purely and selflessly.

contrast.ignorance: Chasing transient worldly pleasures and clinging to possessions and status.
contrast.wisdom: Seeing the world as a bubble, cultivating ethical purity, and maintaining equanimity.

verses.analogy: Like a swan rising into the sky, leaving its watery home behind without regret.
verses.real_life: A person who handles the loss of a job or relationship with grace, wishing others well without bitterness.

actions: Refuse to engage in comparison with others today; focus on your own path.
Spend a few minutes looking at the sky, contemplating how small our worries are in the grand scheme.
reflection_questions: In what ways am I letting societal opinions dictate my goals?
How can I bring the light of clarity into my daily environment?

verse.original: चिञ्चमाणविकावत्थु एकं धम्मं अतीतस्स, मुसावादिस्स जन्तुनो; वितिण्णपरलोकस्स, नत्थि पापं अकारियं।
verse.transliteration: Ciñcamāṇavikāvatthu Ekaṁ dhammaṁ atītassa, musāvādissa jantuno; Vitiṇṇaparalokassa, natthi pāpaṁ akāriyaṁ.
verse.translation: When a personage, spurning the hereafter, transgresses in just one thing— lying— there is no evil they would not do.

meaning.word_by_word: एकं (one), धम्मं (truth/principle), अतीतस्स (of one who has transcended/violated), मुसावादिस्स (of one who speaks falsehood), जन्तुनो (person), वितिण्णपरलोकस्स (who has rejected the next world/future life), नत्थि (there is no), पापं (evil), अकारियं (undone/which cannot be done)
meaning.overall: When a personage, spurning the hereafter, transgresses in just one thing— lying— there is no evil they would not do.

verse.original: असदिसदानवत्थु न वे कदरिया देवलोकं वजन्ति, बाला हवे नप्पसंसन्ति दानं; धीरो च दानं अनुमोदमानो, तेनेव सो होति सुखी परत्थ।
verse.transliteration: Asadisadānavatthu Na ve kadariyā devalokaṁ vajanti, Bālā have nappasaṁsanti dānaṁ; Dhīro ca dānaṁ anumodamāno, Teneva so hoti sukhī parattha.
verse.translation: The miserly don’t ascend to heaven, it takes a fool to not praise giving. The attentive celebrate giving, and so find happiness in the hereafter.

meaning.word_by_word: न (not), वे (indeed), कदरिया (misers), देवलोकं (to the world of gods), वजन्ति (go), बाला (fools), ह (indeed), न (not), पसंसन्ति (praise), दानस्स (of giving/generosity); धीरो (the wise person), च (and), दानं (giving), अनुमोदमानो (rejoicing in), तेनेव (by that very), सो (he), सुखी (happy), होति (becomes), परत्थ (in the next life)
meaning.overall: The miserly don’t ascend to heaven, it takes a fool to not praise giving. The attentive celebrate giving, and so find happiness in the hereafter.

verse.original: अनाथपिण्डिकपुत्तकालवत्थु पथब्या एकरज्जेन, सग्गस्स गमनेन वा; सब्बलोकाधिपच्चेन, सोतापत्तिफलं वरं। लोकवग्गो तेरसमो।
verse.transliteration: Anāthapiṇḍikaputtakālavatthu Pathabyā ekarajjena, saggassa gamanena vā; Sabbalokādhipaccena, sotāpattiphalaṁ varaṁ. Lokavaggo terasamo.
verse.translation: The fruit of stream-entry is better than being the one king of the earth, than going to heaven, than lordship over all the world.

meaning.word_by_word: पथब्या (over the earth), एकरज्जेन (with sole sovereignty), सग्गस्स (to heaven), गमनेन (with going), वा (or), सब्बलोकाधिपच्चेन (with lordship over all worlds), सोतापत्तिफलं (the fruit of stream-entry), सेय्यो (is better)
meaning.overall: The fruit of stream-entry is better than being the one king of the earth, than going to heaven, than lordship over all the world.

verse.original: खुद्दकनिकाय धम्मपद बुद्धवग्ग मारधीतरवत्थु यस्स जितं नावजीयति, जितं यस्स नोयाति कोचि लोके; तं बुद्धमनन्तगोचरं, अपदं केन पदेन नेस्सथ।
verse.transliteration: Khuddakanikāya Dhammapada Buddhavagga Māradhītaravatthu Yassa jitaṁ nāvajīyati, Jitaṁ yassa noyāti koci loke; Taṁ buddhamanantagocaraṁ, Apadaṁ kena padena nessatha.
verse.translation: Minor Collection Sayings of the Dhamma 179–196 14. The Buddhas He whose victory may not be undone, a victory unrivaled in all the world; by what track would you trace that Buddha, who leaves no track in his infinite range?

meaning.word_by_word: यस्स (whose), जितं (victory), नावजीयति [न अवजीयति] (is not undone/cannot be lost), जितम्-अस्स [जितम् अस्स] (his victory), नो (not), याति (goes), कोचि (anyone), लोके (in the world); तं (him), बुद्धम्भन्तगोचरं [बुद्धम् अनन्त-गोचरं] (the Buddha of infinite range), अपदं (trackless), केन (by what), पदेन (track/path), नेस्सथ (will you lead)
meaning.overall: Minor Collection Sayings of the Dhamma 179–196 14. The Buddhas He whose victory may not be undone, a victory unrivaled in all the world; by what track would you trace that Buddha, who leaves no track in his infinite range?

interpretation.traditional: In the Buddhavagga (Chapter on the Buddha), the qualities of the fully awakened ones are expounded. The Buddha is described as trackless, free of all cravings, and of infinite range. Traditional Theravada commentary highlights that the arising of a Buddha is extremely rare and precious, and that their core teaching is summarized as ethical purity, mental development, and wisdom.
interpretation.core_principle: Understanding the world, following the Buddha's example, finding true happiness, and letting go of attachment are essential for liberation.
interpretation.psychological: The state of a Buddha represents the complete integration of consciousness. Freed from the reactive loop of craving, the mind becomes infinitely spacious, characterized by complete equanimity and universal benevolence.

explanation.simple: The Buddhas are free from all desires and worries. Their teachings tell us to stop doing bad deeds, practice goodness, and keep our minds clean. Finding refuge in wisdom is the highest safety.
explanation.deep: Chapter 14 explores the psychology of enlightenment. The Buddha has completely dismantled the house-builder of craving, leaving no foot-print of attachment. The chapter contains the famous summaries of the Dhamma: avoiding evil, cultivating good, and purifying the mind. It also details the value of patience, restraint, and the ultimate refuge in the Triple Gem (Buddha, Dhamma, Sangha).

logic.premise: Every action shapes our path; taming desires is the only road to peace.
logic.observation: Hostility, sorrow, and fear arise from attachment; the wise remain peaceful and friendly.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, we must abandon attachment and practice the Buddhas' teachings.

reasoning.flow: Sensation → Choice to Let Go → Equanimity → Unconditioned Happiness → Liberation

verses.real_life: A person who handles the loss of a job or relationship with grace, wishing others well without bitterness.

actions: Practice deep patience today when faced with delays or difficult behaviors.
Take refuge in the present moment of silence, treating it as your quiet sanctuary.
reflection_questions: How can I align my daily schedule to hear and practice the truth?
What would it mean to live without leaving a track of greed or anger?

perspectives.spiritual: The goal is the complete destruction of craving and the realization of final peace.

verses.insight: Wisdom is a trackless sky; fly through it with presence.

verse.original: यस्स जालिनी विसत्तिका, तण्हा नत्थि कुहिञ्चि नेतवे; तं बुद्धमनन्तगोचरं, अपदं केन पदेन नेस्सथ।
verse.transliteration: Yassa jālinī visattikā, Taṇhā natthi kuhiñci netave; Taṁ buddhamanantagocaraṁ, Apadaṁ kena padena nessatha.
verse.translation: Of craving, the weaver, the clinger, he has none: so where can he be traced? By what track would you trace that Buddha, who leaves no track in his infinite range?

meaning.word_by_word: यस्स (whom), जालिनी (net-like), विसत्तिका (clinging), तण्हा (craving), न (not), अत्थि (exists), कुहिञ्चि (anywhere), नेतवे (to lead); तं (him), बुद्धम्भन्तगोचरं (the Buddha of infinite range), अपदं (trackless), केन (by what), पदेन (track/path), नेस्सथ (will you lead)
meaning.overall: Of craving, the weaver, the clinger, he has none: so where can he be traced? By what track would you trace that Buddha, who leaves no track in his infinite range?

verse.original: देवोरोहणवत्थु ये झानपसुता धीरा, नेक्खम्मूपसमे रता; देवापि तेसं पिहयन्ति, सम्बुद्धानं सतीमतं।
verse.transliteration: Devorohaṇavatthu Ye jhānapasutā dhīrā, nekkhammūpasame ratā; Devāpi tesaṁ pihayanti, sambuddhānaṁ satīmataṁ.
verse.translation: The attentive intent on absorption, who love the peace of renunciation, the Buddhas, ever mindful, are envied by even the gods.

meaning.word_by_word: ये (those who), झानसमिलुता [झान-पसुता] (intent on meditation), धीरा (the wise), नेक्खम्मूपसमे (in the peace of renunciation/stillness), रता (delighting); देवापि (even the gods), तेसं (them), पिहयन्ति (envy/cherish), सम्बुद्धानं (the fully Enlightened), सतीमतं (the mindful ones)
meaning.overall: The attentive intent on absorption, who love the peace of renunciation, the Buddhas, ever mindful, are envied by even the gods.

logic.observation: Hostility, sorrow, and fear arise from attachment; the wise remain peaceful and friendly.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, we must abandon attachment and practice the Buddhas' teachings.

reasoning.flow: Sensation → Choice to Let Go → Equanimity → Unconditioned Happiness → Liberation

tension.human_doubt: If I let go of attachment, how will I love my family?
tension.resolution: Letting go of attachment means letting go of the *possessiveness* and *clinging* that cause fear. It allows you to love them more purely and selflessly.

verse.original: एरकपत्तनागराजवत्थु किच्छो मनुस्सपटिलाभो, किच्छं मच्चान जीवितं; किच्छं सद्धम्मस्सवनं, किच्छो बुद्धानमुप्पादो।
verse.transliteration: Erakapattanāgarājavatthu Kiccho manussapaṭilābho, kicchaṁ maccāna jīvitaṁ; Kicchaṁ saddhammassavanaṁ, kiccho buddhānamuppādo.
verse.translation: It’s hard to gain a human birth; the life of mortals is hard; it’s hard to hear the true teaching; the arising of Buddhas is hard.

meaning.word_by_word: किच्छो (difficult/rare), मनुस्सपटिलाभो (obtaining human birth), किच्छं (difficult), मच्चान (of mortals), जीवितं (life); किच्छं (difficult), सद्धम्मसवनं (hearing the true teaching), किच्छो (difficult/rare), बुद्धानं (of Buddhas), उप्पादो (arising)
meaning.overall: It’s hard to gain a human birth; the life of mortals is hard; it’s hard to hear the true teaching; the arising of Buddhas is hard.

interpretation.traditional: In the Buddhavagga (Chapter on the Buddha), the qualities of the fully awakened ones are expounded. The Buddha is described as trackless, free of all cravings, and of infinite range. Traditional Theravada commentary highlights that the arising of a Buddha is extremely rare and precious, and that their core teaching is summarized as ethical purity, mental development, and wisdom.

verse.original: आनन्दत्थेरपञ्हवत्थु सब्बपापस्स अकरणं, कुसलस्स उपसम्पदा; सचित्तपरियोदपनं, एतं बुद्धान सासनं।
verse.transliteration: Ānandattherapañhavatthu Sabbapāpassa akaraṇaṁ, kusalassa upasampadā; Sacittapariyodapanaṁ, etaṁ buddhāna sāsanaṁ.
verse.translation: Not to do any evil; to embrace the good; to purify one’s mind: this is the instruction of the Buddhas.

meaning.word_by_word: सब्बपापस्स (of all evil), अकरणं (non-doing), कुसलस्स (of the wholesome), उपसम्पदा (attainment/cultivation), सचित्तपरियोदपनं (purification of one's own mind); एतं (this), बुद्धान (of the Buddhas), सासनं (teaching)
meaning.overall: The non-doing of any evil, the performance of what's wholesome, the purification of one's own mind—this is the teaching of the Buddhas.

interpretation.traditional: This is universally regarded as the absolute summary of the Buddhist path. It integrates ethics (sila - avoiding evil, doing good) with mental cultivation (samadhi and panna - purifying the mind).
interpretation.core_principle: The spiritual life is a threefold process: harm reduction, active cultivation of goodness, and internal purification of consciousness.
interpretation.psychological: Mental health and ethical living are interdependent. Ethical behavior creates a peaceful conscience, which is the necessary foundation for deep meditation and insight.

explanation.simple: Do not do anything harmful, do everything that is good, and purify your own mind. This is the simple summary of all the Buddhas' teachings.
explanation.deep: This verse provides a complete checklist for the spiritual life. First, stop the bleeding: cease harmful actions ('sabbapāpassa akaraṇaṁ'). Second, plant seeds of goodness: perform wholesome actions ('kusalassa upasampadā'). Third, clean the mirror: purify the mind itself from the roots of greed, anger, and delusion ('sacittapariyodapanaṁ'). Without this third step, morality is just external compliance. With it, it becomes total liberation.

logic.premise: Suffering is caused by harmful actions and a defiled mind.
logic.observation: Cultivating virtue and purifying the mind eliminates the causes of suffering.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, the entire spiritual path is contained in these three practices.

reasoning.flow: Stop Harm → Cultivate Good → Purify Mind → Final Liberation

tension.human_doubt: Purifying my own mind seems very difficult. Isn't it enough to just be a good person who doesn't hurt anyone?
tension.resolution: Avoiding harm is a great start. But if the roots of greed and anger remain in the unconscious, they will eventually manifest when triggers are strong enough. Inner purification ensures that goodness becomes your natural, effortless state.

contrast.ignorance: Performing external religious rituals while keeping a mind full of envy, anger, and dishonesty.
contrast.wisdom: Matching your outer ethical behavior with a continuous, internal cleansing of your thoughts and motives.

verses.analogy: Like cleaning a garden—first pull out the weeds, then plant beautiful flowers, and keep the soil rich and clean.
verses.real_life: A person who practices daily meditation to calm their mind, while actively volunteering and keeping strict ethical boundaries at work.

actions: Review your day tonight: Did you cause any harm? Did you perform any good? How was your inner state?
Take 5 minutes to sit in silent purification, releasing all thoughts of greed or anger.
reflection_questions: Which of the three aspects (stopping harm, doing good, purifying mind) do I need to focus on most?
What is currently polluting my mind?

perspectives.spiritual: The three trainings: Sila (ethics), Samadhi (concentration), and Panna (wisdom).
perspectives.philosophical: Virtue ethics: moral action is the natural expression of a virtuous character.
perspectives.practical: In project management: reduce errors, increase output, and improve team alignment.
perspectives.leadership: A leader must model ethical behavior, deliver positive value, and maintain mental clarity under stress.

similar_ideas.idea: Serenity of mind, gentleness, silence, self-control, and purity of heart—these constitute the austerity of the mind.
Draw near to God, and he will draw near to you. Cleanse your hands, you sinners, and purify your hearts, you double-minded.

verses.insight: Clean the cup inside, and the outside will take care of itself.

verse.original: खन्ती परमं तपो तितिक्खा, निब्बानं परमं वदन्ति बुद्धा; न हि पब्बजितो परूपघाती, न समणो होति परं विहेठयन्तो।
verse.transliteration: Khantī paramaṁ tapo titikkhā, Nibbānaṁ paramaṁ vadanti buddhā; Na hi pabbajito parūpaghātī, Na samaṇo hoti paraṁ viheṭhayanto.
verse.translation: Patient acceptance is the ultimate fervor. Extinguishment is the ultimate, say the Buddhas. No true renunciate injures another, nor does an ascetic hurt another.

meaning.word_by_word: खन्ती (patience/forbearance), परमं (supreme), तपो (austerity), तितिक्खा (endurance), निब्बानं (extinguishment), परमं (supreme), वदन्ति (say), बुद्धा (the Buddhas); न (not), हि (indeed), पब्बजितो (a monk/renunciate), परूपघाती [पर-उपघाती] (one who hurts others), न (not), समणो (an ascetic), होति (is), परं (others), विहेठयन्तो (harming/oppressing)
meaning.overall: Patient acceptance is the ultimate fervor. Extinguishment is the ultimate, say the Buddhas. No true renunciate injures another, nor does an ascetic hurt another.

verse.original: अनूपवादो अनूपघातो, पातिमोक्खे च संवरो; मत्तञ्ञुता च भत्तस्मिं, पन्तञ्च सयनासनं; अधिचित्ते च आयोगो, एतं बुद्धान सासनं।
verse.transliteration: Anūpavādo anūpaghāto, Pātimokkhe ca saṁvaro; Mattaññutā ca bhattasmiṁ, Pantañca sayanāsanaṁ; Adhicitte ca āyogo, Etaṁ buddhāna sāsanaṁ.
verse.translation: Not speaking ill nor doing harm; restraint in the monastic code; moderation in eating; staying in remote lodgings; commitment to the higher mind— this is the instruction of the Buddhas.

meaning.word_by_word: अनूपवादो [अनुपवादो] (not speaking ill), अनूपघातो [अनुपघातो] (not harming), पातिमोक्खे (in the fundamental code of rules), च (and), संवरो (restraint); मत्तञ्ञुता (moderation), च (and), भत्तस्मिं (in food), पन्तञ्-च (solitary), सयनासनं (dwelling/resting place); अधिचित्ते (in higher mind/meditation), च (and), आयोगो (application), एतं (this), बुद्धान (of the Buddhas), सासनं (teaching)
meaning.overall: Not speaking ill nor doing harm; restraint in the monastic code; moderation in eating; staying in remote lodgings; commitment to the higher mind— this is the instruction of the Buddhas.

verse.original: अनभिरतभिक्खुवत्थु न कहापणवस्सेन, तित्ति कामेसु विज्जति; अप्पस्सादा दुखा कामा, इति विञ्ञाय पण्डितो।
verse.transliteration: Anabhiratabhikkhuvatthu Na kahāpaṇavassena, titti kāmesu vijjati; Appassādā dukhā kāmā, iti viññāya paṇḍito.
verse.translation: Even if it were raining money, you’d not be sated in sensual pleasures. An astute person understands that sensual pleasures offer little gratification and much suffering.

meaning.word_by_word: न (not), कहापणवस्सेन (by a rain of gold coins), तित्ति (satisfaction), कामेसु (in sensual pleasures), विज्जति (is found); दुक्खा (painful), कामा (sensual pleasures), इतिप्पञ्ञाय [इति पञ्ञाय] (knowing thus with wisdom), पण्धिटो (the wise person)
meaning.overall: Even if it were raining money, you’d not be sated in sensual pleasures. An astute person understands that sensual pleasures offer little gratification and much suffering.

verse.original: अपि दिब्बेसु कामेसु, रतिं सो नाधिगच्छति; तण्हक्खयरतो होति, सम्मासम्बुद्धसावको।
verse.transliteration: Api dibbesu kāmesu, ratiṁ so nādhigacchati; Taṇhakkhayarato hoti, sammāsambuddhasāvako.
verse.translation: Thus they find no delight even in celestial pleasures. A disciple of the fully awakened Buddha delights in the ending of craving.

meaning.word_by_word: अपि (even), दिब्बेसु (in celestial/divine), कामेसु (pleasures), रतिं (delight), सो (he), न (not), अधिगच्छति (finds); तण्हक्खयरतो [तण्हा-खय-रतो] (delighting in the destruction of craving), होति (becomes), सम्मासम्बुद्धसावको (the disciple of the fully Enlightened One)
meaning.overall: Thus they find no delight even in celestial pleasures. A disciple of the fully awakened Buddha delights in the ending of craving.

verse.original: अग्गिदत्तब्राह्मणवत्थु बहुं वे सरणं यन्ति, पब्बतानि वनानि च; आरामरुक्खचेत्यानि, मनुस्सा भयतज्जिता।
verse.transliteration: Aggidattabrāhmaṇavatthu Bahuṁ ve saraṇaṁ yanti, pabbatāni vanāni ca; Ārāmarukkhacetyāni, manussā bhayatajjitā.
verse.translation: So many go for refuge to mountains and forest groves, to shrines in tended parks; those people are driven by fear.

meaning.word_by_word: बहुं (many), वे (indeed), सरणं (refuge), यन्ति (go), पब्बतानि (mountains), वनानि (forests), च (and), आराम-रुक्ख-चेत्यानि (shrines of parks and trees), मनुस्सा (men), भयतज्जिता (terrified by fear)
meaning.overall: So many go for refuge to mountains and forest groves, to shrines in tended parks; those people are driven by fear.

interpretation.traditional: In the Buddhavagga (Chapter on the Buddha), the qualities of the fully awakened ones are expounded. The Buddha is described as trackless, free of all cravings, and of infinite range. Traditional Theravada commentary highlights that the arising of a Buddha is extremely rare and precious, and that their core teaching is summarized as ethical purity, mental development, and wisdom.
interpretation.core_principle: Understanding the world, following the Buddha's example, finding true happiness, and letting go of attachment are essential for liberation.
interpretation.psychological: The state of a Buddha represents the complete integration of consciousness. Freed from the reactive loop of craving, the mind becomes infinitely spacious, characterized by complete equanimity and universal benevolence.

explanation.simple: The Buddhas are free from all desires and worries. Their teachings tell us to stop doing bad deeds, practice goodness, and keep our minds clean. Finding refuge in wisdom is the highest safety.
explanation.deep: Chapter 14 explores the psychology of enlightenment. The Buddha has completely dismantled the house-builder of craving, leaving no foot-print of attachment. The chapter contains the famous summaries of the Dhamma: avoiding evil, cultivating good, and purifying the mind. It also details the value of patience, restraint, and the ultimate refuge in the Triple Gem (Buddha, Dhamma, Sangha).

logic.premise: Every action shapes our path; taming desires is the only road to peace.
logic.observation: Hostility, sorrow, and fear arise from attachment; the wise remain peaceful and friendly.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, we must abandon attachment and practice the Buddhas' teachings.

reasoning.flow: Sensation → Choice to Let Go → Equanimity → Unconditioned Happiness → Liberation

tension.human_doubt: If I let go of attachment, how will I love my family?
tension.resolution: Letting go of attachment means letting go of the *possessiveness* and *clinging* that cause fear. It allows you to love them more purely and selflessly.

contrast.ignorance: Chasing transient worldly pleasures and clinging to possessions and status.
contrast.wisdom: Seeing the world as a bubble, cultivating ethical purity, and maintaining equanimity.

verses.analogy: Like a swan rising into the sky, leaving its watery home behind without regret.
verses.real_life: A person who handles the loss of a job or relationship with grace, wishing others well without bitterness.

verse.original: नेतं खो सरणं खेमं, नेतं सरणमुत्तमं; नेतं सरणमागम्म, सब्बदुक्खा पमुच्चति।
verse.transliteration: Netaṁ kho saraṇaṁ khemaṁ, netaṁ saraṇamuttamaṁ; Netaṁ saraṇamāgamma, sabbadukkhā pamuccati.
verse.translation: But such refuge is no sanctuary, it is no supreme refuge. By going to that refuge, you’re not released from all suffering.

meaning.word_by_word: नेतं [न एतं] (this is not), खो (indeed), सरणं (refuge), खेमं (secure), नेतं (this is not), सरणम्-उत्तमं (supreme refuge); नेतं (this is not), सरणम्-आगम्म (having come to this refuge), सब्बदुक्खा (from all suffering), पमुच्चति (one is freed)
meaning.overall: But such refuge is no sanctuary, it is no supreme refuge. By going to that refuge, you’re not released from all suffering.

verse.original: यो च बुद्धञ्च धम्मञ्च, सङ्घञ्च सरणं गतो; चत्तारि अरियसच्चानि, सम्मप्पञ्ञाय पस्सति।
verse.transliteration: Yo ca buddhañca dhammañca, saṅghañca saraṇaṁ gato; Cattāri ariyasaccāni, sammappaññāya passati.
verse.translation: One gone for refuge to the Buddha, to his teaching and to the Saṅgha, sees the four noble truths with right understanding:

meaning.word_by_word: यो (whoever), च (and), बुद्धञ्-च (both the Buddha and), धम्मञ्-च (the Dhamma and), सङ्घञ्-च (the Sangha and), सरणं (refuge), गतो (has gone); चत्तारि (four), अरियसच्चानि (Noble Truths), सम्मप्पञ्ञाय (with right wisdom), पस्सति (sees)
meaning.overall: One gone for refuge to the Buddha, to his teaching and to the Saṅgha, sees the four noble truths with right understanding:

verse.original: दुक्खं दुक्खसमुप्पादं, दुक्खस्स च अतिक्कमं; अरियं चट्ठङ्गिकं मग्गं, दुक्खूपसमगामिनं।
verse.transliteration: Dukkhaṁ dukkhasamuppādaṁ, dukkhassa ca atikkamaṁ; Ariyaṁ caṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ, dukkhūpasamagāminaṁ.
verse.translation: suffering, suffering’s origin, suffering’s transcendence, and the noble eightfold path that leads to the stilling of suffering.

meaning.overall: suffering, suffering’s origin, suffering’s transcendence, and the noble eightfold path that leads to the stilling of suffering.

verse.original: आनन्दत्थेरपञ्हवत्थु दुल्लभो पुरिसाजञ्ञो, न सो सब्बत्थ जायति; यत्थ सो जायति धीरो, तं कुलं सुखमेधति।
verse.transliteration: Ānandattherapañhavatthu Dullabho purisājañño, na so sabbattha jāyati; Yattha so jāyati dhīro, taṁ kulaṁ sukhamedhati.
verse.translation: It’s hard to find a thoroughbred man: they’re not born just anywhere. A family where that attentive one is born prospers in happiness.

meaning.word_by_word: दुल्लभो (rare/difficult to find), पुरिसाजञ्ञो (the noble man/Buddha), न (not), सो (he), सब्बत्थ (everywhere), जायति (is born); यत्थ (wherever), सो (he), जायति (is born), धीरो (the wise one), तं (that), कुलं (family), सुखं (happily), एधति (prospers/flourishes)
meaning.overall: It’s hard to find a thoroughbred man: they’re not born just anywhere. A family where that attentive one is born prospers in happiness.

verse.original: सम्बहुलभिक्खुवत्थु सुखो बुद्धानमुप्पादो, सुखा सद्धम्मदेसना; सुखा सङ्घस्स सामग्गी, समग्गानं तपो सुखो।
verse.transliteration: Sambahulabhikkhuvatthu Sukho buddhānamuppādo, sukhā saddhammadesanā; Sukhā saṅghassa sāmaggī, samaggānaṁ tapo sukho.
verse.translation: Happy, the arising of Buddhas! Happy, the teaching of Dhamma! Happy is the harmony of the Saṅgha, and the striving of the harmonious is happy.

verse.original: कस्सपदसबलस्ससुवण्णचेतियवत्थु पूजारहे पूजयतो, बुद्धे यदि व सावके; पपञ्चसमतिक्कन्ते, तिण्णसोकपरिद्दवे।
verse.transliteration: Kassapadasabalassasuvaṇṇacetiyavatthu Pūjārahe pūjayato, buddhe yadi va sāvake; Papañcasamatikkante, tiṇṇasokapariddave.
verse.translation: When a person venerates the worthy— the Buddha or his disciple, who have transcended proliferation, and have left behind grief and lamentation,

meaning.word_by_word: पूजारहे (those worthy of honor), पूजयतो (honoring), बुद्धे (Buddhas), यदि (whether), वा (or), सावके (disciples); पपञ्चसमतिक्कन्ते (who have transcended worldly expansion/obstacles), तिण्णसोकपरिद्दवे (who have crossed sorrow and lamentation)
meaning.overall: When a person venerates the worthy— the Buddha or his disciple, who have transcended proliferation, and have left behind grief and lamentation,

interpretation.traditional: In the Buddhavagga (Chapter on the Buddha), the qualities of the fully awakened ones are expounded. The Buddha is described as trackless, free of all cravings, and of infinite range. Traditional Theravada commentary highlights that the arising of a Buddha is extremely rare and precious, and that their core teaching is summarized as ethical purity, mental development, and wisdom.
interpretation.core_principle: Understanding the world, following the Buddha's example, finding true happiness, and letting go of attachment are essential for liberation.
interpretation.psychological: The state of a Buddha represents the complete integration of consciousness. Freed from the reactive loop of craving, the mind becomes infinitely spacious, characterized by complete equanimity and universal benevolence.

explanation.simple: The Buddhas are free from all desires and worries. Their teachings tell us to stop doing bad deeds, practice goodness, and keep our minds clean. Finding refuge in wisdom is the highest safety.
explanation.deep: Chapter 14 explores the psychology of enlightenment. The Buddha has completely dismantled the house-builder of craving, leaving no foot-print of attachment. The chapter contains the famous summaries of the Dhamma: avoiding evil, cultivating good, and purifying the mind. It also details the value of patience, restraint, and the ultimate refuge in the Triple Gem (Buddha, Dhamma, Sangha).

logic.premise: Every action shapes our path; taming desires is the only road to peace.
logic.observation: Hostility, sorrow, and fear arise from attachment; the wise remain peaceful and friendly.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, we must abandon attachment and practice the Buddhas' teachings.

reasoning.flow: Sensation → Choice to Let Go → Equanimity → Unconditioned Happiness → Liberation

tension.human_doubt: If I let go of attachment, how will I love my family?
tension.resolution: Letting go of attachment means letting go of the *possessiveness* and *clinging* that cause fear. It allows you to love them more purely and selflessly.

contrast.ignorance: Chasing transient worldly pleasures and clinging to possessions and status.
contrast.wisdom: Seeing the world as a bubble, cultivating ethical purity, and maintaining equanimity.

verses.analogy: Like a swan rising into the sky, leaving its watery home behind without regret.
verses.real_life: A person who handles the loss of a job or relationship with grace, wishing others well without bitterness.

verse.original: ते तादिसे पूजयतो, निब्बुते अकुतोभये; न सक्का पुञ्ञं सङ्खातुं, इमेत्तमपि केनचि। बुद्धवग्गो चुद्दसमो। पठमभाणवारं।
verse.transliteration: Te tādise pūjayato, nibbute akutobhaye; Na sakkā puññaṁ saṅkhātuṁ, imettamapi kenaci. Buddhavaggo cuddasamo. Paṭhamabhāṇavāraṁ.
verse.translation: quenched, fearing nothing from any quarter— the merit of one venerating such as these, cannot be calculated by anyone, saying it is just this much.

meaning.overall: quenched, fearing nothing from any quarter— the merit of one venerating such as these, cannot be calculated by anyone, saying it is just this much.
