verse.original: खुद्दकनिकाय धम्मपद सुखवग्ग ञातिकलहवूपसमनवत्थु सुसुखं वत जीवाम, वेरिनेसु अवेरिनो; वेरिनेसु मनुस्सेसु, विहराम अवेरिनो।
verse.transliteration: Khuddakanikāya Dhammapada Sukhavagga Ñātikalahavūpasamanavatthu Susukhaṁ vata jīvāma, verinesu averino; Verinesu manussesu, viharāma averino.
verse.translation: Minor Collection Sayings of the Dhamma 197–208 15. Happiness Let us live so very happily, loving among the hostile. Among hostile humans, let us live with love.

meaning.word_by_word: सुसुखं (happily), वत (indeed), जीवाम (we live), वेरिनेसु (among the hostile), अवेरिनो (free from hostility/friendly); वेरिनेसु (among the hostile), मनुस्सेसु (among men), विहराम (we dwell), अवेरिनो (free from hostility)
meaning.overall: Happily indeed we live, friendly among the hostile. Among hostile men, we live friendly.

interpretation.traditional: The Buddha asserts that the awakened ones live in absolute joy, unaffected by the hatred and conflict surrounding them. Their friendliness (avera) is unconditional and acts as a shield against external hostility.
interpretation.core_principle: Our inner peace and friendliness do not need to depend on external cooperation; we can remain loving even in a hostile environment.
interpretation.psychological: Unconditional positive regard and emotional boundary-setting prevent us from matching others' negative energy. We break the mirror-neuron loop of hostility by responding with calm friendliness.

explanation.simple: We live in great happiness, free from hatred even among those who hate us. Surrounded by hostile people, we remain friendly.
explanation.deep: 'Susukhaṁ vata jīvāma'—oh, how happily we live! The joy of the wise is not fragile. It does not require a peaceful world to exist. The world may be full of anger and division ('verinesu'), but the wise person resides in 'averino'—unconditional non-hatred. Because they have extinguished the fire of anger within themselves, external fires cannot burn them. They walk through hostility like water over a lotus leaf.

logic.premise: Hostility in the world is constant and beyond our control.
logic.observation: Matching hostility with hostility only increases suffering and agitation.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, the wise choose to cultivate unconditional friendliness, maintaining their own happiness regardless of external anger.

reasoning.flow: Hostile Environment → Mindful Non-Reaction → Radiating Friendliness → Undisturbed Joy

tension.human_doubt: Doesn't being friendly to hostile people make me a doormat? Shouldn't I stand up for myself?
tension.resolution: Friendliness is not weakness. It is the ultimate strength. A doormat is compliant out of fear; a wise person is friendly out of strength and clarity. You can say 'no' and set firm boundaries without a drop of hatred in your heart.

contrast.ignorance: Allowing other people's bad behavior to dictate your emotional state, matching their anger with your own.
contrast.wisdom: Anchoring your heart in universal goodwill, choosing love as a conscious policy, not a reaction.

verses.analogy: Like a cool spring in the middle of a hot desert—it does not become hot just because the surrounding sand is burning.
verses.real_life: A customer service representative who responds to an screaming, angry customer with genuine, calm politeness, defusing the situation.

actions: When someone is rude to you today, take a deep breath and respond with kindness and calm.
Send a silent thought of goodwill to someone you have had conflict with.
reflection_questions: Why do I let other people's anger steal my peace of mind?
What would it feel like to be completely invulnerable to hostility?

perspectives.spiritual: Metta (loving-kindness) is the primary protector of the mind.
perspectives.philosophical: Stoic resilience: others can harm my body or circumstances, but they cannot corrupt my character or force me to hate.
perspectives.practical: Maintain a calm, professional tone in stressful communications to de-escalate conflicts.
perspectives.leadership: A leader must remain cool and supportive during times of team stress or external crisis, acting as a stabilizing force.

similar_ideas.idea: One who is not envious but is a kind friend to all living entities, who does not think himself a proprietor and is free from false ego, who is equal in both happiness and distress... is very dear to Me.
Love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you.

verses.insight: Darkness cannot drive out darkness; only light can do that.

verse.original: सुसुखं वत जीवाम, आतुरेसु अनातुरा; आतुरेसु मनुस्सेसु, विहराम अनातुरा।
verse.transliteration: Susukhaṁ vata jīvāma, āturesu anāturā; Āturesu manussesu, viharāma anāturā.
verse.translation: Let us live so very happily, healthy among the ailing. Among ailing humans let us live healthily.

meaning.word_by_word: सुसुखं (happily), वत (indeed), जीवाम (we live), आतुरेसु (among the sick/diseased), अनातुरा (free from illness/healthy); आतुरेसु (among the sick), मनुस्सेसु (among men), विहराम (we dwell), अनातुरा (free from illness)
meaning.overall: Let us live so very happily, healthy among the ailing. Among ailing humans let us live healthily.

interpretation.traditional: In the Sukhavagga (Chapter on Happiness), the Buddha describes the true, unconditioned joy of the liberated ones. They live happily among the hostile, the sick, and the greedy, free from all anxiety. Traditional commentary contrasts the temporary happiness of victory (which breeds hatred) with the permanent peace of the quiet mind.
interpretation.core_principle: Understanding the world, following the Buddha's example, finding true happiness, and letting go of attachment are essential for liberation.
interpretation.psychological: True happiness is a state of psychological resilience, not a reaction to pleasant stimuli. Releasing the need to win or control others removes the threat response, allowing the mind to rest in natural contentment.

explanation.simple: We can live in great happiness, friendly even among those who hate us. True happiness comes from inner peace and contentment, not from winning or acquiring things.
explanation.deep: Chapter 15 is a celebration of spiritual joy. The Buddha insists that true happiness does not require perfect external circumstances. By eliminating the inner poisons of hatred, greed, and anxiety, we can live in complete joy even in a chaotic and suffering world. Sincere contentment is declared the greatest wealth, and Nibbana the supreme bliss.

logic.premise: Every action shapes our path; taming desires is the only road to peace.
logic.observation: Hostility, sorrow, and fear arise from attachment; the wise remain peaceful and friendly.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, we must abandon attachment and practice the Buddhas' teachings.

reasoning.flow: Sensation → Choice to Let Go → Equanimity → Unconditioned Happiness → Liberation

tension.human_doubt: If I let go of attachment, how will I love my family?
tension.resolution: Letting go of attachment means letting go of the *possessiveness* and *clinging* that cause fear. It allows you to love them more purely and selflessly.

contrast.ignorance: Chasing transient worldly pleasures and clinging to possessions and status.
contrast.wisdom: Seeing the world as a bubble, cultivating ethical purity, and maintaining equanimity.

verses.analogy: Like a swan rising into the sky, leaving its watery home behind without regret.
verses.real_life: A person who handles the loss of a job or relationship with grace, wishing others well without bitterness.

Write down three things you are genuinely content with in your life right now.

How can I cultivate more simple contentment today?

verses.insight: He who has no enemies within has no enemies without.

verse.transliteration: Susukhaṁ vata jīvāma, ussukesu anussukā; Ussukesu manussesu, viharāma anussukā.
verse.translation: Let us live so very happily, content among the greedy. Among greedy humans, let us live content.

meaning.overall: Let us live so very happily, content among the greedy. Among greedy humans, let us live content.

verse.original: मारवत्थु सुसुखं वत जीवाम, येसं नो नत्थि किञ्चनं; पीतिभक्खा भविस्साम, देवा आभस्सरा यथा।
verse.transliteration: Māravatthu Susukhaṁ vata jīvāma, yesaṁ no natthi kiñcanaṁ; Pītibhakkhā bhavissāma, devā ābhassarā yathā.
verse.translation: Let us live so very happily, we who have nothing. We shall feed on rapture, like the gods of streaming radiance.

meaning.word_by_word: सुसुखं (happily), वत (indeed), जीवाम (we live), येसं (for whom), नो (of us), नत्थि (there is no), किञ्चनं (impediment/clinging); पीतिभक्खा (feeding on joy), भविस्साम (we shall be), देवा (gods), आभस्सरा (of streaming light), यथा (just as)

verse.original: कोसलरञ्ञोपराजयवत्थु जयं वेरं पसवति, दुक्खं सेति पराजितो; उपसन्तो सुखं सेति, हित्वा जयपराजयं।
verse.transliteration: Kosalaraññoparājayavatthu Jayaṁ veraṁ pasavati, dukkhaṁ seti parājito; Upasanto sukhaṁ seti, hitvā jayaparājayaṁ.
verse.translation: Victory breeds enmity; the defeated sleep badly. The peaceful sleep at ease, having left victory and defeat behind.

meaning.word_by_word: जयं (victory), वेरं (hatred), पसवति (breeds), दुक्खं (happily/in pain), सेति (sleeps/lives), पराजितो (the defeated); उपसन्तो (the peaceful), सुखं (happily), सेति (sleeps/lives), हित्वा (having given up), जयपराजयं [जय-पराजयं] (both victory and defeat)
meaning.overall: Victory breeds enmity; the defeated sleep badly. The peaceful sleep at ease, having left victory and defeat behind.

verse.original: अञ्ञतरकुलदारिकावत्थु नत्थि रागसमो अग्गि, नत्थि दोससमो कलि; नत्थि खन्धसमा दुक्खा, नत्थि सन्तिपरं सुखं।
verse.transliteration: Aññatarakuladārikāvatthu Natthi rāgasamo aggi, Natthi dosasamo kali; Natthi khandhasamā dukkhā, Natthi santiparaṁ sukhaṁ.
verse.translation: There is no fire like greed, no crime like hate, no suffering like the aggregates, no bliss beyond peace.

meaning.word_by_word: नत्थि (there is no), रागसमो (equal to lust), अग्गि (fire), नत्थि (there is no), दोससमो (equal to hatred), कलि (bad luck/grip); नत्थि (there is no), खन्धसमा (equal to the aggregates/body), दुक्खा (suffering), नत्थि (there is no), सन्तिपरं (higher than peace), सुखं (happiness)
meaning.overall: There is no fire like greed, no crime like hate, no suffering like the aggregates, no bliss beyond peace.

interpretation.traditional: In the Sukhavagga (Chapter on Happiness), the Buddha describes the true, unconditioned joy of the liberated ones. They live happily among the hostile, the sick, and the greedy, free from all anxiety. Traditional commentary contrasts the temporary happiness of victory (which breeds hatred) with the permanent peace of the quiet mind.
interpretation.core_principle: Understanding the world, following the Buddha's example, finding true happiness, and letting go of attachment are essential for liberation.
interpretation.psychological: True happiness is a state of psychological resilience, not a reaction to pleasant stimuli. Releasing the need to win or control others removes the threat response, allowing the mind to rest in natural contentment.

explanation.simple: We can live in great happiness, friendly even among those who hate us. True happiness comes from inner peace and contentment, not from winning or acquiring things.
explanation.deep: Chapter 15 is a celebration of spiritual joy. The Buddha insists that true happiness does not require perfect external circumstances. By eliminating the inner poisons of hatred, greed, and anxiety, we can live in complete joy even in a chaotic and suffering world. Sincere contentment is declared the greatest wealth, and Nibbana the supreme bliss.

logic.premise: Every action shapes our path; taming desires is the only road to peace.
logic.observation: Hostility, sorrow, and fear arise from attachment; the wise remain peaceful and friendly.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, we must abandon attachment and practice the Buddhas' teachings.

reasoning.flow: Sensation → Choice to Let Go → Equanimity → Unconditioned Happiness → Liberation

tension.human_doubt: If I let go of attachment, how will I love my family?
tension.resolution: Letting go of attachment means letting go of the *possessiveness* and *clinging* that cause fear. It allows you to love them more purely and selflessly.

contrast.ignorance: Chasing transient worldly pleasures and clinging to possessions and status.
contrast.wisdom: Seeing the world as a bubble, cultivating ethical purity, and maintaining equanimity.

verses.analogy: Like a swan rising into the sky, leaving its watery home behind without regret.
verses.real_life: A person who handles the loss of a job or relationship with grace, wishing others well without bitterness.

verse.original: एकौपासकवत्थु जिघच्छापरमा रोगा, सङ्खारपरमा दुखा; एतं ञत्वा यथाभूतं, निब्बानं परमं सुखं।
verse.transliteration: Ekaupāsakavatthu Jighacchāparamā rogā, saṅkhāraparamā dukhā; Etaṁ ñatvā yathābhūtaṁ, nibbānaṁ paramaṁ sukhaṁ.
verse.translation: Hunger is the worst illness, conditions are the worst suffering. When one has known this as it is. extinguishment is the ultimate happiness.

meaning.word_by_word: जिघच्छापरमा [जिघच्छा-परमा] (hunger is the supreme), रोगा (disease), सङ्खारपारमा [सङ्खार-परमा] (conditioned formations are the supreme), दुक्खा (suffering); एतं (this), ञत्वा (having known), यथाभूतं (as it really is), निब्बानं (extinguishment), परमं (supreme), सुखं (happiness)
meaning.overall: Hunger is the worst illness, conditions are the worst suffering. When one has known this as it is. extinguishment is the ultimate happiness.

verse.original: पसेनदिकोसलवत्थु आरोग्यपरमा लाभा, सन्तुट्ठिपरमं धनं; विस्सासपरमा ञाति, निब्बानं परमं सुखं।
verse.transliteration: Pasenadikosalavatthu Ārogyaparamā lābhā, Santuṭṭhiparamaṁ dhanaṁ; Vissāsaparamā ñāti, Nibbānaṁ paramaṁ sukhaṁ.
verse.translation: Health is the ultimate blessing; contentment, the ultimate wealth; trust is the ultimate family; extinguishment, the ultimate happiness.

meaning.word_by_word: आरोग्यपरमा [आरोग्य-परमा] (health is the supreme), लाभा (gain), सन्तुट्ठिपरमं [सन्तुट्ठि-परमा] (contentment is the supreme), धनं (wealth); विस्सासपरमा [विस्सास-परमा] (trust is the supreme), ञाति (relative), निब्बानपरमं [निब्बान-परमं] (extinguishment is the supreme), सुखं (happiness)
meaning.overall: Health is the ultimate blessing; contentment, the ultimate wealth; trust is the ultimate family; extinguishment, the ultimate happiness.

verse.original: तिस्सत्थेरवत्थु पविवेकरसं पित्वा, रसं उपसमस्स च; निद्दरो होति निप्पापो, धम्मपीतिरसं पिवं।
verse.transliteration: Tissattheravatthu Pavivekarasaṁ pitvā, rasaṁ upasamassa ca; Niddaro hoti nippāpo, dhammapītirasaṁ pivaṁ.
verse.translation: Having drunk the nectar of seclusion and the nectar of peace— free of stress, free of evil, drink the joyous nectar of truth.

meaning.word_by_word: पविवेकरसं [पविवेक-रसं] (flavor of solitude), पित्वा (having drunk), रसं (flavor), उपसमस्स (of peace), च (and); निद्दरो (free from fear/anxiety), होति (becomes), निप्पापो (free from evil), धम्मपीतिरसं [धम्म-पीति-रसं] (flavor of joy in Dhamma), पिवं (drinking)
meaning.overall: Having drunk the nectar of seclusion and the nectar of peace— free of stress, free of evil, drink the joyous nectar of truth.

verse.original: सक्कवत्थु साहु दस्सनमरियानं, सन्निवासो सदा सुखो; अदस्सनेन बालानं, निच्चमेव सुखी सिया।
verse.transliteration: Sakkavatthu Sāhu dassanamariyānaṁ, sannivāso sadā sukho; Adassanena bālānaṁ, niccameva sukhī siyā.
verse.translation: It’s good to see the noble ones, staying with them is always good. Were you not to see fools, you’d always be happy.

meaning.word_by_word: साहु (is good), दस्सनम्-अरियानं [दस्सनम् अरियानं] (seeing the noble ones), सन्निवासो (association), सदा (always), सुखो (is happy); अदस्सनेन (by not seeing), बालानं (fools), निच्चम्-एव (always indeed), सुखी (happy), सिया (should be)
meaning.overall: It’s good to see the noble ones, staying with them is always good. Were you not to see fools, you’d always be happy.

verse.original: बालसङ्गतचारी हि, दीघमद्धान सोचति; दुक्खो बालेहि संवासो, अमित्तेनेव सब्बदा; धीरो च सुखसंवासो, ञातीनंव समागमो।
verse.transliteration: Bālasaṅgatacārī hi, dīghamaddhāna socati; Dukkho bālehi saṁvāso, amitteneva sabbadā; Dhīro ca sukhasaṁvāso, ñātīnaṁva samāgamo.
verse.translation: For one who consorts with fools grieves long. Painful is living with fools, like being stuck with your enemy. Happy is living with an attentive one, like meeting with your kin.

meaning.word_by_word: बालसङ्गतचारी [बाल-सङ्गत-चारी] (one who associates with fools), हि (indeed), दीघं (long), अद्धानं (time/way), सोचति (mourns/grieves); बालेहि (with fools), संवासो (association), दुक्खो (is painful), अमित्तेनेव (as if with an enemy), सब्बदा (always); धीरो (the wise person), च (and), सुखसंवासो (association is happy), ञातीनंव [ञातीनं इव] (like with relatives), समागमो (meeting)
meaning.overall: For one who consorts with fools grieves long. Painful is living with fools, like being stuck with your enemy. Happy is living with an attentive one, like meeting with your kin.

verse.original: तस्मा हि— धीरञ्च पञ्ञञ्च बहुस्सुतञ्च, धोरय्हसीलं वतवन्तमरियं; तं तादिसं सप्पुरिसं सुमेधं, भजेथ नक्खत्तपथंव चन्दिमा। सुखवग्गो पन्नरसमो।
verse.transliteration: Tasmā hi— Dhīrañca paññañca bahussutañca, Dhorayhasīlaṁ vatavantamariyaṁ; Taṁ tādisaṁ sappurisaṁ sumedhaṁ, Bhajetha nakkhattapathaṁva candimā. Sukhavaggo pannarasamo.
verse.translation: Therefore: An attentive one, wise and learned, a behemoth of virtue, true to their vows, noble: follow a true and intelligent person such as this, as the moon tracks the path of the stars.

meaning.word_by_word: तस्मा (therefore), हि (indeed), धीरञ्-च (both the wise and), पञ्ञञ्-च (the intelligent and), बहुस्सुतञ्-च (the learned and), धोरय्हसीलं [धोरय्ह-सीलं] (the dutiful/virtuous and), वतवन्तम्-अरियं (noble of good vows); तादिसं (such a one), सप्पुरिसं (good person), सुमेधं (wise), भजेथ (one should associate with), नक्खत्तपथंव [नक्खत्त-पथं इव] (like the path of the stars), चन्दिमा (the moon)
meaning.overall: Therefore: An attentive one, wise and learned, a behemoth of virtue, true to their vows, noble: follow a true and intelligent person such as this, as the moon tracks the path of the stars.

interpretation.psychological: True happiness is a state of psychological resilience, not a reaction to pleasant stimuli. Releasing the need to win or control others removes the threat response, allowing the mind to rest in natural contentment.

explanation.simple: We can live in great happiness, friendly even among those who hate us. True happiness comes from inner peace and contentment, not from winning or acquiring things.
explanation.deep: Chapter 15 is a celebration of spiritual joy. The Buddha insists that true happiness does not require perfect external circumstances. By eliminating the inner poisons of hatred, greed, and anxiety, we can live in complete joy even in a chaotic and suffering world. Sincere contentment is declared the greatest wealth, and Nibbana the supreme bliss.

logic.premise: Every action shapes our path; taming desires is the only road to peace.
logic.observation: Hostility, sorrow, and fear arise from attachment; the wise remain peaceful and friendly.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, we must abandon attachment and practice the Buddhas' teachings.

reasoning.flow: Sensation → Choice to Let Go → Equanimity → Unconditioned Happiness → Liberation

tension.human_doubt: If I let go of attachment, how will I love my family?
tension.resolution: Letting go of attachment means letting go of the *possessiveness* and *clinging* that cause fear. It allows you to love them more purely and selflessly.

contrast.ignorance: Chasing transient worldly pleasures and clinging to possessions and status.
contrast.wisdom: Seeing the world as a bubble, cultivating ethical purity, and maintaining equanimity.

verses.analogy: Like a swan rising into the sky, leaving its watery home behind without regret.
verses.real_life: A person who handles the loss of a job or relationship with grace, wishing others well without bitterness.

actions: Notice if you feel competitive today, and practice letting go of the need to win.
Write down three things you are genuinely content with in your life right now.
reflection_questions: Am I making my happiness dependent on things I cannot control?
How can I cultivate more simple contentment today?

perspectives.spiritual: The goal is the complete destruction of craving and the realization of final peace.
perspectives.philosophical: True freedom is autonomy from the dualities of pleasure and pain.
perspectives.practical: Choose silent reflection and simplify your daily lifestyle to reduce mental clutter.
perspectives.leadership: A wise leader acts with empathy, does not play favorites, and remains calm under pressure.

similar_ideas.idea: The mind is restless and hard to curb, but it can be controlled by practice.
Indeed, Allah will not change the condition of a people until they change what is in themselves.

verses.insight: He who has no enemies within has no enemies without.

verse.original: खुद्दकनिकाय धम्मपद पियवग्ग तयोजनपब्बजितवत्थु अयोगे युञ्जमत्तानं, योगस्मिञ्च अयोजयं; अत्थं हित्वा पियग्गाही, पिहेतत्तानुयोगिनं।
verse.transliteration: Khuddakanikāya Dhammapada Piyavagga Tayojanapabbajitavatthu Ayoge yuñjamattānaṁ, yogasmiñca ayojayaṁ; Atthaṁ hitvā piyaggāhī, pihetattānuyoginaṁ.
verse.translation: Minor Collection Sayings of the Dhamma 209–220 16. The Beloved Applying yourself where you ought not, neglecting what you should be doing, forgetting your goal, you cling to what you hold dear, jealous of those devoted to their heart’s goal.

meaning.word_by_word: अयोगे (in what is improper/unfitting), युञ्जम्-अत्तानं [युञ्जम् अत्तानं] (engaging oneself), योगस्मिञ्-च (and in what is proper/devotion), अयोजयं (not applying); अत्थं (true good/welfare), हित्वा (having left behind), पियग्गाही [पिय-गाही] (clinging to the pleasant), पिहयत्तानुयोगिनं [पिहयति अत्तानुयोगिनं] (envies those who apply themselves)
meaning.overall: Minor Collection Sayings of the Dhamma 209–220 16. The Beloved Applying yourself where you ought not, neglecting what you should be doing, forgetting your goal, you cling to what you hold dear, jealous of those devoted to their heart’s goal.

interpretation.traditional: In the Piyavagga (Chapter on Pleasure/Affection), the Buddha analyzes the dual nature of affection (piya) and aversion (appiya). Clinging to favorites and rejecting the unpleasant are shown to be the primary hooks of sorrow and fear. Traditional commentators explain that the Arahants have gone beyond liking and disliking, finding freedom from all mental bonds.
interpretation.core_principle: Understanding the world, following the Buddha's example, finding true happiness, and letting go of attachment are essential for liberation.
interpretation.psychological: Attachment styles shape our emotional stability. Clinging to others for validation or safety creates anxiety. Cultivating secure, spacious connection without ownership allows us to love without fear.

explanation.simple: Do not cling to favorites or make things dear to you, because losing them is painful. When you are free from personal likes and dislikes, you are free from sorrow and fear.
explanation.deep: Chapter 16 analyzes the anatomy of attachment. The Buddha shows that sorrow (soka) and fear (bhaya) arise directly from what we hold dear (piya). Clinging to transient things is a recipe for suffering. He encourages us to cultivate a mind of clear insight and virtue, which makes us dear to others while keeping us free from personal clutching.

How does fear of separation affect my capacity to love?

verse.original: मा पियेहि समागञ्छि, अप्पियेहि कुदाचनं; पियानं अदस्सनं दुक्खं, अप्पियानञ्च दस्सनं।
verse.transliteration: Mā piyehi samāgañchi, appiyehi kudācanaṁ; Piyānaṁ adassanaṁ dukkhaṁ, appiyānañca dassanaṁ.
verse.translation: Don’t ever get too close to those you like or dislike. For not seeing the liked is suffering, and so is seeing the disliked.

meaning.word_by_word: मा (do not), पियेहि (with the loved), समागञ्छि (associate), अप्पियेहि (with the unloved), कुदाचनं (ever); पियानं (of the loved), अदस्सनं (not seeing), दुक्खं (is painful), अप्पियानञ्-च (and of the unloved), दस्सनं (seeing)
meaning.overall: Don’t ever get too close to those you like or dislike. For not seeing the liked is suffering, and so is seeing the disliked.

interpretation.traditional: In the Piyavagga (Chapter on Pleasure/Affection), the Buddha analyzes the dual nature of affection (piya) and aversion (appiya). verse.original: तस्मा पियं न कयिराथ, पियापायो हि पापको; गन्था तेसं न विज्जन्ति, येसं नत्थि पियाप्पियं।
verse.transliteration: Tasmā piyaṁ na kayirātha, piyāpāyo hi pāpako; Ganthā tesaṁ na vijjanti, yesaṁ natthi piyāppiyaṁ.
verse.translation: Therefore don’t hold anything dear, for it’s bad to lose those you love. No ties are found in they who hold nothing loved or loathed.

meaning.word_by_word: तस्मा (therefore), पियं (dear/loved), न (not), कयिराथ (make), पियापायो [पिय-अपायो] (separation from the loved), हि (indeed), पापको (is bad/painful); गन्था (bonds), तेसं (for them), न (not), विज्जञ्ति (exist), येसं (for whom), नत्थि (there is no), पियाप्पियं [पिय-अप्पियं] (dear and undear)
meaning.overall: Therefore hold nothing dear, for separation from the dear is painful. No bonds exist for those to whom there is neither dear nor undear.

interpretation.traditional: The Buddha points to the root of sorrow: attachment (piya). Clinging to people or things guarantees suffering because impermanence makes separation inevitable. Going beyond the duality of 'dear' (piya) and 'undear' (appiya) is the path to complete freedom.
interpretation.core_principle: Personal attachment and aversion are the bonds that chain us to suffering; equanimity transcends these dualities.
interpretation.psychological: Conditional happiness (attaching our peace to a specific person or object remaining unchanged) makes us fragile. True psychological freedom is a state of open appreciation without ownership or clutching.

explanation.simple: Do not cling to things or people, because losing them is painful. When you have no preferences of liking or disliking, you are completely free from bonds.
explanation.deep: This is one of the Buddha's most radical psychological teachings. 'Tasmā piyaṁ na kayirātha'—therefore do not make things dear. To the ordinary mind, this sounds cold or depressing. But the Buddha is pointing to the difference between *love* and *clinging*. Love is spacious, giving, and accepts impermanence. Clinging is clutching, defensive, and terrified of loss. Clinging creates 'piyāppiyaṁ'—a world of favorites and enemies. When we release the grip, the bonds ('ganthā') dissolve, leaving room for universal, unconditioned love.

logic.premise: Everything we cling to will change and separate from us.
logic.observation: Separation from what is clung to causes grief and fear.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, to end grief and fear, we must transcend the dualities of personal attachment and aversion.

reasoning.flow: Attachment to Favorites → Fear of Loss → Inevitable Separation → Grief. Resolution: Equanimity & Letting Go.

tension.human_doubt: If I don't hold anyone dear, won't my relationships become cold, detached, and unloving?
tension.resolution: No. The opposite is true. When you stop clinging, you stop trying to control or use the other person for your security. Your love becomes cleaner, more supportive, and completely free from the shadow of anxiety and jealousy. You love them for *their* sake, not for *your* security.

contrast.ignorance: Believing that jealousy, possessiveness, and fear of loss are signs of deep, genuine love.
contrast.wisdom: Caring deeply for others while fully accepting that they are not yours, and that they will change and eventually pass away.

verses.analogy: Like enjoying a beautiful sunset—you don't try to capture it in a box; you appreciate it fully as it fades.
verses.real_life: A parent who supports their child's decision to move to another country, feeling joy for their growth rather than clinging to their own comfort.

actions: Identify one relationship or possession you are clutching tightly, and practice saying internally: 'This is not mine. This is a temporary gift.'
Notice when you split the world into 'things I like' and 'things I hate,' and step back into equanimity.
reflection_questions: What is the difference between loving someone and clinging to them?
Why am I afraid of letting go of my attachments?

perspectives.spiritual: Transcending the dualities of attraction (raga) and repulsion (dvesha) is the goal of yoga.
perspectives.philosophical: Stoic detachment: recognize that external relationships are 'indifferents' to be appreciated but not clung to.
perspectives.practical: In investments and business: make decisions based on data, not emotional attachments to projects or assets.
perspectives.leadership: A leader must remain objective, avoiding favorites (piya) or personal biases (appiya) in team management.

similar_ideas.idea: One who is without attachment in all circumstances, who does not rejoice when obtaining good nor grieve when obtaining evil, is firmly established in wisdom.
When you kiss your child or wife, say to yourself that you are kissing a mortal; then, if they die, you will not be disturbed.

verses.insight: Clinging is a fist; love is an open hand.

verse.original: अञ्ञतरकुटुम्बिकवत्थु पियतो जायती सोको, पियतो जायती भयं; पियतो विप्पमुत्तस्स, नत्थि सोको कुतो भयं।
verse.transliteration: Aññatarakuṭumbikavatthu Piyato jāyatī soko, piyato jāyatī bhayaṁ; Piyato vippamuttassa, natthi soko kuto bhayaṁ.
verse.translation: Sorrow springs from what we hold dear, fear springs from what we hold dear; one free from holding anything dear has no sorrow, let alone fear.

meaning.word_by_word: पियतो (from what is loved), जायती (arises), सोको (sorrow), पियतो (from what is loved), जायती (arises), भयं (fear); पियतो (from what is loved), विप्पमुत्तस्स (for one who is fully freed), नत्थि (there is no), सोको (sorrow), कुतो (whence), भयं (fear)
meaning.overall: Sorrow springs from what we hold dear, fear springs from what we hold dear; one free from holding anything dear has no sorrow, let alone fear.

verse.original: अनित्थिगन्धकुमारवत्थु कामतो जायती सोको, कामतो जायती भयं; कामतो विप्पमुत्तस्स, नत्थि सोको कुतो भयं।
verse.transliteration: Anitthigandhakumāravatthu Kāmato jāyatī soko, kāmato jāyatī bhayaṁ; Kāmato vippamuttassa, natthi soko kuto bhayaṁ.
verse.translation: Sorrow springs from desire, fear springs from desire; one free from desire has no sorrow, let alone fear.

meaning.word_by_word: कामतो (from sensual desire), जायती (arises), सोको (sorrow), कामतो (from sensual desire), जायती (arises), भयं (fear); कामतो (from sensual desire), विप्पमुत्तस्स (for one who is fully freed), नत्थि (there is no), सोको (sorrow), कुतो (whence), भयं (fear)
meaning.overall: Sorrow springs from desire, fear springs from desire; one free from desire has no sorrow, let alone fear.

interpretation.traditional: In the Piyavagga (Chapter on Pleasure/Affection), the Buddha analyzes the dual nature of affection (piya) and aversion (appiya). Clinging to favorites and rejecting the unpleasant are shown to be the primary hooks of sorrow and fear. Traditional commentators explain that the Arahants have gone beyond liking and disliking, finding freedom from all mental bonds.
interpretation.core_principle: Understanding the world, following the Buddha's example, finding true happiness, and letting go of attachment are essential for liberation.
interpretation.psychological: Attachment styles shape our emotional stability. Clinging to others for validation or safety creates anxiety. Cultivating secure, spacious connection without ownership allows us to love without fear.

explanation.simple: Do not cling to favorites or make things dear to you, because losing them is painful. When you are free from personal likes and dislikes, you are free from sorrow and fear.
explanation.deep: Chapter 16 analyzes the anatomy of attachment. The Buddha shows that sorrow (soka) and fear (bhaya) arise directly from what we hold dear (piya). Clinging to transient things is a recipe for suffering. He encourages us to cultivate a mind of clear insight and virtue, which makes us dear to others while keeping us free from personal clutching.

logic.premise: Every action shapes our path; taming desires is the only road to peace.
logic.observation: Hostility, sorrow, and fear arise from attachment; the wise remain peaceful and friendly.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, we must abandon attachment and practice the Buddhas' teachings.

reasoning.flow: Sensation → Choice to Let Go → Equanimity → Unconditioned Happiness → Liberation

tension.human_doubt: If I let go of attachment, how will I love my family?
tension.resolution: Letting go of attachment means letting go of the *possessiveness* and *clinging* that cause fear. It allows you to love them more purely and selflessly.

contrast.ignorance: Chasing transient worldly pleasures and clinging to possessions and status.
contrast.wisdom: Seeing the world as a bubble, cultivating ethical purity, and maintaining equanimity.

verses.analogy: Like a swan rising into the sky, leaving its watery home behind without regret.
verses.real_life: A person who handles the loss of a job or relationship with grace, wishing others well without bitterness.

verse.original: पञ्चसतदारकवत्थु सीलदस्सनसम्पन्नं, धम्मट्ठं सच्चवेदिनं; अत्तनो कम्म कुब्बानं, तं जनो कुरुते पियं।
verse.transliteration: Pañcasatadārakavatthu Sīladassanasampannaṁ, dhammaṭṭhaṁ saccavedinaṁ; Attano kamma kubbānaṁ, taṁ jano kurute piyaṁ.
verse.translation: One accomplished in virtue and vision, firmly principled, and truthful, doing oneself what ought be done: that’s who the people love.

meaning.word_by_word: सीलदस्सनसम्पन्नं [सील-दस्सन-सम्पन्नं] (endowed with virtue and insight), धम्मट्ठं (established in Dhamma), सच्चवादिनं (speaking truth); अत्तनो (one's own), कम्मं (work/duty), कुब्बानं (performing), तं (him), जनो (people), कुरुते (make), पियं (dear)
meaning.overall: One accomplished in virtue and vision, firmly principled, and truthful, doing oneself what ought be done: that’s who the people love.

verse.original: एकअनागामित्थेरवत्थु छन्दजातो अनक्खाते, मनसा च फुटो सिया; कामेसु च अप्पटिबद्धचित्तो, उद्धंसोतोति वुच्चति।
verse.transliteration: Ekaanāgāmittheravatthu Chandajāto anakkhāte, Manasā ca phuṭo siyā; Kāmesu ca appaṭibaddhacitto, Uddhaṁsototi vuccati.
verse.translation: One eager to realize the ineffable would be filled with awareness. Their mind not bound to pleasures of sense, they’re said to be heading upstream.

meaning.word_by_word: छन्दजातो (in whom desire has arisen), अनक्खाते (for the undeclared/Nibbana), मनसा (with the mind), च (and), फुटो (touched/suffused); कामेसु (in sensual pleasures), च (and), अप्पतिबद्धचित्तो [अप्पतिबद्ध-चित्तो] (whose mind is unattached), उद्धंसोतो [उद्धं-सोतो] (upstream-bound/bound for higher realms), ति (thus), वुच्चति (is called)
meaning.overall: One eager to realize the ineffable would be filled with awareness. Their mind not bound to pleasures of sense, they’re said to be heading upstream.

verse.original: नन्दियवत्थु चिरप्पवासिं पुरिसं, दूरतो सोत्थिमागतं; ञातिमित्ता सुहज्जा च, अभिनन्दन्ति आगतं।
verse.transliteration: Nandiyavatthu Cirappavāsiṁ purisaṁ, dūrato sotthimāgataṁ; Ñātimittā suhajjā ca, abhinandanti āgataṁ.
verse.translation: When a man returns safely after a long time spent abroad, family, friends, and loved ones celebrate his return.

meaning.overall: When a man returns safely after a long time spent abroad, family, friends, and loved ones celebrate his return.

verse.original: तथेव कतपुञ्ञम्पि, अस्मा लोका परं गतं; पुञ्ञानि पटिगण्हन्ति, पियं ञातीव आगतं। पियवग्गो सोऌअसमो।
verse.transliteration: Tatheva katapuññampi, asmā lokā paraṁ gataṁ; Puññāni paṭigaṇhanti, piyaṁ ñātīva āgataṁ. Piyavaggo soḷasamo.
verse.translation: Just so, when one who has done good goes from this world to the next, their good deeds receive them there, as family welcomes home one they love.

meaning.word_by_word: तथेव (just so), कतपुञ्ञम्पि [कत-पुञ्ञं अपि] (one who has done good deeds), अस्मा (from this), लोका (world), परं (to the next), गतं (gone); पुण्यानि (good deeds), पटिगण्हन्ति (receive), पियं (dear), ञातीव (like relatives), आगतं (returned)
meaning.overall: Just so, when one who has done good goes from this world to the next, their good deeds receive them there, as family welcomes home one they love.

verse.original: खुद्दकनिकाय धम्मपद कोधवग्ग रोहिनीखत्तियकञ्ञावत्थु कोधं जहे विप्पजहेय्य मानं, संयोजनं सब्बमतिक्कमेय्य; तं नामरूपस्मिमसज्जमानं, अकिञ्चनं नानुपतन्ति दुक्खा।
verse.transliteration: Khuddakanikāya Dhammapada Kodhavagga Rohinīkhattiyakaññāvatthu Kodhaṁ jahe vippajaheyya mānaṁ, Saṁyojanaṁ sabbamatikkameyya; Taṁ nāmarūpasmimasajjamānaṁ, Akiñcanaṁ nānupatanti dukkhā.
verse.translation: Minor Collection Sayings of the Dhamma 221–234 17. Anger Give up anger, get rid of conceit, and escape every fetter. Sufferings don’t befall one who has nothing, not clinging to name and form.

meaning.word_by_word: कोधं (anger), जहे (one should give up), विप्पजहेय्य (one should abandon), मानं (pride), सञ्ञोजनं (fetter), सब्बं (all), अतिक्कमेय्य (one should transcend); तं (him), नामरूपस्मिं (in mind and body), अ-सज्जमानं (not clinging), अकिञ्चनं (having nothing/free from attachments), न (not), अनुपतन्ति (befall/follow), दुक्खा (suffering)
meaning.overall: Minor Collection Sayings of the Dhamma 221–234 17. Anger Give up anger, get rid of conceit, and escape every fetter. Sufferings don’t befall one who has nothing, not clinging to name and form.

interpretation.traditional: In the Kodhavagga (Chapter on Anger), the Buddha describes the destructive nature of anger (kodha) and the value of restraint. Anger is compared to a speeding, out-of-control chariot. Traditional Theravada commentary highlights that the true charioteer is one who restrains rising anger, while others merely hold the reins. The wise conquer anger with non-anger (love) and falsehood with truth.
interpretation.core_principle: Every action has consequences; the wise purify their minds gradually and follow the path of truth.
interpretation.psychological: Anger is a high-arousal threat response. When triggered, the amygdala overrides the rational prefrontal cortex. Restraining anger is the practice of self-regulation, using mindfulness to pause the reaction loop and choose an empathetic response.

explanation.simple: Conquer anger with kindness, wickedness with goodness, stinginess with giving, and lies with truth. Watch your actions, words, and thoughts carefully so you do not hurt others.
explanation.deep: Chapter 17 analyzes anger as a primary defilement of the mind. The Buddha teaches that anger is a fire that burns the vessel containing it before hurting others. He emphasizes that the path of peace requires guarding the three doors of action: body, speech, and mind. Restraining anger is the highest self-mastery, yielding immediate peace and long-term spiritual growth.

logic.premise: Moral conduct and mindfulness are the foundations of mental purification.
logic.observation: Taming anger, removing impurities, and weighing decisions impartially lead to peace.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, one must consistently practice self-restraint and walk the Eightfold Path.

actions: Practice pausing and taking three deep breaths whenever you feel frustrated today.
Respond to a harsh comment with gentleness or polite silence.

verse.original: अञ्ञतरभिक्खुवत्थु यो वे उप्पतितं कोधं, रथं भन्तंव वारये; तमहं सारथिं ब्रूमि, रस्मिग्गाहो इतरो जनो।
verse.transliteration: Aññatarabhikkhuvatthu Yo ve uppatitaṁ kodhaṁ, rathaṁ bhantaṁva vāraye; Tamahaṁ sārathiṁ brūmi, rasmiggāho itaro jano.
verse.translation: When anger surges like a lurching chariot, keep it in check. That’s what I call a charioteer; others just hold the reins.

meaning.word_by_word: यो (whoever), वे (indeed), उप्पतितं (arisen), कोधं (anger), धरयति (restrains/holds back), रथं (chariot), भन्तं (rolling/speeding), व (like); तम्-अहं (him I), सारथिं (charioteer), ब्रूमि (call), रस्मिग्गाहो (holding the reins), इतरो (others), जनो (people)
meaning.overall: He who restrains rising anger like a rolling chariot—him I call a charioteer; others are merely holding the reins.

interpretation.traditional: Theravada commentary explains that the Buddha distinguishes between active control of the mind and passive compliance. One who actually halts the speed of anger is a true master (sārathi), while others merely hold the reins of their habits without directing them.
interpretation.core_principle: True self-control is active restraint of destructive impulses in real time, not merely maintaining the appearance of discipline.
interpretation.psychological: Anger triggers a powerful biological cascade (fight-or-flight response). Restraining it requires high cognitive control (prefrontal cortex) to interrupt the automatic reaction and steer the 'chariot' of the mind safely.

explanation.simple: If you can control your anger when it rises, like stopping a fast-moving chariot, you are a true driver. Anyone else is just holding the reins.
explanation.deep: The image of the 'bhanta' chariot—speeding, swaying, out of control—is a perfect metaphor for anger. Once anger rises, it has momentum. Most people are swept away by it; they are just 'rasmiggāho'—passively holding the reins as the horses drag them off a cliff. The true yogi ('sārathi') has the strength and presence of mind to pull the reins and halt the vehicle. This requires immense, immediate conscious energy.

logic.premise: Anger is a rapid, high-momentum force that overrides rational control.
logic.observation: A skilled charioteer can bring a speeding, swerving chariot to a sudden halt.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, the one who actively halts rising anger is a true master, whereas others merely have the appearance of control.

reasoning.flow: Anger Arises (speeding chariot) → Immediate Awareness → Active Restraint → Re-directing Energy → True Mastery

tension.human_doubt: Isn't it healthy to express my anger? Doesn't restraining it cause psychological suppression?
tension.resolution: There is a difference between suppression (pretending you are not angry while boiling inside) and restraint (fully seeing the anger, choosing not to act on it, and letting the energy cool down). Restraint is born of wisdom; suppression is born of fear. Wisdom heals; suppression harms.

contrast.ignorance: Letting anger dictate your words and actions, calling it 'authenticity' or 'venting.'
contrast.wisdom: Interrogating the rising heat of anger and choosing silence or constructive action instead.

verses.analogy: Like a driver who slams on the brakes when they see a cliff ahead, rather than letting the car plunge over.
verses.real_life: A manager who receives a disrespectful email from an employee, feels a wave of fury, but closes the laptop to walk around the block before responding.

actions: When you feel anger or frustration rising today, pause and say: 'The chariot is rolling. Pull the reins.'
Take three slow, deep breaths before speaking in any tense situation.
reflection_questions: Am I a true driver of my mind, or do my emotions drag me where they want?
What is the trigger that makes my chariot roll fastest?

perspectives.spiritual: Anger (kodha) is one of the three primary unwholesome roots (akusala-mula) that bind us to samsara.
perspectives.philosophical: Seneca on Anger: 'Anger is a temporary madness... the best cure for anger is delay.'
perspectives.practical: Use the '24-hour rule' before responding to conflict in business or personal life.
perspectives.leadership: A leader who controls their temper maintains authority and respect; an angry leader signals vulnerability and lack of control.

similar_ideas.idea: From anger arises delusion, from delusion loss of memory, from loss of memory ruin of reason, from ruin of reason one perishes.
How much more harmful are the consequences of anger and grief than the circumstances that aroused them.

verses.insight: To rule yourself is the highest throne.

verse.original: उत्तराउपासिकवत्थु अक्कोधेन जिने कोधं, असाधुं साधुना जिने; जिने कदरियं दानेन, सच्चेनालिकवादिनं।
verse.transliteration: Uttarāupāsikavatthu Akkodhena jine kodhaṁ, asādhuṁ sādhunā jine; Jine kadariyaṁ dānena, saccenālikavādinaṁ.
verse.translation: Defeat anger with kindness, villainy with virtue, stinginess with giving, and lies with truth.

meaning.word_by_word: अक्कोधेन (by non-anger/love), जिने (conquer), कोधं (anger), असाधुं (bad/wicked), साधुना (by good), जिने (conquer); जिने (conquer), कदरियं (the stingy/miser), दानेन (by giving/generosity), सच्चेन (by truth), अलिकवादिनं (the liar/speaker of untruth)
meaning.overall: Defeat anger with kindness, villainy with virtue, stinginess with giving, and lies with truth.

interpretation.traditional: In the Kodhavagga (Chapter on Anger), the Buddha describes the destructive nature of anger (kodha) and the value of restraint. Anger is compared to a speeding, out-of-control chariot. Traditional Theravada commentary highlights that the true charioteer is one who restrains rising anger, while others merely hold the reins. The wise conquer anger with non-anger (love) and falsehood with truth.
interpretation.core_principle: Every action has consequences; the wise purify their minds gradually and follow the path of truth.

verse.original: महामोग्गल्लानपञ्हवत्थु सच्चं भणे न कुज्झेय्य, दज्जा अप्पम्पि याचितो; एतेहि तीहि ठानेहि, गच्छे देवान सन्तिके।
verse.transliteration: Mahāmoggallānapañhavatthu Saccaṁ bhaṇe na kujjheyya, dajjā appampi yācito; Etehi tīhi ṭhānehi, gacche devāna santike.
verse.translation: Speak the truth, do not be angry, and give when asked, if only a little. By these three means, you may enter the presence of the gods.

meaning.word_by_word: सच्चं (truth), भणे (one should speak), न (not), कुज्झेय्य (should get angry), दज्जा (one should give), अप्पस्मिञ्-च (even from a little), याचितो (being asked); एतेहि (by these), तीहि (three), ठानेहि (factors), गच्छे (one goes), देवान (of gods), सन्तिके (to the presence)
meaning.overall: Speak the truth, do not be angry, and give when asked, if only a little. By these three means, you may enter the presence of the gods.

verse.original: बुद्धपितुब्राह्मणवत्थु अहिंसका ये मुनयो, निच्चं कायेन संवुता; ते यन्ति अच्चुतं ठानं, यत्थ गन्त्वा न सोचरे।
verse.transliteration: Buddhapitubrāhmaṇavatthu Ahiṁsakā ye munayo, niccaṁ kāyena saṁvutā; Te yanti accutaṁ ṭhānaṁ, yattha gantvā na socare.
verse.translation: Those harmless sages, always restrained in body, go to the state that does not pass, where there is no sorrow.

meaning.word_by_word: अहिंसका (non-harming/harmless), ये (those who), मुनिनो (sages), निच्चं (always), कायेन (by the body), संवुता (restrained); ते (they), यन्ति (go), अच्चुतं (the deathless/unchanging), ठानं (state), यत्थ (where), गन्त्वा (having gone), न (not), सोचरे (they sorrow)
meaning.overall: Those harmless sages, always restrained in body, go to the state that does not pass, where there is no sorrow.

verse.original: पुण्णदासीवत्थु सदा जागरमानानं, अहोरत्तानुसिक्खिनं; निब्बानं अधिमुत्तानं, अत्थं गच्छन्ति आसवा।
verse.transliteration: Puṇṇadāsīvatthu Sadā jāgaramānānaṁ, ahorattānusikkhinaṁ; Nibbānaṁ adhimuttānaṁ, atthaṁ gacchanti āsavā.
verse.translation: Always wakeful, practicing night and day, focused only on extinguishment, their defilements come to an end.

meaning.word_by_word: सदा (always), जागरमानानं (of those who stay awake/vigilant), अहोरत्त-अनुसिक्खिनं (training day and night), निब्बानं (extinguishment), अधिमुत्तानं (intent upon), अत्थं (destruction), गच्छन्ति (go), आसवा (mental taints)
meaning.overall: Always wakeful, practicing night and day, focused only on extinguishment, their defilements come to an end.

verse.original: अतुलौपासकवत्थु पोराणमेतं अतुल, नेतं अज्जतनामिव; निन्दन्ति तुण्हिमासीनं, निन्दन्ति बहुभाणिनं; मितभाणिम्पि निन्दन्ति, नत्थि लोके अनिन्दितो।
verse.transliteration: Atulaupāsakavatthu Porāṇametaṁ atula, netaṁ ajjatanāmiva; Nindanti tuṇhimāsīnaṁ, nindanti bahubhāṇinaṁ; Mitabhāṇimpi nindanti, natthi loke anindito.
verse.translation: It’s always been this way, Atula, it’s not just today. They blame you when you’re silent, they blame you when you speak a lot, and even when you speak just right: no-one in the world escapes blame.

meaning.word_by_word: पोराणम्-एतं [पोराणम् एतं] (this is an ancient saying), अतुल (O Atula), नेतं [न एतं] (this is not), अज्जतनं (of today); निन्दन्ति (they blame), तुण्ही-म्-आसीनं (one sitting silent), निन्दन्ति (they blame), बहु-भाणिनं (one speaking much), मितभाणिम्पि [मित-भाणिनं अपि] (even one speaking moderately), निन्दन्ति (they blame), नत्थि (there is no), लोके (in the world), अनिन्दितो (unblamed person)
meaning.overall: It’s always been this way, Atula, it’s not just today. They blame you when you’re silent, they blame you when you speak a lot, and even when you speak just right: no-one in the world escapes blame.

verse.original: न चाहु न च भविस्सति, न चेतरहि विज्जति; एकन्तं निन्दितो पोसो, एकन्तं वा पसंसितो।
verse.transliteration: Na cāhu na ca bhavissati, na cetarahi vijjati; Ekantaṁ nindito poso, ekantaṁ vā pasaṁsito.
verse.translation: There never was, nor will be, nor is there today, someone who is wholly praised or wholly blamed.

meaning.word_by_word: न (not), चाहु [च अहु] (and there was not), न (not), च (and), भविस्सिमि (will be), न (not), च (and), एतरहि (now), विज्जति (is found), एकन्तं (solely/entirely), निन्दितो (blamed person), पोषो (person), एकन्तं (solely), वा (or), पसंसितो (praised)
meaning.overall: There never was, nor will be, nor is there today, someone who is wholly praised or wholly blamed.

interpretation.traditional: In the Kodhavagga (Chapter on Anger), the Buddha describes the destructive nature of anger (kodha) and the value of restraint. Anger is compared to a speeding, out-of-control chariot. Traditional Theravada commentary highlights that the true charioteer is one who restrains rising anger, while others merely hold the reins. The wise conquer anger with non-anger (love) and falsehood with truth.
interpretation.core_principle: Every action has consequences; the wise purify their minds gradually and follow the path of truth.
interpretation.psychological: Anger is a high-arousal threat response. When triggered, the amygdala overrides the rational prefrontal cortex. Restraining anger is the practice of self-regulation, using mindfulness to pause the reaction loop and choose an empathetic response.

explanation.simple: Conquer anger with kindness, wickedness with goodness, stinginess with giving, and lies with truth. Watch your actions, words, and thoughts carefully so you do not hurt others.
explanation.deep: Chapter 17 analyzes anger as a primary defilement of the mind. The Buddha teaches that anger is a fire that burns the vessel containing it before hurting others. He emphasizes that the path of peace requires guarding the three doors of action: body, speech, and mind. Restraining anger is the highest self-mastery, yielding immediate peace and long-term spiritual growth.

logic.premise: Moral conduct and mindfulness are the foundations of mental purification.
logic.observation: Taming anger, removing impurities, and weighing decisions impartially lead to peace.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, one must consistently practice self-restraint and walk the Eightfold Path.

reasoning.flow: Stimulus → Mindfulness → Restraint of Senses → Pure Intention → Liberation

tension.human_doubt: How do I stay committed when the world constantly tempts my greed and anger?
tension.resolution: Recognize that temptation and anger are just passing states. Guard your mind like a fortress and find the inner refuge that never changes.

verse.original: यञ्चे विञ्ञू पसंसन्ति, अनुविच्च सुवे सुवे; अच्छिद्दवुत्तिं मेधाविं, पञ्ञासीलसमाहितं।
verse.transliteration: Yañce viññū pasaṁsanti, anuvicca suve suve; Acchiddavuttiṁ medhāviṁ, paññāsīlasamāhitaṁ.
verse.translation: If, after watching them day in day out, discerning people praise that sage of impeccable conduct, endowed with ethics and wisdom;

meaning.overall: If, after watching them day in day out, discerning people praise that sage of impeccable conduct, endowed with ethics and wisdom;

verse.original: निक्खं जम्बोनदस्सेव, को तं निन्दितुमरहति; देवापि नं पसंसन्ति, ब्रह्मुनापि पसंसितो।
verse.transliteration: Nikkhaṁ jambonadasseva, ko taṁ ninditumarahati; Devāpi naṁ pasaṁsanti, brahmunāpi pasaṁsito.
verse.translation: like a pendant of Black Plum River gold, who is worthy to criticize them? Even the gods praise them, and by the Divinity, too, they’re praised.

meaning.word_by_word: निक्खं (gold coin), जम्बोनदस्स्ēव [जम्बोनदस इव] (like of pure gold), को (who), तं (him), निन्दितु-मरहति [निन्दितुम् अरहति] (is worthy to blame); देवापि (even the gods), नं (him), पसंसन्ति (praise), ब्रह्मुनापि [ब्रह्मुना अपि] (even by Brahma), पसंसितो (he is praised)
meaning.overall: like a pendant of Black Plum River gold, who is worthy to criticize them? Even the gods praise them, and by the Divinity, too, they’re praised.

verse.original: छब्बग्गियवत्थु कायप्पकोपं रक्खेय्य, कायेन संवुतो सिया; कायदुच्चरितं हित्वा, कायेन सुचरितं चरे।
verse.transliteration: Chabbaggiyavatthu Kāyappakopaṁ rakkheyya, kāyena saṁvuto siyā; Kāyaduccaritaṁ hitvā, kāyena sucaritaṁ care.
verse.translation: Guard against ill-tempered deeds, be restrained in body. Giving up bad bodily conduct, conduct yourself well in body.

meaning.word_by_word: कायप्पकोपं [काय-पकोपं] (bodily anger/misconduct), रक्खेय्य (one should guard against), कायेन (in body), संवुतो (restrained), सिया (should be); काय-दुच्चरितं (bodily misconduct), हित्वा (having abandoned), कायेन (with the body), सुचरितं (good conduct), चरे (one should practice)
meaning.overall: Guard against ill-tempered deeds, be restrained in body. Giving up bad bodily conduct, conduct yourself well in body.

verse.original: कायेन संवुता धीरा, अथो वाचाय संवुता; मनसा संवुता धीरा, ते वे सुपरिसंवुता। कोधवग्गो सत्तरसमो।
verse.transliteration: Kāyena saṁvutā dhīrā, atho vācāya saṁvutā; Manasā saṁvutā dhīrā, te ve suparisaṁvutā. Kodhavaggo sattarasamo.
verse.translation: An attentive one is restrained in body restrained also in speech, in thought, too, they are restrained: they are restrained in every way.

meaning.word_by_word: कायेन (in body), संवुता (restrained), धीरा (the wise), अथो (likewise), वाचाय (in speech), संवुता (restrained); मनसा (in mind), संवुता (restrained), धीरा (the wise), ते (they), वे (indeed), परिसंवुता (fully restrained)
meaning.overall: An attentive one is restrained in body restrained also in speech, in thought, too, they are restrained: they are restrained in every way.

He emphasizes that the path of peace requires guarding the three doors of action: body, speech, and mind. Restraining anger is the highest self-mastery, yielding immediate peace and long-term spiritual growth.

logic.premise: Moral conduct and mindfulness are the foundations of mental purification.
logic.observation: Taming anger, removing impurities, and weighing decisions impartially lead to peace.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, one must consistently practice self-restraint and walk the Eightfold Path.

reasoning.flow: Stimulus → Mindfulness → Restraint of Senses → Pure Intention → Liberation

tension.human_doubt: How do I stay committed when the world constantly tempts my greed and anger?
tension.resolution: Recognize that temptation and anger are just passing states. Guard your mind like a fortress and find the inner refuge that never changes.

contrast.ignorance: Giving in to anger, projecting faults onto others, and neglecting self-improvement.
contrast.wisdom: Dismantling ego-attachments, practicing loving-kindness, and seeking the light of wisdom.

verses.analogy: Like a silversmith patiently removing dross to make silver pure and brilliant.
verses.real_life: A person who receives criticism calmly, examines their own behavior, and continues to practice patience daily.

actions: Practice pausing and taking three deep breaths whenever you feel frustrated today.
Respond to a harsh comment with gentleness or polite silence.
reflection_questions: What is the root cause of my anger—is it fear, pride, or a desire for control?
How can I better guard the three doors of body, speech, and mind today?

perspectives.spiritual: The journey is one of gradual purification and complete awakening from ignorance.
perspectives.philosophical: True righteousness is objective, ethical, and aligned with cosmic truth (Dhamma).
perspectives.practical: Create quiet routines and keep supportive company to strengthen your character.
perspectives.leadership: A righteous leader is impartial, values transparency, and manages their own reactions before guiding others.

similar_ideas.idea: From anger arises delusion, from delusion loss of memory, from loss of memory ruin of reason, from ruin of reason one perishes.
Go from the presence of a foolish man, when thou perceivest not in him the lips of knowledge.

verses.insight: He who conquers his own anger defeats his greatest enemy.

verse.original: खुद्दकनिकाय धम्मपद मलवग्ग गोघातकपुत्तवत्थु पण्डुपलासोव दानिसि, यमपुरिसापि च ते उपट्ठिता; उय्योगमुखे च तिट्ठसि, पाथेय्यम्पि च ते न विज्जति।
verse.transliteration: Khuddakanikāya Dhammapada Malavagga Goghātakaputtavatthu Paṇḍupalāsova dānisi, Yamapurisāpi ca te upaṭṭhitā; Uyyogamukhe ca tiṭṭhasi, Pātheyyampi ca te na vijjati.
verse.translation: Minor Collection Sayings of the Dhamma 235–255 18. Stains Today you’re like a withered leaf, Yama’s men await you. You stand at the departure gates, yet you have no supplies for the road.

meaning.word_by_word: पण्डुपालसोव [पण्डु-पलासो इव] (like a withered leaf), दानिसि [दानि असि] (now you are), यमपुरिसापि [यमपुरिसा अपि] (even Yama's messengers), च (and), ते (your), उपट्ठिता (stand near); उय्योगमुखे (at the threshold of departure), च (and), तिट्ठसि (you stand), पाथेय्यं (provision for the journey), पि (also), च (and), ते (your), न (not), विज्जति (exists)
meaning.overall: Minor Collection Sayings of the Dhamma 235–255 18. Stains Today you’re like a withered leaf, Yama’s men await you. You stand at the departure gates, yet you have no supplies for the road.

interpretation.traditional: In the Malavagga (Chapter on Impurities), the Buddha uses the metaphor of a silversmith refining silver to describe the gradual removal of mental impurities (malas). The greatest impurity is declared to be ignorance (avijjā). Traditional commentary stresses that just as rust eats away the iron from which it is born, a person's own bad deeds lead them to ruin.
interpretation.core_principle: Every action has consequences; the wise purify their minds gradually and follow the path of truth.
interpretation.psychological: Projection is the ego's defense mechanism: we focus on others' faults to avoid facing our own shadows. Honest self-reflection brings integration, allowing us to gradually dismantle our maladaptive behaviors.

explanation.simple: Clean your mind from bad habits bit by bit, just as a silversmith cleans silver. Focus on correcting your own mistakes rather than pointing out other people's faults.
explanation.deep: Chapter 18 is a call to continuous self-refinement. We are urged to establish a 'lamp for ourselves' before life reaches its end. The Buddha lists various impurities—laziness in home life, misconduct in relationships, stinginess in giving—and identifies ignorance as the root dross. The wise person inspects their own faults constantly, rather than winnowing others' faults like chaff.

logic.conclusion: Therefore, one must consistently practice self-restraint and walk the Eightfold Path.

Spend 10 minutes in quiet reflection on your habits, identifying one area that needs refining.

What is the dross in my character that most needs refining right now?

verse.original: सो करोहि दीपमत्तनो, खिप्पं वायम पण्डितो भव; निद्धन्तमलो अनङ्गणो, दिब्बं अरियभूमिं उपेहिसि।
verse.transliteration: So karohi dīpamattano, Khippaṁ vāyama paṇḍito bhava; Niddhantamalo anaṅgaṇo, Dibbaṁ ariyabhūmiṁ upehisi.
verse.translation: Make an island of yourself! Swiftly strive, learn to be wise! Purged of stains, flawless, you’ll go to the heavenly realm of the noble ones.

meaning.word_by_word: सो (you), करोहि (make), दीपम्-अत्तनो [दीपम् अत्तनो] (a lamp for yourself), खिप्पं (quickly), वायम (strive), पण्डितो (wise), भव (become); निद्धन्तमलो [निद्धन्त-मलो] (blown away your impurities), अनङ्गणो (blemishless), दिब्बं (divine), अरियभूमिं (noble land), उपेहिसि (you will enter)
meaning.overall: Make an island of yourself! Swiftly strive, learn to be wise! Purged of stains, flawless, you’ll go to the heavenly realm of the noble ones.

verse.original: उपनीतवयो च दानिसि, सम्पयातोसि यमस्स सन्तिकं; वासो ते नत्थि अन्तरा, पाथेय्यम्पि च ते न विज्जति।
verse.transliteration: Upanītavayo ca dānisi, Sampayātosi yamassa santikaṁ; Vāso te natthi antarā, Pātheyyampi ca te na vijjati.
verse.translation: You’ve journeyed the stages of life, and now you set out to meet Yama. Along the way there’s nowhere to stay, yet you have no supplies for the road.

meaning.word_by_word: उपनीतवयो [उपनीत-वयो] (brought to the end of life), च (and), दानिसि (now you are), यमस्स (of Yama/Death), सन्तिके (presence), सम्पयातोसि [सम्पयातो असि] (you have set forth); वासो (resting place), पि (also), च (and), ते (your), न (not), अत्थि (exists), अन्तरा (on the way), पाथेय्यं (provision), पि (also), च (and), ते (your), न (not), विज्जति (exists)
meaning.overall: You’ve journeyed the stages of life, and now you set out to meet Yama. Along the way there’s nowhere to stay, yet you have no supplies for the road.

meaning.overall: Make an island of yourself! Swiftly strive, learn to be wise! Purged of stains, flawless, you’ll not come again to rebirth and old age.

verse.original: अञ्ञतरब्राह्मणवत्थु अनुपुब्बेन मेधावी, थोकं थोकं खणे खणे; कम्मारो रजतस्सेव, निद्धमे मलमत्तनो।
verse.transliteration: Aññatarabrāhmaṇavatthu Anupubbena medhāvī, thokaṁ thokaṁ khaṇe khaṇe; Kammāro rajatasseva, niddhame malamattano.
verse.translation: A smart person would purge their own stains gradually, bit by bit, moment by moment, like a smith smelting silver.

meaning.word_by_word: अनुपुब्बेन (gradually), मेधावी (the wise person), थोकं थोकं (bit by bit), खणे खणे (moment by moment); कम्मारो (silversmith), रजतस्सेव [रजतस्स इव] (like of silver), निद्धमे (should blow away), मलम्-अत्तनो [मलम् अत्तनो] (his own impurities)
meaning.overall: Bit by bit, moment by moment, a wise person should blow away their own impurities, as a silversmith blows away dross from silver.

interpretation.traditional: The Buddha emphasizes that spiritual purification is not a sudden, magical event. It is a gradual (anupubbena), meticulous process requiring constant vigilance, just as a silversmith repeatedly heats and refines silver to remove the dross (mala).
interpretation.core_principle: Spiritual and personal growth is a gradual, continuous process of removing negative habits and conditioning.
interpretation.psychological: Neuroplasticity and behavioral change happen through small, consistent, daily efforts ('thokaṁ thokaṁ') rather than sporadic, massive adjustments. Incremental improvement is the key to lasting change.

explanation.simple: Like a silversmith blowing away the impurities from silver bit by bit and moment by moment, a wise person cleans their own mind from bad habits gradually.
explanation.deep: The silversmith ('kammāro') does not refine silver in one heat. They apply fire, scrape off the rising dross, let it cool, and repeat. If they apply too much heat at once, they ruin the metal. The spiritual path ('anupubbena') requires the same gentle, persistent heat of discipline. We must watch our thoughts 'khaṇe khaṇe'—moment by moment—gently removing greed, anger, and distraction as they rise to the surface.

logic.premise: The mind is covered in deep-seated impurities (conditioning).
logic.observation: A silversmith refines precious metal through repeated, incremental purification.
logic.conclusion: Similarly, the wise purify their minds through patient, continuous, moment-by-moment effort.

reasoning.flow: Awareness of Dross → Applying Discipline (heat) → Removing Impurity → Cool Reflection → Repeat Gradually

tension.human_doubt: If the path is so slow and gradual, won't I get discouraged? I want peace now.
tension.resolution: Impatience is just another form of dross. Enjoy the process of refinement itself. Every small speck of dross you remove makes the silver shine slightly brighter. Focus on the next moment, not the final product.

contrast.ignorance: Expecting sudden, effortless enlightenment or behavioral shifts without daily practice.
contrast.wisdom: Valuing small, daily victories over distraction and anger, knowing they build the structure of freedom.

verses.analogy: Like a sculptor tapping away at a block of stone—each strike is small, but eventually, the statue emerges.
verses.real_life: A writer who commits to writing 300 words every morning, rather than waiting for erratic, overwhelming inspiration.

actions: Identify one tiny bad habit (e.g. checking phone first thing in morning) and commit to removing it today.
Bring complete presence to one routine task today (like washing dishes or walking to the car).
reflection_questions: Am I looking for quick spiritual bypasses, or am I willing to do the gradual work?
What is the dross that rose to the surface of my mind today?

perspectives.spiritual: The gradual path (anupubbi-patipada) is the Buddha's standard method of instruction.
perspectives.philosophical: Kaizen: continuous, incremental improvement as a philosophy of life.
perspectives.practical: Focus on building systems and habits rather than chasing big, emotional goals.
perspectives.leadership: A leader builds organizational excellence through continuous micro-improvements in processes and culture.

similar_ideas.idea: Gradually, step by step, one should achieve quietude by means of intellect sustained by full conviction.
And we all... are being transformed into his image with ever-increasing glory.

verses.insight: The mountain is climbed one small step at a time.

verse.original: तिस्सत्थेरवत्थु अयसाव मलं समुट्ठितं, ततुट्ठाय तमेव खादति; एवं अतिधोनचारिनं, सानि कम्मानि नयन्ति दुग्गतिं।
verse.transliteration: Tissattheravatthu Ayasāva malaṁ samuṭṭhitaṁ, Tatuṭṭhāya tameva khādati; Evaṁ atidhonacārinaṁ, Sāni kammāni nayanti duggatiṁ.
verse.translation: It is the rust born on the iron that eats away the place it arose. And so it is their own deeds that lead the overly-ascetic to a bad place.

meaning.word_by_word: अयसाव [अयसा इव] (like from iron), मलं (rust), समुट्ठितं (arisen), तदुट्ठाय [तत् उट्ठाय] (born from that), तमेव (that very), खादति (eats away/corrodes); एवं (so), अतिधोन-चारिनं [अतिधोनचारिनं] (one of transgressing conduct), सानि (his own), कम्मानि (deeds), नयन्ति (lead), दुग्गतिं (to state of suffering)
meaning.overall: It is the rust born on the iron that eats away the place it arose. And so it is their own deeds that lead the overly-ascetic to a bad place.

verse.original: लाऌउदायीवत्थु असज्झायमला मन्ता, अनुट्ठानमला घरा; मलं वण्णस्स कोसज्जं, पमादो रक्खतो मलं।
verse.transliteration: Lāḷudāyīvatthu Asajjhāyamalā mantā, anuṭṭhānamalā gharā; Malaṁ vaṇṇassa kosajjaṁ, pamādo rakkhato malaṁ.
verse.translation: Not rehearsing is the stain of hymns. The stain of houses is neglect. Laziness is the stain of beauty. A guard’s stain is negligence.

meaning.overall: Not rehearsing is the stain of hymns. The stain of houses is neglect. Laziness is the stain of beauty. A guard’s stain is negligence.

verse.original: अञ्ञतरकुलपुत्तवत्थु मलित्थिया दुच्चरितं, मच्छेरं ददतो मलं; मला वे पापका धम्मा, अस्मिं लोके परम्हि च।
verse.transliteration: Aññatarakulaputtavatthu Malitthiyā duccaritaṁ, maccheraṁ dadato malaṁ; Malā ve pāpakā dhammā, asmiṁ loke paramhi ca.
verse.translation: Misconduct is a woman’s stain. A giver’s stain is stinginess. Bad qualities are a stain in this world and the next.

meaning.word_by_word: मलप्पदुसिरिया [मलप्पदुस-इरिया] (misconduct is the impurity), इत्थिया (of woman), मच्छेरा (stinginess), ददतो (of the giver), मलं (impurity); मला (impurities), वे (indeed), पापका (evil), धम्मा (qualities), अस्मिं (in this), लोके (world), परम्हि (in the next), च (and)
meaning.overall: Misconduct is a woman’s stain. A giver’s stain is stinginess. Bad qualities are a stain in this world and the next.

verse.original: ततो मला मलतरं, अविज्जा परमं मलं; एतं मलं पहन्त्वान, निम्मला होथ भिक्खवो।
verse.transliteration: Tato malā malataraṁ, avijjā paramaṁ malaṁ; Etaṁ malaṁ pahantvāna, nimmalā hotha bhikkhavo.
verse.translation: But a worse stain than these is ignorance, the worst stain of all. Having given up that stain, be without stains, mendicants!

meaning.word_by_word: ततो (than that), मला (impurity), मलतरं (greater impurity), अविज्जा (ignorance), परमं (supreme), मलं (impurity); एतं (this), मलं (impurity), पहत्वान (having abandoned), निम्मला (spotless/free from impurity), भवथ (become), भिक्खवो (O monks!)
meaning.overall: But a worse stain than these is ignorance, the worst stain of all. Having given up that stain, be without stains, mendicants!

verse.original: चूऌअसारिभिक्खुवत्थु सुजीवं अहिरिकेन, काकसूरेन धंसिना; पक्खन्दिना पगब्भेन, सङ्किलिट्ठेन जीवितं।
verse.transliteration: Cūḷasāribhikkhuvatthu Sujīvaṁ ahirikena, kākasūrena dhaṁsinā; Pakkhandinā pagabbhena, saṅkiliṭṭhena jīvitaṁ.
verse.translation: Life is easy for the shameless. With all the rude courage of a crow, they live pushy, rude, and corrupt.

meaning.word_by_word: सुजीवं (easy is life), अहिरिकेन (for the shameless), काकसूरेन (bold as a crow), धंसिना (backbiting/arrogant), पक्खन्धिना (boastful), पगब्भेन (impudent), सङ्किलिट्ठेन (corrupt)
meaning.overall: Life is easy for the shameless. With all the rude courage of a crow, they live pushy, rude, and corrupt.

verse.original: हिरीमता च दुज्जीवं, निच्चं सुचिगवेसिना; अलीनेनाप्पगब्भेन, सुद्धाजीवेन पस्सता।
verse.transliteration: Hirīmatā ca dujjīvaṁ, niccaṁ sucigavesinā; Alīnenāppagabbhena, suddhājīvena passatā.
verse.translation: Life is hard for the conscientious, always seeking purity, neither clinging nor rude, pure of livelihood and discerning.

meaning.overall: Life is hard for the conscientious, always seeking purity, neither clinging nor rude, pure of livelihood and discerning.

verse.original: पञ्चौपासकवत्थु यो पाणमतिपातेति, मुसावादञ्च भासति; लोके अदिन्नमादियति, परदारञ्च गच्छति।
verse.transliteration: Pañcaupāsakavatthu Yo pāṇamatipāteti, musāvādañca bhāsati; Loke adinnamādiyati, paradārañca gacchati.
verse.translation: Take anyone in this world who kills living creatures, speaks falsely, steals, commits adultery,

verse.original: सुरामेरयपानञ्च, यो नरो अनुयुञ्जति; इधेव मेसो लोकस्मिं, मूलं खणति अत्तनो।
verse.transliteration: Surāmerayapānañca, yo naro anuyuñjati; Idheva meso lokasmiṁ, mūlaṁ khaṇati attano.
verse.translation: and indulges in drinking beer and wine. Right here they dig up the root of their own self.

meaning.overall: and indulges in drinking beer and wine. Right here they dig up the root of their own self.

verse.original: एवं भो पुरिस जानाहि, पापधम्मा असञ्ञता; मा तं लोभो अधम्मो च, चिरं दुक्खाय रन्धयुं।

verse.original: तिस्सदहरवत्थु ददाति वे यथासद्धं, यथापसादनं जनो; तत्थ यो च मङ्कु भवति, परेसं पानभोजने; न सो दिवा वा रत्तिं वा, समाधिमधिगच्छति।
verse.transliteration: Tissadaharavatthu Dadāti ve yathāsaddhaṁ, yathāpasādanaṁ jano; Tattha yo ca maṅku bhavati, paresaṁ pānabhojane; Na so divā vā rattiṁ vā, samādhimadhigacchati.
verse.translation: The people give according to their faith, according to their confidence. If you get upset over that, over others’ food and drink, you’ll not, by day or by night, become immersed in samādhi.

meaning.overall: The people give according to their faith, according to their confidence. If you get upset over that, over others’ food and drink, you’ll not, by day or by night, become immersed in samādhi.

verse.original: यस्स चेतं समुच्छिन्नं, मूलघच्चं समूहतं; स वे दिवा वा रत्तिं वा, समाधिमधिगच्छति।
verse.transliteration: Yassa cetaṁ samucchinnaṁ, mūlaghaccaṁ samūhataṁ; Sa ve divā vā rattiṁ vā, samādhimadhigacchati.
verse.translation: Those who have cut that out, dug it up at the root, eradicated it, they will, by day or by night, become immersed in samādhi.

meaning.word_by_word: यस्स (for whom), चेतं [च एतं] (and this), समुच्छिन्नं (cut off), मूलघातकं [मूल-घच्चं] (uprooted/destroyed at the root), समूहतं (eradicated); स (he), वे (indeed), दिवा (by day), वा (or), रत्तिं (by night), वा (or), समाधिं (concentration), अधिगच्छति (attains)
meaning.overall: Those who have cut that out, dug it up at the root, eradicated it, they will, by day or by night, become immersed in samādhi.

verse.original: पञ्चौपासकवत्थु नत्थि रागसमो अग्गि, नत्थि दोससमो गहो; नत्थि मोहसमं जालं, नत्थि तण्हासमा नदी।
verse.transliteration: Pañcaupāsakavatthu Natthi rāgasamo aggi, natthi dosasamo gaho; Natthi mohasamaṁ jālaṁ, natthi taṇhāsamā nadī.
verse.translation: There is no fire like greed, no crime like hate, no net like delusion, no river like craving.

meaning.word_by_word: नत्थि (there is no), रागसमो (equal to lust), अग्गि (fire), नत्थि (there is no), दोससमो (equal to hatred), गहो (grip/seizure); नत्थि (there is no), मोहसमं (equal to delusion), जालं (net), नत्थि (there is no), तण्हासमो (equal to craving), नदी (river)
meaning.overall: There is no fire like greed, no crime like hate, no net like delusion, no river like craving.

verse.original: मेण्डकसेट्ठिवत्थु सुदस्सं वज्जमञ्ञेसं, अत्तनो पन दुद्दसं; परेसं हि सो वज्जानि, ओपुनाति यथा भुसं; अत्तनो पन छादेति, कलिंव कितवा सठो।
verse.transliteration: Meṇḍakaseṭṭhivatthu Sudassaṁ vajjamaññesaṁ, attano pana duddasaṁ; Paresaṁ hi so vajjāni, opunāti yathā bhusaṁ; Attano pana chādeti, kaliṁva kitavā saṭho.
verse.translation: It’s easy to see the faults of others, hard to see one’s own. For the faults of others are tossed high like hay, while one’s own are hidden, as a cheat hides a bad hand.

meaning.word_by_word: सुदस्सं (easy to see), वज्जं (fault), परेसं (of others), अत्तनो (one's own), पन (but), दुदस्सं (hard to see); परेसं (others'), हि (indeed), सो (he), वज्जानि (faults), ओपुणाति (winnows), यथा (like), भुसं (chaff); अत्तनो (one's own), पन (but), छादेति (hides), कलिंव [कलिं इव] (like a cheat), कितवा (a gambler), सठो (dishonest)
meaning.overall: It’s easy to see the faults of others, hard to see one’s own. For the faults of others are tossed high like hay, while one’s own are hidden, as a cheat hides a bad hand.

verse.original: उज्झानसञ्ञित्थेरवत्थु परवज्जानुपस्सिस्स, निच्चं उज्झानसञ्ञिनो; आसवा तस्स वड्ढन्ति, आरा सो आसवक्खया।
verse.transliteration: Ujjhānasaññittheravatthu Paravajjānupassissa, niccaṁ ujjhānasaññino; Āsavā tassa vaḍḍhanti, ārā so āsavakkhayā.
verse.translation: When you look for the flaws of others, always finding fault, your defilements only grow, you’re far from ending defilements.

meaning.word_by_word: परेसं (others'), अनुपस्सिस्स (of one who looks at/judges), निच्चं (always), उज्झानसञ्ञिनो (with thoughts of finding fault); आसवा (mental taints), तस्स (his), वड्ढन्ति (increase), आरा (far), सो (he), आसवक्खया (from destruction of taints)
meaning.overall: When you look for the flaws of others, always finding fault, your defilements only grow, you’re far from ending defilements.
