verse.original: सुभद्दपरिब्बाजकवत्थु आकासेव पदं नत्थि, समणो नत्थि बाहिरे; पपञ्चाभिरता पजा, निप्पपञ्चा तथागता।
verse.transliteration: Subhaddaparibbājakavatthu Ākāseva padaṁ natthi, samaṇo natthi bāhire; Papañcābhiratā pajā, nippapañcā tathāgatā.
verse.translation: In the atmosphere there is no track, there’s no true ascetic outside here. People enjoy proliferation, the Realized Ones are free of proliferation.

meaning.word_by_word: आकासेव [आकासे इव] (like in the sky), पदं (path/footprint), नत्थि (does not exist), समणो (ascetic), नत्थि (does not exist), बाहिरे (outside); पपञ्चाभिरता [पपञ्च-अभिरता] (delighting in worldly concepts/multiplicity), पजा (mass of people), निप्पपञ्चा (free from conceptual expansion), तथागता (the Tathagatas/Buddhas)
meaning.overall: In the atmosphere there is no track, there’s no true ascetic outside here. People enjoy proliferation, the Realized Ones are free of proliferation.

interpretation.traditional: In the Malavagga (Chapter on Impurities), the Buddha uses the metaphor of a silversmith refining silver to describe the gradual removal of mental impurities (malas). The greatest impurity is declared to be ignorance (avijjā). Traditional commentary stresses that just as rust eats away the iron from which it is born, a person's own bad deeds lead them to ruin.
interpretation.core_principle: Every action has consequences; the wise purify their minds gradually and follow the path of truth.
interpretation.psychological: Projection is the ego's defense mechanism: we focus on others' faults to avoid facing our own shadows. Honest self-reflection brings integration, allowing us to gradually dismantle our maladaptive behaviors.

explanation.simple: Clean your mind from bad habits bit by bit, just as a silversmith cleans silver. Focus on correcting your own mistakes rather than pointing out other people's faults.
explanation.deep: Chapter 18 is a call to continuous self-refinement. We are urged to establish a 'lamp for ourselves' before life reaches its end. The Buddha lists various impurities—laziness in home life, misconduct in relationships, stinginess in giving—and identifies ignorance as the root dross. The wise person inspects their own faults constantly, rather than winnowing others' faults like chaff.

logic.premise: Moral conduct and mindfulness are the foundations of mental purification.
logic.observation: Taming anger, removing impurities, and weighing decisions impartially lead to peace.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, one must consistently practice self-restraint and walk the Eightfold Path.

reasoning.flow: Stimulus → Mindfulness → Restraint of Senses → Pure Intention → Liberation

tension.human_doubt: How do I stay committed when the world constantly tempts my greed and anger?
tension.resolution: Recognize that temptation and anger are just passing states. Guard your mind like a fortress and find the inner refuge that never changes.

contrast.ignorance: Giving in to anger, projecting faults onto others, and neglecting self-improvement.
contrast.wisdom: Dismantling ego-attachments, practicing loving-kindness, and seeking the light of wisdom.

verses.analogy: Like a silversmith patiently removing dross to make silver pure and brilliant.
verses.real_life: A person who receives criticism calmly, examines their own behavior, and continues to practice patience daily.

actions: Review your own actions today, focusing solely on how you can improve rather than judging others.
Spend 10 minutes in quiet reflection on your habits, identifying one area that needs refining.
reflection_questions: Why is it easier for me to spot others' flaws than to admit my own?
What is the dross in my character that most needs refining right now?

perspectives.spiritual: The journey is one of gradual purification and complete awakening from ignorance.
perspectives.philosophical: True righteousness is objective, ethical, and aligned with cosmic truth (Dhamma).
perspectives.practical: Create quiet routines and keep supportive company to strengthen your character.
perspectives.leadership: A righteous leader is impartial, values transparency, and manages their own reactions before guiding others.

similar_ideas.idea: There is no purifier in this world comparable to transcendental knowledge.
Wisdom is the principal thing; therefore get wisdom: and with all thy getting get understanding.

verses.insight: Purity is not an status; it is a continuous refinement.

verse.original: आकासेव पदं नत्थि, समणो नत्थि बाहिरे; सङ्खारा सस्सता नत्थि, नत्थि बुद्धानमिञ्जितं। मलवग्गो अट्ठारसमो।
verse.transliteration: Ākāseva padaṁ natthi, samaṇo natthi bāhire; Saṅkhārā sassatā natthi, natthi buddhānamiñjitaṁ. Malavaggo aṭṭhārasamo.
verse.translation: In the atmosphere there is no track, there’s no true ascetic outside here. No conditions last forever, the Awakened Ones are not shaken.

meaning.word_by_word: आकासेव (like in the sky), पदं (path), नत्थि (does not exist), समणो (ascetic), नत्थि (does not exist), बाहिरे (outside); सङ्खारा (conditioned formations), सस्सता (eternal), नत्थि (do not exist), नत्थि (do not exist), बुद्धानं (of the Buddhas), इञ्जितं (perturbation/movement of ego)
meaning.overall: In the atmosphere there is no track, there’s no true ascetic outside here. No conditions last forever, the Awakened Ones are not shaken.

verse.original: खुद्दकनिकाय धम्मपद धम्मट्ठवग्ग विनिच्छयमहामत्तवत्थु न तेन होति धम्मट्ठो, येनत्थं साहसा नये; यो च अत्थं अनत्थञ्च, उभो निच्छेय्य पण्डितो।
verse.transliteration: Khuddakanikāya Dhammapada Dhammaṭṭhavagga Vinicchayamahāmattavatthu Na tena hoti dhammaṭṭho, yenatthaṁ sāhasā naye; Yo ca atthaṁ anatthañca, ubho niccheyya paṇḍito.
verse.translation: Minor Collection Sayings of the Dhamma 256–272 19. The Just You don’t become just by passing hasty judgment. An astute person evaluates both what is pertinent and what is irrelevant.

meaning.word_by_word: न (not), तेन (by that), होति (becomes), धम्मट्ठो (righteous/established in Dhamma), येनत्थं [येन अत्थं] (if by which the case), सहसा (arbitrarily/by violence), नये (one decides); यो (whoever), च (and), अत्थं (the case), अनत्थञ्-च (and what is not the case/wrong), उभो (both), निच्छेय्य (judges), पण्डितो (the wise person)
meaning.overall: One is not righteous because one decides a case with prejudice. The wise one who weighs both right and wrong is called righteous.

interpretation.traditional: In the Dhammatthavagga (The Righteous), the Buddha defines true justice and righteousness. A righteous judge (dhammaṭṭho) does not act in haste or out of bias (sahasā). They examine all evidence impartially, weighing both sides before rendering a decision.
interpretation.core_principle: True righteousness and justice require absolute impartiality, careful investigation, and intellectual honesty.
interpretation.psychological: Confirmation bias and emotional reactivity make us jump to conclusions ('sahasā'). Overriding these biases requires a cognitive pause to weigh all perspectives objectively.

explanation.simple: You are not a just or good person if you make decisions in a hurry or with bias. A wise person is one who carefully examines both sides of a matter before deciding.
explanation.deep: 'Sahasā' means with force, haste, or arbitrariness. When we decide a case (or judge a person) based on our preferences, fears, or first impressions, we violate Dhamma. The wise person ('paṇḍito') is willing to hold judgment in abeyance. They weigh 'attha' (the meaningful/right) and 'anattha' (the meaningless/wrong) with the precision of a scale. This is the integration of intellect and ethics.

logic.premise: Haste and prejudice distort truth and justice.
logic.observation: Righteousness requires seeing facts as they are, without personal bias.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, the wise person investigates thoroughly and remains impartial to maintain justice.

reasoning.flow: Encountering Dispute → Suspending Judgment → Investigating Both Sides → Objective Decision → Just Living

tension.human_doubt: Doesn't being impartial make me indecisive? Sometimes we must take a strong stand immediately.
tension.resolution: Impartiality is not indecision; it is the prerequisite for *correct* decision. A strong stand built on prejudice is destructive. Once the facts are clear, the wise person acts decisively in alignment with truth.

contrast.ignorance: Believing gossip, taking sides instantly, and refusing to listen to opposing viewpoints.
contrast.wisdom: Acknowledging that every story has multiple sides, and seeking objective reality before forming an opinion.

verses.analogy: Like a balanced scale that does not tip until the weights are placed fairly on both sides.
verses.real_life: A team leader who, when two employees have a conflict, sits down with each of them separately to listen to their accounts before taking action.

actions: The next time you hear a rumor or gossip today, refuse to accept it as truth until you have direct evidence.
Identify one strong opinion you hold and write down the best arguments for the opposite view.
reflection_questions: Where in my life am I judging others hastily or with bias?
How can I bring more intellectual honesty into my decisions?

perspectives.spiritual: Justice (dhamma) is the external manifestation of a mind free from aversion and delusion.
perspectives.philosophical: The golden mean and the Socratic pursuit of objective justice (dikaiosyne).
perspectives.practical: In business, perform thorough root-cause analysis rather than blaming individuals during a system failure.
perspectives.leadership: A just leader earns the trust of their organization by treating all members equitably and resolving conflicts transparently.

similar_ideas.idea: Understanding which knows what is to be done and what is not to be done, what is to be feared and what is not to be feared... is in the mode of goodness.
Do not judge by appearances, but judge with right judgment.

verses.insight: Truth does not take sides.

verse.original: असाहसेन धम्मेन, समेन नयती परे; धम्मस्स गुत्तो मेधावी, “धम्मट्ठो”ति पवुच्चति।
verse.transliteration: Asāhasena dhammena, samena nayatī pare; Dhammassa gutto medhāvī, “dhammaṭṭho”ti pavuccati.
verse.translation: A wise one judges others without haste, justly and impartially; that guardian of the law is said to be just.

meaning.word_by_word: असहसेन (non-arbitrarily/without violence), धम्मेन (with righteousness), समेन (impartially/equitably), नयती [नयति] (decides/leads), परे (others); धम्मरक्खो (guarded by Dhamma), मेधावी (wise), धम्मट्ठोति [धम्मट्ठो इति] (righteous thus), वुच्चति (is called)
meaning.overall: A wise one judges others without haste, justly and impartially; that guardian of the law is said to be just.

interpretation.traditional: In the Dhammaṭṭhavagga (Chapter on the Righteous), the Buddha defines what it means to be established in Dhamma. A righteous person is not defined by grey hair, eloquence, or a shaven head, but by non-harming, truthfulness, and impartiality. Traditional commentators explain that the true monk or elder is one who has discarded both merit and demerit, living in pure awareness.
interpretation.core_principle: Every action has consequences; the wise purify their minds gradually and follow the path of truth.
interpretation.psychological: Intellectual honesty and equity require cognitive flexibility and the willingness to override personal biases. Aligning our decisions with objective reality rather than self-interest builds integrity and trust.

explanation.simple: You are not wise because you talk a lot, or old because your hair is grey. A truly good person is one who is harmless, truthful, self-controlled, and treats everyone with justice.
explanation.deep: Chapter 19 dismantles the empty, ritualistic definitions of holiness. The Buddha insists that spirituality is a matter of character, not appearance. A true judge decides cases with equity and impartiality, not with prejudice. True wisdom is defined by fearlessness and friendliness, and the true monk is one who lives chaste and harmless towards all living beings.

logic.premise: Moral conduct and mindfulness are the foundations of mental purification.
logic.observation: Taming anger, removing impurities, and weighing decisions impartially lead to peace.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, one must consistently practice self-restraint and walk the Eightfold Path.

reasoning.flow: Stimulus → Mindfulness → Restraint of Senses → Pure Intention → Liberation

contrast.wisdom: Dismantling ego-attachments, practicing loving-kindness, and seeking the light of wisdom.

actions: Suspend judgment during a discussion today; listen carefully to all viewpoints before responding.
Speak only what is true and beneficial today, avoiding exaggeration.

How can I bring more equity and fairness into my daily decisions?

verse.original: छब्बग्गियवत्थु न तेन पण्डितो होति, यावता बहु भासति; खेमी अवेरी अभयो, “पण्डितो”ति पवुच्चति।
verse.transliteration: Chabbaggiyavatthu Na tena paṇḍito hoti, yāvatā bahu bhāsati; Khemī averī abhayo, “paṇḍito”ti pavuccati.
verse.translation: You’re not an astute scholar just because you speak a lot. One who is secure, free of enmity and fear, is said to be astute.

meaning.word_by_word: न (not), तेन (by that), पण्डितो (wise), होति (becomes), यावता (merely because), बहु (much), भासति (speaks); खेमी (peaceful), अवेरी (free from hatred), अभयो (fearless), पण्डितोति (wise thus), वुच्चति (is called)
meaning.overall: You’re not an astute scholar just because you speak a lot. One who is secure, free of enmity and fear, is said to be astute.

verse.original: एकुदानखीणासववत्थु न तावता धम्मधरो, यावता बहु भासति; यो च अप्पम्पि सुत्वान, धम्मं कायेन पस्सति; स वे धम्मधरो होति, यो धम्मं नप्पमज्जति।
verse.transliteration: Ekudānakhīṇāsavavatthu Na tāvatā dhammadharo, yāvatā bahu bhāsati; Yo ca appampi sutvāna, dhammaṁ kāyena passati; Sa ve dhammadharo hoti, yo dhammaṁ nappamajjati.
verse.translation: You’re not one who has memorized the teaching just because you recite a lot. Someone who directly sees the teaching after hearing only a little is truly one who has memorized the teaching, for they can never forget it.

meaning.word_by_word: न (not), तावता (merely by that), धम्मधरो (holder of Dhamma/expert in Dhamma), होति (becomes), यावता (merely because), बहु (much), भासति (speaks); यो (whoever), च (and), अप्पम्पि [अप्पं अपि] (even a little), सुत्वान (having heard), धम्मं (Dhamma), कायेन (by the body/experientially), पस्सति (sees); स (he), वे (indeed), धम्मधरो (holder of Dhamma), होति (becomes), यो (who), धम्मं (Dhamma), न (not), पमज्जति (does not neglect)
meaning.overall: You’re not one who has memorized the teaching just because you recite a lot. Someone who directly sees the teaching after hearing only a little is truly one who has memorized the teaching, for they can never forget it.

verse.original: लकुण्डकभद्दियत्थेरवत्थु न तेन थेरो सो होति, येनस्स पलितं सिरो; परिपक्को वयो तस्स, “मोघजिण्णो”ति वुच्चति।
verse.transliteration: Lakuṇḍakabhaddiyattheravatthu Na tena thero so hoti, yenassa palitaṁ siro; Paripakko vayo tassa, “moghajiṇṇo”ti vuccati.
verse.translation: You don’t become a senior by getting some grey hairs; for one ripe only in age, is said to have aged in vain.

meaning.word_by_word: न (not), तेन (by that), थेरो (elder), होति (becomes), येनस्स [येन अस्स] (because his), पलितं (grey), सिरो (head); परिपक्को (fully ripe/old), वयो (age), तस्स (his), मोघजिण्णोति [मोघ-जिण्णो इति] (aged in vain thus), वुच्चति (is called)
meaning.overall: You don’t become a senior by getting some grey hairs; for one ripe only in age, is said to have aged in vain.

verse.original: यम्हि सच्चञ्च धम्मो च, अहिंसा संयमो दमो; स वे वन्तमलो धीरो, “थेरो” इति पवुच्चति।
verse.transliteration: Yamhi saccañca dhammo ca, ahiṁsā saṁyamo damo; Sa ve vantamalo dhīro, “thero” iti pavuccati.
verse.translation: One who is truthful and principled, harmless, restrained, and self-controlled, attentive, purged of stains, is said to be a senior.

meaning.word_by_word: यम्हि [यस्मिं] (in whom), सच्चञ्-च (both truth and), धम्मो (righteousness/Dhamma), च (and), अहिंसा (non-harming), संयमो (restraint), दमो (taming/self-control); स (he), वे (indeed), वन्तमलो [वन्त-मलो] (who has spat out impurities), धीरो (wise), थेरोति (elder thus), वुच्चति (is called)
meaning.overall: One who is truthful and principled, harmless, restrained, and self-controlled, attentive, purged of stains, is said to be a senior.

interpretation.traditional: In the Dhammaṭṭhavagga (Chapter on the Righteous), the Buddha defines what it means to be established in Dhamma. A righteous person is not defined by grey hair, eloquence, or a shaven head, but by non-harming, truthfulness, and impartiality. Traditional commentators explain that the true monk or elder is one who has discarded both merit and demerit, living in pure awareness.
interpretation.core_principle: Every action has consequences; the wise purify their minds gradually and follow the path of truth.
interpretation.psychological: Intellectual honesty and equity require cognitive flexibility and the willingness to override personal biases. Aligning our decisions with objective reality rather than self-interest builds integrity and trust.

explanation.simple: You are not wise because you talk a lot, or old because your hair is grey. A truly good person is one who is harmless, truthful, self-controlled, and treats everyone with justice.
explanation.deep: Chapter 19 dismantles the empty, ritualistic definitions of holiness. The Buddha insists that spirituality is a matter of character, not appearance. A true judge decides cases with equity and impartiality, not with prejudice. True wisdom is defined by fearlessness and friendliness, and the true monk is one who lives chaste and harmless towards all living beings.

logic.premise: Moral conduct and mindfulness are the foundations of mental purification.
logic.observation: Taming anger, removing impurities, and weighing decisions impartially lead to peace.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, one must consistently practice self-restraint and walk the Eightfold Path.

reasoning.flow: Stimulus → Mindfulness → Restraint of Senses → Pure Intention → Liberation

tension.human_doubt: How do I stay committed when the world constantly tempts my greed and anger?
tension.resolution: Recognize that temptation and anger are just passing states. Guard your mind like a fortress and find the inner refuge that never changes.

contrast.ignorance: Giving in to anger, projecting faults onto others, and neglecting self-improvement.
contrast.wisdom: Dismantling ego-attachments, practicing loving-kindness, and seeking the light of wisdom.

verses.analogy: Like a silversmith patiently removing dross to make silver pure and brilliant.
verses.real_life: A person who receives criticism calmly, examines their own behavior, and continues to practice patience daily.

verse.original: सम्बहुलभिक्खुवत्थु न वाक्करणमत्तेन, वण्णपोक्खरताय वा; साधुरूपो नरो होति, इस्सुकी मच्छरी सठो।
verse.transliteration: Sambahulabhikkhuvatthu Na vākkaraṇamattena, vaṇṇapokkharatāya vā; Sādhurūpo naro hoti, issukī maccharī saṭho.
verse.translation: Not by mere enunciation, or a beautiful complexion does a person become holy, if they’re jealous, stingy, and devious.

meaning.word_by_word: न (not), वाक्करणमातेन [वाक्करण-मत्तेन] (merely by eloquence), वण्णपोक्खरताय [वण्ण-पोक्खरताय] (by beauty of complexion/form), वा (or); साधुरूपो (respectable/good man), नरो (man), होति (becomes), इस्सुकी (envious), मच्छरी (stingy), सठो (dishonest)
meaning.overall: Not by mere enunciation, or a beautiful complexion does a person become holy, if they’re jealous, stingy, and devious.

verse.original: यस्स चेतं समुच्छिन्नं, मूलघच्चं समूहतं; स वन्तदोसो मेधावी, “साधुरूपो”ति वुच्चति।
verse.transliteration: Yassa cetaṁ samucchinnaṁ, mūlaghaccaṁ samūhataṁ; Sa vantadoso medhāvī, “sādhurūpo”ti vuccati.
verse.translation: But if they’ve cut that out, dug it up at the root, eradicated it, that wise one, purged of vice, is said to be holy.

meaning.word_by_word: यस्स (for whom), चेतं (and this), समुच्छिन्नं (cut off), मूलघातकं (uprooted), समूहतं (eradicated); स (he), वन्तदोसो [वन्त-दोसो] (who has spat out anger/defilements), मेधावी (wise), साधुरूपोति (good man thus), वुच्चति (is called)
meaning.overall: But if they’ve cut that out, dug it up at the root, eradicated it, that wise one, purged of vice, is said to be holy.

verse.original: हत्थकवत्थु न मुण्डकेन समणो, अब्बतो अलिकं भणं; इच्छालोभसमापन्नो, समणो किं भविस्सति।
verse.transliteration: Hatthakavatthu Na muṇḍakena samaṇo, abbato alikaṁ bhaṇaṁ; Icchālobhasamāpanno, samaṇo kiṁ bhavissati.
verse.translation: A liar and breaker of vows is no ascetic just because they shave their head. How on earth can one be an ascetic who’s full of desire and greed?

meaning.word_by_word: न (not), मुण्डकेन (by a shaven head), समणो (ascetic), अब्बतो (without vows/undisciplined), अलिकं (lying), भणं (speaking); इच्छालोभसमापन्नो [इच्छा-लोभ-समापन्नो] (filled with desire and greed), समणो (ascetic), किं (how), भविस्सतिवे [भविस्सति एव] (will he be)
meaning.overall: A liar and breaker of vows is no ascetic just because they shave their head. How on earth can one be an ascetic who’s full of desire and greed?

verse.original: यो च समेति पापानि, अणुंथूलानि सब्बसो; समितत्ता हि पापानं, “समणो”ति पवुच्चति।
verse.transliteration: Yo ca sameti pāpāni, aṇuṁthūlāni sabbaso; Samitattā hi pāpānaṁ, “samaṇo”ti pavuccati.
verse.translation: One who stops all wicked deeds, great and small, because of stopping wicked deeds is said to be an ascetic.

meaning.word_by_word: यो (whoever), च (and), समेति (quiets/stills), पापानि (evils), अणुं (subtle), थूलानि [थूलानि] (and gross), च (and), सब्बसो (in every way); समितत्ता (by taming/stilling), हि (indeed), पापानं (of evils), समणोति (ascetic thus), वुच्चति (is called)
meaning.overall: One who stops all wicked deeds, great and small, because of stopping wicked deeds is said to be an ascetic.

verse.original: अञ्ञतरब्राह्मणवत्थु न तेन भिक्खु सो होति, यावता भिक्खते परे; विस्सं धम्मं समादाय, भिक्खु होति न तावता।
verse.transliteration: Aññatarabrāhmaṇavatthu Na tena bhikkhu so hoti, yāvatā bhikkhate pare; Vissaṁ dhammaṁ samādāya, bhikkhu hoti na tāvatā.
verse.translation: You don’t become a mendicant just by begging from others. One who has undertaken domestic duties has not yet become a mendicant.

meaning.word_by_word: न (not), तेन (by that), भिक्खु (monk), होति (becomes), यावता (merely because), पिण्डं (alms), याचति (begs), परे (from others); विस्सं (foul/impurity), धम्मं (nature/conduct), समादाय (having adopted), भिक्खु (monk), न (not), तावता (by that much), होति (becomes)
meaning.overall: You don’t become a mendicant just by begging from others. One who has undertaken domestic duties has not yet become a mendicant.

verse.original: योध पुञ्ञञ्च पापञ्च, बाहेत्वा ब्रह्मचरियवा; सङ्खाय लोके चरति, स वे “भिक्खू”ति वुच्चति।
verse.transliteration: Yodha puññañca pāpañca, bāhetvā brahmacariyavā; Saṅkhāya loke carati, sa ve “bhikkhū”ti vuccati.
verse.translation: But one living a spiritual life, who has banished both merit and evil, who wanders having appraised the world, is said to be a mendicant.

meaning.word_by_word: योध [यो इध] (who here), पुञ्ञञ्-च (both merit and), पापञ्-च (and evil), बाहेत्वा (having discarded), ब्रह्मचरियवा [ब्रह्मचरियवा] (living the holy life); सङ्खाय (with understanding/reflection), लोके (in the world), चरति (lives/wanders), स (he), वे (indeed), भिक्खूति (monk thus), चुस्तोद्य् (is called)
meaning.overall: But one living a spiritual life, who has banished both merit and evil, who wanders having appraised the world, is said to be a mendicant.

verse.original: तित्थियवत्थु न मोनेन मुनी होति, मूऌहरूपो अविद्दसु; यो च तुलंव पग्गय्ह, वरमादाय पण्डितो।
verse.transliteration: Titthiyavatthu Na monena munī hoti, mūḷharūpo aviddasu; Yo ca tulaṁva paggayha, varamādāya paṇḍito.
verse.translation: You don’t become a sage by being sagelike, while still confused and ignorant. The astute one who holds the scales, taking only the best,

meaning.word_by_word: मोनेन (by silence), मुनि (sage), न (not), होति (becomes), मूऌहरूपो (foolish form), अविद्दसु (ignorant); यो (whoever), च (and), तुलं (scale), व (like), पग्गय्ह (holding), वरम्-आदाय [वरम् इव आदाय] (having taken the noble/best), पण्डितो (the wise person)
meaning.overall: You don’t become a sage by being sagelike, while still confused and ignorant. The astute one who holds the scales, taking only the best,

interpretation.traditional: In the Dhammaṭṭhavagga (Chapter on the Righteous), the Buddha defines what it means to be established in Dhamma. A righteous person is not defined by grey hair, eloquence, or a shaven head, but by non-harming, truthfulness, and impartiality. Traditional commentators explain that the true monk or elder is one who has discarded both merit and demerit, living in pure awareness.
interpretation.core_principle: Every action has consequences; the wise purify their minds gradually and follow the path of truth.
interpretation.psychological: Intellectual honesty and equity require cognitive flexibility and the willingness to override personal biases. Aligning our decisions with objective reality rather than self-interest builds integrity and trust.

explanation.simple: You are not wise because you talk a lot, or old because your hair is grey. A truly good person is one who is harmless, truthful, self-controlled, and treats everyone with justice.
explanation.deep: Chapter 19 dismantles the empty, ritualistic definitions of holiness. The Buddha insists that spirituality is a matter of character, not appearance. A true judge decides cases with equity and impartiality, not with prejudice. True wisdom is defined by fearlessness and friendliness, and the true monk is one who lives chaste and harmless towards all living beings.

verse.original: पापानि परिवज्जेति, स मुनी तेन सो मुनि; यो मुनाति उभो लोके, “मुनि” तेन पवुच्चति।
verse.transliteration: Pāpāni parivajjeti, sa munī tena so muni; Yo munāti ubho loke, “muni” tena pavuccati.
verse.translation: and shunning the bad— that is a sage, <j>and that is how one becomes a sage. One who sagely weighs both in the world, is thereby said to be a sage.

meaning.word_by_word: पापानि (evils), परिवज्जेति (avoids), स (he), मुनि (is a sage), तेन (by that), सो (he), मुनि (is a sage); यो (whoever), मुनाति (weighs/judges), उभो (both), लोके (worlds/aspects), तेन (by that), मुनीति (sage thus), वुच्चति (is called)
meaning.overall: and shunning the bad— that is a sage, <j>and that is how one becomes a sage. One who sagely weighs both in the world, is thereby said to be a sage.

verse.original: बालिसिकवत्थु न तेन अरियो होति, येन पाणानि हिंसति; अहिंसा सब्बपाणानं, “अरियो”ति पवुच्चति।
verse.transliteration: Bālisikavatthu Na tena ariyo hoti, yena pāṇāni hiṁsati; Ahiṁsā sabbapāṇānaṁ, “ariyo”ti pavuccati.
verse.translation: You don’t become a noble one by harming living beings. One harmless towards all living beings is said to be a noble one.

meaning.word_by_word: न (not), तेन (by that), अरियो (noble), होति (becomes), येन (because), पाणानि (living beings), हिंसति (harms); अहिंसा (non-harming), सब्बपाणानं [सब्ब-पाणानं] (towards all living beings), अरियोति (noble thus), देवेति [वुच्चति] (is called)
meaning.overall: You don’t become a noble one by harming living beings. One harmless towards all living beings is said to be a noble one.

verse.original: सम्बहुलसीलादिसम्पन्नभिक्खुवत्थु न सीलब्बतमत्तेन, बाहुसच्चेन वा पन; अथ वा समाधिलाभेन, विवित्तसयनेन वा।
verse.transliteration: Sambahulasīlādisampannabhikkhuvatthu Na sīlabbatamattena, bāhusaccena vā pana; Atha vā samādhilābhena, vivittasayanena vā.
verse.translation: Not by precepts and observances, nor by much learning, nor by meditative immersion, nor by living in seclusion,

meaning.overall: Not by precepts and observances, nor by much learning, nor by meditative immersion, nor by living in seclusion,

verse.original: फुसामि नेक्खम्मसुखं, अपुथुज्जनसेवितं; भिक्खु विस्सासमापादि, अप्पत्तो आसवक्खयं। धम्मट्ठवग्गो एकूनवीसतिमो।
verse.transliteration: Phusāmi nekkhammasukhaṁ, aputhujjanasevitaṁ; Bhikkhu vissāsamāpādi, appatto āsavakkhayaṁ. Dhammaṭṭhavaggo ekūnavīsatimo.
verse.translation: do I experience the bliss of renunciation not frequented by ordinary people. A mendicant cannot rest confident without attaining the end of defilements.

meaning.word_by_word: फुसामि (I experience), नेक्खम्मसुखं [नेक्खम्म-सुखं] (joy of renunciation), अपुथुज्जनसेवितं [अपुथुज्जन-सेवितं] (not practiced by worldlings); भिक्खु (monk), विस्सास-म्-आपादि [विस्सासम् आपादि] (do not fall into confidence/complacency), अप्पत्तो (not having reached), आसवक्खयं (destruction of taints)
meaning.overall: do I experience the bliss of renunciation not frequented by ordinary people. A mendicant cannot rest confident without attaining the end of defilements.

verse.original: खुद्दकनिकाय धम्मपद मग्गवग्ग पञ्चसतभिक्खुवत्थु मग्गानट्ठङ्गिको सेट्ठो, सच्चानं चतुरो पदा; विरागो सेट्ठो धम्मानं, द्विपदानञ्च चक्खुमा।
verse.transliteration: Khuddakanikāya Dhammapada Maggavagga Pañcasatabhikkhuvatthu Maggānaṭṭhaṅgiko seṭṭho, saccānaṁ caturo padā; Virāgo seṭṭho dhammānaṁ, dvipadānañca cakkhumā.
verse.translation: Minor Collection Sayings of the Dhamma 273–289 20. The Path Of paths, the eightfold is the best; of truths, the four statements; dispassion is the best of things, and the Clear-eyed One is the best of humans.

meaning.word_by_word: मग्गानं (of paths), अट्ठङ्गिको (the eightfold), सेय्यो (is best), सच्चानं (of truths), चतुरो (four), पदा (lines/factors); विरागो (detachment), सेट्ठो (is supreme), धम्मानं (of states/teachings), द्विपदानञ्-च (and of two-legged beings), चक्खुमा (the one with vision/Buddha)
meaning.overall: Minor Collection Sayings of the Dhamma 273–289 20. The Path Of paths, the eightfold is the best; of truths, the four statements; dispassion is the best of things, and the Clear-eyed One is the best of humans.

interpretation.traditional: In the Maggavagga (Chapter on the Path), the Buddha outlines the Eightfold Path as the best of all paths, leading to the end of suffering. The chapter introduces the core Vipassana insights into impermanence, pain, and non-self. Traditional commentary emphasizes that while the Buddhas only point the way, the practitioner must do the work of walking it.
interpretation.core_principle: Every action has consequences; the wise purify their minds gradually and follow the path of truth.
interpretation.psychological: Existential freedom requires taking active responsibility for our choices. The path is a system of cognitive and behavioral practices that train the mind to stay anchored in presence, breaking the habits of anxiety and attachment.

explanation.simple: The Eightfold Path is the best way to peace. The Buddhas can only show you the way, but you must take the steps yourself. Understand that everything changes, and train your mind to let go of desire.
explanation.deep: Chapter 20 is the guide to liberation. It details the necessity of effort ('ātappaṁ') and mindfulness. The Buddha warns against laziness, which blocks the path to wisdom. He urges us to cut down the 'forest of desire' and cultivate the path of peace taught by the Happy One. Understanding the transience of all conditioned things is the gateway to purity.

verse.original: एसेव मग्गो नत्थञ्ञो, दस्सनस्स विसुद्धिया; एतञ्हि तुम्हे पटिपज्जथ, मारस्सेतं पमोहनं।
verse.transliteration: Eseva maggo natthañño, Dassanassa visuddhiyā; Etañhi tumhe paṭipajjatha, Mārassetaṁ pamohanaṁ.
verse.translation: This is the path, there is no other for the purification of vision. You all must practice this, it is the way to baffle Māra.

meaning.word_by_word: एषोव [एसो एव] (this indeed), मग्गो (is the path), नत्थञ्ञो [नत्थि अञ्ञो] (there is no other), दस्सनस्स (of vision), विसुद्धिया (for purification); एतञ्-हि (this indeed), तुम्हे (you), पटिपज्जथ (follow), मारस्सेतं [मारस्स एतं] (this is of Mara), पमोहनं (delusion/confusing path)
meaning.overall: This is the path, there is no other for the purification of vision. You all must practice this, it is the way to baffle Māra.

interpretation.traditional: In the Maggavagga (Chapter on the Path), the Buddha outlines the Eightfold Path as the best of all paths, leading to the end of suffering. The chapter introduces the core Vipassana insights into impermanence, pain, and non-self. Traditional commentary emphasizes that while the Buddhas only point the way, the practitioner must do the work of walking it.
interpretation.core_principle: Every action has consequences; the wise purify their minds gradually and follow the path of truth.
interpretation.psychological: Existential freedom requires taking active responsibility for our choices. The path is a system of cognitive and behavioral practices that train the mind to stay anchored in presence, breaking the habits of anxiety and attachment.

explanation.simple: The Eightfold Path is the best way to peace. The Buddhas can only show you the way, but you must take the steps yourself. Understand that everything changes, and train your mind to let go of desire.
explanation.deep: Chapter 20 is the guide to liberation. It details the necessity of effort ('ātappaṁ') and mindfulness. The Buddha warns against laziness, which blocks the path to wisdom. He urges us to cut down the 'forest of desire' and cultivate the path of peace taught by the Happy One. Understanding the transience of all conditioned things is the gateway to purity.

logic.premise: Moral conduct and mindfulness are the foundations of mental purification.
logic.observation: Taming anger, removing impurities, and weighing decisions impartially lead to peace.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, one must consistently practice self-restraint and walk the Eightfold Path.

reasoning.flow: Stimulus → Mindfulness → Restraint of Senses → Pure Intention → Liberation

tension.human_doubt: How do I stay committed when the world constantly tempts my greed and anger?
tension.resolution: Recognize that temptation and anger are just passing states. Guard your mind like a fortress and find the inner refuge that never changes.

contrast.ignorance: Giving in to anger, projecting faults onto others, and neglecting self-improvement.
contrast.wisdom: Dismantling ego-attachments, practicing loving-kindness, and seeking the light of wisdom.

verses.analogy: Like a silversmith patiently removing dross to make silver pure and brilliant.
verses.real_life: A person who receives criticism calmly, examines their own behavior, and continues to practice patience daily.

actions: Remind yourself today: 'This too shall change.' whenever you experience a victory or setback.
Exert clear, mindful effort in one task you have been avoiding out of laziness.
reflection_questions: Am I waiting for someone else to walk my path, or am I taking responsibility for my own steps?
What underbrush of desire am I currently allowing to grow in my mind?

perspectives.spiritual: The journey is one of gradual purification and complete awakening from ignorance.
perspectives.philosophical: True righteousness is objective, ethical, and aligned with cosmic truth (Dhamma).
perspectives.practical: Create quiet routines and keep supportive company to strengthen your character.
perspectives.leadership: A righteous leader is impartial, values transparency, and manages their own reactions before guiding others.

similar_ideas.idea: The mind is restless and hard to curb, but it can be controlled by practice.
Indeed, Allah will not change the condition of a people until they change what is in themselves.

verses.insight: The teacher points to the mountain, but you must climb it.

verse.original: एतञ्हि तुम्हे पटिपन्ना, दुक्खस्सन्तं करिस्सथ; अक्खातो वो मया मग्गो, अञ्ञाय सल्लकन्तनं।
verse.transliteration: Etañhi tumhe paṭipannā, dukkhassantaṁ karissatha; Akkhāto vo mayā maggo, aññāya sallakantanaṁ.
verse.translation: When you all are practicing this, you’ll make an end of suffering. I have explained the path to you for extracting the thorn with wisdom.

meaning.word_by_word: एतञ्-हि (this indeed), तुम्हे (you), पटिपन्ना (having followed), दुक्खस्सन्तं [दुक्खस्स अन्तं] (end of suffering), करिस्सथ (you will make); अक्खातो (declared), वो [वे] (indeed), मया (by me), मग्गो (path), अञ्ञाय (having understood), सल्लकन्तनं [सल्ल-सन्थनं/सल्लकत्तनं] (the removing of thorns/surgical extraction)
meaning.overall: When you all are practicing this, you’ll make an end of suffering. I have explained the path to you for extracting the thorn with wisdom.

verse.original: तुम्हेहि किच्चमातप्पं, अक्खातारो तथागता; पटिपन्ना पमोक्खन्ति, झायिनो मारबन्धना।
verse.transliteration: Tumhehi kiccamātappaṁ, akkhātāro tathāgatā; Paṭipannā pamokkhanti, jhāyino mārabandhanā.
verse.translation: You yourselves must do the work, the Realized Ones just show the way. Meditators practicing absorption are released from Māra’s bonds.

meaning.word_by_word: तुम्हेहि (by you), किच्चं (duty/striving), आतप्पं (exertion), अक्खातारो (teachers), तथागता (the Tathagatas/Buddhas); पटिपन्ना (those who enter the path), पमोक्खन्ति (will be freed), झायिनो (meditators), मारबन्धना (from the bonds of Mara)
meaning.overall: You yourselves must do the work, the Realized Ones just show the way. Meditators practicing absorption are released from Māra’s bonds.

verse.original: अनिच्चलक्खणवत्थु “सब्बे सङ्खारा अनिच्चा”ति, यदा पञ्ञाय पस्सति; अथ निब्बिन्दति दुक्खे, एस मग्गो विसुद्धिया।
verse.transliteration: Aniccalakkhaṇavatthu “Sabbe saṅkhārā aniccā”ti, yadā paññāya passati; Atha nibbindati dukkhe, esa maggo visuddhiyā.
verse.translation: All conditions are impermanent— when this is seen with wisdom, one grows disillusioned with suffering: this is the path to purity.

meaning.word_by_word: सब्बे (all), सङ्खारा (conditioned things), अनिच्चाति [अनिच्चा इति] (are impermanent thus), यदा (when), पञ्ञाय (with wisdom), पस्सति (sees); अथ (then), निब्बिन्दति (he tires/is disillusioned), दुक्खे (with suffering), एस (this), मग्गो (is the path), विसुद्धिया (to purity)
meaning.overall: All conditioned things are impermanent—when one sees this with wisdom, one tires of suffering. This is the path to purity.

interpretation.traditional: This is the first of three verses outlining the Three Marks of Existence (tilakkhana): impermanence (anicca), unsatisfactoriness (dukkha), and non-self (anatta). Seeing impermanence with direct insight leads to 'nibbidā' (disillusionment/detachment), which frees the mind from grasping.
interpretation.core_principle: Recognizing that all conditioned phenomena are temporary is the key to ending attachment and finding liberation.
interpretation.psychological: Grief and anxiety arise from trying to hold onto impermanent things. Accepting that change is the fundamental law of reality allows us to release control, reducing stress.

explanation.simple: Everything in the world is temporary and changes. When you see this clearly with wisdom, you will lose interest in the things that cause suffering. This is the path to peace.
explanation.deep: 'Sabbe saṅkhārā aniccā'—all constructed or conditioned things are in a constant state of flux. Nothing is solid. When this is seen not as a concept, but with direct vision ('paññāya passati'), the mind experiences 'nibbidā'—it tires of chasing bubbles. This is not depression; it is the relief of a child who realizes the movie monster is just a projection. Grasping stops. This release is the path to purity ('visuddhi').

logic.premise: All conditioned things are subject to change and decay.
logic.observation: Chasing and clinging to impermanent things inevitably produces suffering.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, seeing impermanence clearly leads to disillusionment with grasping, which is the path to freedom.

reasoning.flow: Contemplating Conditioned Things → Seeing Inevitable Change → Releasing Clinging → Experiencing Peace (purity)

tension.human_doubt: If everything is impermanent, isn't love also impermanent and therefore useless?
tension.resolution: No. Impermanence makes love more precious, not less. When you know a flower will fade, you appreciate its beauty more deeply in the present. Releasing the attachment to its survival allows you to love it without the anxiety of its loss.

contrast.ignorance: Living as if your wealth, status, body, and relationships will last forever, and being devastated when they change.
contrast.wisdom: Fully engaging with life's gifts while carrying a quiet, background awareness of their temporary nature.

verses.analogy: Like staying at a beautiful hotel—you enjoy the room and the views, but you do not redecorate or panic when it is time to check out.
verses.real_life: A person who is able to hold their grandfather's hand during his final hours with calm love, accepting death as the natural flow of life.

actions: Look at your most prized possession today and say: 'This is temporary. One day it will return to the elements.'
Notice how your thoughts arise, stay for a few seconds, and pass away on their own.
reflection_questions: What am I currently fighting that is simply the natural law of change?
How would my choices change if I truly integrated the reality of impermanence?

perspectives.spiritual: Anicca-anupassana (contemplation of impermanence) is the primary gateway to Vipassana (insight).
perspectives.philosophical: Heraclitus: 'Panta rhei' (everything flows). Nothing is, everything is becoming.
perspectives.practical: In business, design for change and adaptability; do not rely on static market conditions.
perspectives.leadership: A wise leader leads with the awareness that both organizational crises and victories are temporary, maintaining a steady, long-term vision.

similar_ideas.idea: The nonpermanent appearance of happiness and distress, and their disappearance in due course, are like the appearance and disappearance of winter and summer seasons. One must learn to tolerate them without being disturbed.
Is anyone afraid of change? Why, what can take place without change? Can you take a hot bath unless the firewood undergoes change?

verses.insight: Change is not the enemy of peace; it is the nature of life.

verse.original: दुक्खलक्खणवत्थु “सब्बे सङ्खारा दुक्खा”ति, यदा पञ्ञाय पस्सति; अथ निब्बिन्दति दुक्खे, एस मग्गो विसुद्धिया।
verse.transliteration: Dukkhalakkhaṇavatthu “Sabbe saṅkhārā dukkhā”ti, yadā paññāya passati; Atha nibbindati dukkhe, esa maggo visuddhiyā.

meaning.overall: All conditions are suffering— when this is seen with wisdom, one grows disillusioned with suffering: this is the path to purity.

verse.translation: All things are not-self— when this is seen with wisdom, one grows disillusioned with suffering: this is the path to purity.

meaning.word_by_word: सब्बे (all), धम्मा (things/phenomena), अनत्ताति [अनत्ता इति] (are non-self thus), यदा (when), पञ्ञाय (with wisdom), पस्सति (sees); अथ (then), निब्बिन्दति (he tires/is disillusioned), दुक्खे (with suffering), एस (this), मग्गो (is the path), विसुद्धिया (to purity)
meaning.overall: All things are not-self— when this is seen with wisdom, one grows disillusioned with suffering: this is the path to purity.

verse.original: पधानकम्मिकतिस्सत्थेरवत्थु उट्ठानकालम्हि अनुट्ठहानो, युवा बली आलसियं उपेतो; संसन्नसङ्कप्पमनो कुसीतो, पञ्ञाय मग्गं अलसो न विन्दति।
verse.transliteration: Padhānakammikatissattheravatthu Uṭṭhānakālamhi anuṭṭhahāno, Yuvā balī ālasiyaṁ upeto; Saṁsannasaṅkappamano kusīto, Paññāya maggaṁ alaso na vindati.
verse.translation: They don’t get going when it’s time to start; they’re young and strong, but given to sloth. Their mind depressed in sunken thought, lazy and slothful, they can’t discern the path.

meaning.word_by_word: उट्ठानकालम्हि (at the time of rising/exertion), अनुट्ठहानो [अन्-उट्ठहानो] (not exerting), युवा (young), बली (strong), अलस्यं (laziness), उप्ēतो (sunk in); संसन्नसङ्कप्पमनो [संसन्न-सङ्कप्प-मनो] (with depressed thoughts and mind), अलसवीरियो [अलस-वीरियो] (lazy in energy), पञ्ञाय (of wisdom), मग्गं (path), अलसो (the lazy one), न (not), विन्दति (finds)
meaning.overall: They don’t get going when it’s time to start; they’re young and strong, but given to sloth. Their mind depressed in sunken thought, lazy and slothful, they can’t discern the path.

verse.original: सूकरपेतवत्थु वाचानुरक्खी मनसा सुसंवुतो, कायेन च नाकुसलं कयिरा; एते तयो कम्मपथे विसोधये, आराधये मग्गमिसिप्पवेदितं।
verse.transliteration: Sūkarapetavatthu Vācānurakkhī manasā susaṁvuto, Kāyena ca nākusalaṁ kayirā; Ete tayo kammapathe visodhaye, Ārādhaye maggamisippaveditaṁ.
verse.translation: Guarded in speech, restrained in mind, doing no unskillful bodily deed. Purify these three ways of performing deeds, and win the path known to seers.

meaning.word_by_word: वाचा-अनुरक्खी [वाचानुरक्खी] (guarding speech), मनसा (in mind), सुसंवुतो (well-restrained), कायेन (in body), च (and), न (not), अकुसलं (unwholesome), कयिरा; एते (these), तयो (three), कम्मपथे (tracks of action), विसोधये (should purify), आराधये (should accomplish), मग्गं (path), इसिप्पवेदितं (declared by sages)
meaning.overall: Guarded in speech, restrained in mind, doing no unskillful bodily deed. Purify these three ways of performing deeds, and win the path known to seers.

verse.original: पोट्ठिलत्थेरवत्थु योगा वे जायती भूरि, अयोगा भूरिसङ्खयो; एतं द्वेधापथं ञत्वा, भवाय विभवाय च; तथात्तानं निवेसेय्य, यथा भूरि पवड्ढति।
verse.transliteration: Poṭṭhilattheravatthu Yogā ve jāyatī bhūri, ayogā bhūrisaṅkhayo; Etaṁ dvedhāpathaṁ ñatvā, bhavāya vibhavāya ca; Tathāttānaṁ niveseyya, yathā bhūri pavaḍḍhati.
verse.translation: From meditation springs wisdom, without meditation, wisdom ends. Knowing these two paths— of progress and decline— you should conduct yourself so that wisdom grows.

meaning.word_by_word: योगा (from application/devotion), वे (indeed), भूरी (wisdom), जायति (arises), अयोगा (from non-application), भूरीसङ्खयो [भूरी-सङ्खयो] (destruction of wisdom); एतं (this), द्वेधापथं [द्वेधा-पथं] (twofold path), ञत्वा (having known), भवाय (for growth), विभवाय (and decay), च (and); तथा (so), अत्तानं (oneself), निवेसेय्य (should establish), यथा (just as), भूरी (wisdom), वड्ढति (increases)
meaning.overall: From meditation springs wisdom, without meditation, wisdom ends. Knowing these two paths— of progress and decline— you should conduct yourself so that wisdom grows.

verse.original: पञ्चमहल्लकभिक्खुवत्थु वनं छिन्दथ मा रुक्खं, वनतो जायते भयं; छेत्वा वनञ्च वनथञ्च, निब्बना होथ भिक्खवो।
verse.transliteration: Pañcamahallakabhikkhuvatthu Vanaṁ chindatha mā rukkhaṁ, vanato jāyate bhayaṁ; Chetvā vanañca vanathañca, nibbanā hotha bhikkhavo.
verse.translation: Cut down the jungle, not just a tree; from the jungle springs fear. Having cut down jungle and snarl, be free of jungles, mendicants!

meaning.word_by_word: वनं (forest of desires), छिन्दथ (cut down), मा (not), रुक्खं (single tree), वनतो (from the forest), जायती (arises), भयं (fear); छेत्वा (having cut), वनञ्-च (both the forest and), वनथञ्-च (underbrush/desires), निब्बना [निब्बना] (free from forest/desires), भवाथ (become), भिक्खवो (O monks!)
meaning.overall: Cut down the jungle, not just a tree; from the jungle springs fear. Having cut down jungle and snarl, be free of jungles, mendicants!

verse.original: याव हि वनथो न छिज्जति, अणुमत्तोपि नरस्स नारिसु; पटिबद्धमनोव ताव सो, वच्छो खीरपकोव मातरि।
verse.transliteration: Yāva hi vanatho na chijjati, Aṇumattopi narassa nārisu; Paṭibaddhamanova tāva so, Vaccho khīrapakova mātari.
verse.translation: So long as the vine, no matter how small, that ties a man to women is not cut, his mind remains trapped, like a calf suckling its mother.

meaning.word_by_word: याव (as long as), वनथो (underbrush of desire), न (not), छिज्जति (is cut), अणुमत्तोपि [अणु-मत्तो अपि] (even a tiny bit), नरस्स (of a man), नारिसु (towards women); पटिबद्धमनोव [पटिबद्ध-मनो एव] (bound in mind indeed), ताव (so long), सो (he), वच्छो (calf), खीरपाकोव [खीर-पाको इव] (drinking milk indeed), मातरि (on the mother)
meaning.overall: So long as the vine, no matter how small, that ties a man to women is not cut, his mind remains trapped, like a calf suckling its mother.

verse.original: सुवण्णकारत्थेरवत्थु उच्छिन्द सिनेहमत्तनो, कुमुदं सारदिकंव पाणिना; सन्तिमग्गमेव ब्रूहय, निब्बानं सुगतेन देसितं।
verse.transliteration: Suvaṇṇakārattheravatthu Ucchinda sinehamattano, Kumudaṁ sāradikaṁva pāṇinā; Santimaggameva brūhaya, Nibbānaṁ sugatena desitaṁ.
verse.translation: Cut out affection for oneself, like plucking an autumn lotus. Foster only the path to peace, the extinguishment the Holy One taught.

meaning.word_by_word: उच्छिन्द (cut off), सिनेहम्-अत्तनो [सिनेहम् अत्तनो] (your own affection), कुमुदं (lotus), सारदिकं (autumnal), व (like), पाणिना (with the hand); सन्तिमग्ग-मेव [सन्ति-मग्गं एव] (the path of peace indeed), ब्रूहय (cultivate), निब्बानं (extinguishment), सुगतेन (by the Happy One), देसितं (taught)
meaning.overall: Cut out affection for oneself, like plucking an autumn lotus. Foster only the path to peace, the extinguishment the Holy One taught.

verse.original: महाधनवाणिजवत्थु इध वस्सं वसिस्सामि, इध हेमन्तगिम्हिसु; इति बालो विचिन्तेति, अन्तरायं न बुज्झति।
verse.transliteration: Mahādhanavāṇijavatthu Idha vassaṁ vasissāmi, idha hemantagimhisu; Iti bālo vicinteti, antarāyaṁ na bujjhati.
verse.translation: “Here I will stay for the rains; here for winter, here the summer”; thus the fool thinks, not realizing the danger.

meaning.word_by_word: इध (here), वस्सं (rainy season), वसिस्सामि (I will dwell), इध (here), हेमन्तगिम्हिसु [हेमन्त-गिम्हिसु] (in winter and summer); इति (thus), बालो (the fool), विचिन्तेति (thinks), अन्तरायं (danger/death), न (not), बुज्झति (realizes)
meaning.overall: “Here I will stay for the rains; here for winter, here the summer”; thus the fool thinks, not realizing the danger.

verse.original: किसागोतमीवत्थु तं पुत्तपसुसम्मत्तं, ब्यासत्तमनसं नरं; सुत्तं गामं महोघोव, मच्चु आदाय गच्छति।
verse.transliteration: Kisāgotamīvatthu Taṁ puttapasusammattaṁ, byāsattamanasaṁ naraṁ; Suttaṁ gāmaṁ mahoghova, maccu ādāya gacchati.
verse.translation: As a mighty flood sweeps away a sleeping village, death steals away a man who dotes on children and cattle, his mind caught up in them.

meaning.word_by_word: तं (him), पुत्तपसुसम्मत्तं [पुत्त-पसु-सम्मत्तं] (doting on children and cattle), ब्यासत्तमनसं [ब्यासत्त-मनसं] (with clinging mind), नरं (man); सुत्तं (sleeping), गामं (village), महोघोवेर [महा-ओघो इव] (like a great flood), मच्चु (death), आदाय (having taken), गोग्ग्लेस् (goes/sweeps away)
meaning.overall: As a mighty flood sweeps away a sleeping village, death steals away a man who dotes on children and cattle, his mind caught up in them.

interpretation.traditional: In the Maggavagga (Chapter on the Path), the Buddha outlines the Eightfold Path as the best of all paths, leading to the end of suffering. The chapter introduces the core Vipassana insights into impermanence, pain, and non-self. Traditional commentary emphasizes that while the Buddhas only point the way, the practitioner must do the work of walking it.
interpretation.core_principle: Every action has consequences; the wise purify their minds gradually and follow the path of truth.
interpretation.psychological: Existential freedom requires taking active responsibility for our choices. The path is a system of cognitive and behavioral practices that train the mind to stay anchored in presence, breaking the habits of anxiety and attachment.

explanation.simple: The Eightfold Path is the best way to peace. The Buddhas can only show you the way, but you must take the steps yourself. Understand that everything changes, and train your mind to let go of desire.
explanation.deep: Chapter 20 is the guide to liberation. It details the necessity of effort ('ātappaṁ') and mindfulness. The Buddha warns against laziness, which blocks the path to wisdom. He urges us to cut down the 'forest of desire' and cultivate the path of peace taught by the Happy One. Understanding the transience of all conditioned things is the gateway to purity.

logic.premise: Moral conduct and mindfulness are the foundations of mental purification.
logic.observation: Taming anger, removing impurities, and weighing decisions impartially lead to peace.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, one must consistently practice self-restraint and walk the Eightfold Path.

reasoning.flow: Stimulus → Mindfulness → Restraint of Senses → Pure Intention → Liberation

tension.human_doubt: How do I stay committed when the world constantly tempts my greed and anger?
tension.resolution: Recognize that temptation and anger are just passing states. Guard your mind like a fortress and find the inner refuge that never changes.

contrast.ignorance: Giving in to anger, projecting faults onto others, and neglecting self-improvement.
contrast.wisdom: Dismantling ego-attachments, practicing loving-kindness, and seeking the light of wisdom.

verse.original: पटाचारावत्थु न सन्ति पुत्ता ताणाय, न पिता नापि बन्धवा; अन्तकेनाधिपन्नस्स, नत्थि ञातीसु ताणता।
verse.transliteration: Paṭācārāvatthu Na santi puttā tāṇāya, na pitā nāpi bandhavā; Antakenādhipannassa, natthi ñātīsu tāṇatā.
verse.translation: Children provide you no shelter, nor does father, nor relatives. When you’re seized by the terminator, there’s no shelter in family.

meaning.word_by_word: न (not), सन्ति (exist), पुत्ता (sons), ताणाय (for protection), न (not), पिता (father), न (not), पि (also), बन्धवा (relatives); अन्तकेनाधिपन्नस्स [अन्तकेन अधिपन्नस्स] (for one overcome by the Ender/Death), नत्थि (there is no), ञातीसु (among relatives), ताणता (protection)
meaning.overall: Children provide you no shelter, nor does father, nor relatives. When you’re seized by the terminator, there’s no shelter in family.

verse.original: एतमत्थवसं ञत्वा, पण्डितो सीलसंवुतो; निब्बानगमनं मग्गं, खिप्पमेव विसोधये। मग्गवग्गो वीसतिमो।
verse.transliteration: Etamatthavasaṁ ñatvā, paṇḍito sīlasaṁvuto; Nibbānagamanaṁ maggaṁ, khippameva visodhaye. Maggavaggo vīsatimo.
verse.translation: Knowing the reason for this, astute, and ethically restrained, one would quickly clear the path that leads to extinguishment.

meaning.word_by_word: एतम्-अत्थ-वसं [एतम् अत्थ-वसं] (this significance), ञत्वा (having known), पण्डितो (the wise person), सीलसंवुतो (restrained by virtue); निब्बानगमनं [निब्बान-गमनं] (leading to Nibbana), मग्गं (path), खिप्पम्-एव [खिप्पम् एव] (very quickly), सोधये (should cleanse)
meaning.overall: Knowing the reason for this, astute, and ethically restrained, one would quickly clear the path that leads to extinguishment.

verse.original: खुद्दकनिकाय धम्मपद पकिण्णकवग्ग अत्तनोपुब्बकम्मवत्थु मत्तासुखपरिच्चागा, पस्से चे विपुलं सुखं; चजे मत्तासुखं धीरो, सम्पस्सं विपुलं सुखं।
verse.transliteration: Khuddakanikāya Dhammapada Pakiṇṇakavagga Attanopubbakammavatthu Mattāsukhapariccāgā, passe ce vipulaṁ sukhaṁ; Caje mattāsukhaṁ dhīro, sampassaṁ vipulaṁ sukhaṁ.
verse.translation: Minor Collection Sayings of the Dhamma 290–305 21. Miscellaneous If by giving up material happiness one sees abundant happiness, the attentive would give up material happiness, seeing the abundant happiness.

meaning.word_by_word: मात्तासुख-परिच्चागा [मात्ता-सुख-परिच्चागा] (by giving up a lesser happiness), चे (if), पस्सेय्य (one would see), विपुलं (greater/abundant), सुखं (happiness); चजे (should give up), मात्तासुखं (lesser happiness), धीरो (the wise person), सम्पस्सं (beholding/regarding), विपुलं (greater), सुखं (happiness)
meaning.overall: If by giving up a lesser happiness one finds a greater happiness, the wise person should give up the lesser, beholding the greater.

interpretation.traditional: Theravada commentary describes this as the core principle of renunciation (nekkhamma). The wise willingly sacrifice immediate, transient, sensory pleasure (māttā-sukha) to obtain the vast, unconditioned peace of Nibbana (vipula-sukha).
interpretation.core_principle: True wisdom involves trading short-term gratification for long-term spiritual growth and lasting peace.
interpretation.psychological: Delayed gratification is a primary indicator of emotional intelligence and cognitive maturity. Overriding immediate impulses to secure a much larger future benefit reduces long-term anxiety and builds self-efficacy.

explanation.simple: If giving up a small pleasure helps you find a much bigger happiness, then be wise and let the small one go to get the greater one.
explanation.deep: We often clutch onto 'māttāsukhaṁ'—the tiny, fleeting happiness of sensory stimulation, comfort, and ego validation—because it is immediate. The Buddha invites us to develop a strategic spiritual vision ('sampassaṁ'—beholding clearly). When we weigh the temporary comfort of a bad habit against the vast, stable peace of a clear mind ('vipulaṁ sukhaṁ'), the trade becomes obvious. Renunciation is not loss; it is a highly profitable exchange.

logic.premise: Short-term pleasure and long-term well-being are often in conflict.
logic.observation: The wise person has the capacity to see the superior value of long-term peace.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, the wise person willingly surrenders temporary gratification to secure lasting happiness.

reasoning.flow: Immediate Pleasure Present → Recognizing the Cost → Beholding the Greater Peace → Releasing the Lesser → Gaining the Greater

tension.human_doubt: Doesn't this mean I can never enjoy the present moment? Am I always supposed to postpone joy for the future?
tension.resolution: No. The 'greater happiness' is available *in the present* once the agitation of craving is removed. Letting go of the lesser pleasure actually reveals the spacious joy of presence, which is immediate and unconditioned, unlike sensory pleasure which depends on external triggers.

contrast.ignorance: Chasing instant gratification (dopamine hits) at the expense of your mental clarity, relationships, and long-term peace.
contrast.wisdom: Choosing to sit in meditation, exercise, or study rather than scrolling on social media or indulging in habits you will regret.

verses.analogy: Like a child who drops a small piece of copper because someone offers them a solid bar of gold.
verses.real_life: An addict who endures the temporary discomfort of withdrawal because they value the greater happiness of a sober, self-determined life.

actions: Identify one immediate comfort (like a sweet snack or digital distraction) and choose to skip it today to preserve your focus.
Spend 10 minutes meditating, consciously trading busywork for inner quiet.
reflection_questions: What small happiness am I currently clutching that is preventing a greater peace from entering my life?
How can I strengthen my vision of the 'greater gold' when temptation arises?

perspectives.spiritual: The transition from sensory attachment (kama-chanda) to spiritual joy (dhamma-pramojja).
perspectives.philosophical: Rational utility: sacrificing the lower value for the sake of the higher value (Platonic hierarchy of pleasure).
perspectives.practical: Apply the principle of leverage: automate or cut away low-value tasks to free up energy for high-impact creative work.
perspectives.leadership: A leader must sacrifice short-term quarterly metrics (lesser happiness) to build a sustainable, ethical, long-term organizational culture (greater happiness).

similar_ideas.idea: That which in the beginning may be just like poison but at the end is like nectar... is said to be happiness in the mode of goodness.
Set aside the pleasures that lead to regret; seek the joy that remains constant.

verses.insight: Drop the glass beads to pick up the diamonds.

verse.original: कुक्कुटण्डखादिकावत्थु परदुक्खूपधानेन, अत्तनो सुखमिच्छति; वेरसंसग्गसंसट्ठो, वेरा सो न परिमुच्चति।
verse.transliteration: Kukkuṭaṇḍakhādikāvatthu Paradukkhūpadhānena, attano sukhamicchati; Verasaṁsaggasaṁsaṭṭho, verā so na parimuccati.
verse.translation: Seeking their own happiness by imposing suffering on others, intimate with enmity, they’re not freed from enmity.

meaning.word_by_word: परसुख-उपधानेन (by causing pain to others), अत्तनो (one's own), सुखम्-इच्छति (wishes for happiness); वेरसंसग्गसम्सट्ठो [वेर-संसग्ग-सम्सट्ठो] (entangled in bonds of hatred), वेरा (from hatred), सो (he), न (not), पमुच्चति (is freed)
meaning.overall: Seeking their own happiness by imposing suffering on others, intimate with enmity, they’re not freed from enmity.

interpretation.traditional: In the Pakiṇṇakavagga (Chapter on Miscellaneous), the Buddha collects various teachings on delayed gratification, the danger of neglecting duty, and the power of mindfulness. Traditional Theravada commentary highlights that the disciples of the Buddha are 'happily awake' (suppabuddhaṁ) because they keep their minds constantly anchored in the Buddha, Dhamma, Sangha, non-harming, and meditation.
interpretation.core_principle: Every action has consequences; the wise purify their minds gradually and follow the path of truth.
interpretation.psychological: Constant redirection of attention to positive anchors (mindfulness of body, compassion, and teachings) trains the brain to ignore task-irrelevant distractions, reducing cognitive load and anxiety.

explanation.simple: Train your mind to be awake and alert. Think about what is good, practice kindness, and do not waste your time on things that don't help you grow. Focus on your duty.
explanation.deep: Chapter 21 is a mosaic of practical wisdom. It establishes that to find greater happiness, one must willingly surrender the lesser. It also warns against arrogance and neglecting what should be done. The core of the chapter is the description of the 'awake' ones who maintain continuous mindfulness, directing their attention away from worldly distractions and towards inner cultivation.

logic.premise: Moral conduct and mindfulness are the foundations of mental purification.
logic.observation: Taming anger, removing impurities, and weighing decisions impartially lead to peace.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, one must consistently practice self-restraint and walk the Eightfold Path.

reasoning.flow: Stimulus → Mindfulness → Restraint of Senses → Pure Intention → Liberation

tension.human_doubt: How do I stay committed when the world constantly tempts my greed and anger?
tension.resolution: Recognize that temptation and anger are just passing states. Guard your mind like a fortress and find the inner refuge that never changes.

contrast.ignorance: Giving in to anger, projecting faults onto others, and neglecting self-improvement.
contrast.wisdom: Dismantling ego-attachments, practicing loving-kindness, and seeking the light of wisdom.

verses.analogy: Like a silversmith patiently removing dross to make silver pure and brilliant.
verses.real_life: A person who receives criticism calmly, examines their own behavior, and continues to practice patience daily.

actions: Keep your mind anchored in a positive thought or breath whenever you are waiting in line today.
Perform your tasks today with active diligence, ignoring the urge to procrastinate.
reflection_questions: What am I currently neglecting in my personal or spiritual development?
How can I help my mind stay 'happily awake' throughout the day?

Wisdom is the principal thing; therefore get wisdom: and with all thy getting get understanding.

verses.insight: Mindfulness is the thread that keeps the scattered mind together.

verse.original: भद्दियानंभिक्खूनंवत्थु यञ्हि किच्चं अपविद्धं, अकिच्चं पन करीयति; उन्नऌआनं पमत्तानं, तेसं वड्ढन्ति आसवा।
verse.transliteration: Bhaddiyānaṁbhikkhūnaṁvatthu Yañhi kiccaṁ apaviddhaṁ, akiccaṁ pana karīyati; Unnaḷānaṁ pamattānaṁ, tesaṁ vaḍḍhanti āsavā.
verse.translation: They dump what should be done, and do what should not be done. For the insolent and the negligent, their defilements only grow.

meaning.word_by_word: यञ्-हि (what indeed), किच्चं (duty/what should be done), तदपविद्धं [तत् अपविद्धं] (that is cast aside/neglected), अकिच्चं (what should not be done), पन (but), करीयति (is done); उन्नऌआनं (of the arrogant), पमत्तानं (of the negligent), वड्ढन्ति (increase), तेसं (their), आसवा (mental taints)
meaning.overall: They dump what should be done, and do what should not be done. For the insolent and the negligent, their defilements only grow.

interpretation.traditional: In the Pakiṇṇakavagga (Chapter on Miscellaneous), the Buddha collects various teachings on delayed gratification, the danger of neglecting duty, and the power of mindfulness. verse.original: येसञ्च सुसमारद्धा, निच्चं कायगता सति; अकिच्चं ते न सेवन्ति, किच्चे सातच्चकारिनो; सतानं सम्पजानानं, अत्थं गच्छन्ति आसवा।
verse.transliteration: Yesañca susamāraddhā, niccaṁ kāyagatā sati; Akiccaṁ te na sevanti, kicce sātaccakārino; Satānaṁ sampajānānaṁ, atthaṁ gacchanti āsavā.
verse.translation: Those that have properly undertaken constant mindfulness of the body, don’t cultivate what should not be done, but always do what should be done. Mindful and aware, their defilements come to an end.

meaning.word_by_word: येसञ्-च (for whom indeed), सुसमारद्धा (well and constantly practiced), निच्चं (always), कायगतासति [कायगता सति] (mindfulness of the body); अकिच्चं (what should not be done), ते (they), न (not), सेवन्ति (do not practice), किच्चे (in duty), सातच्चकारिनो (living with perseverance); सतानं (of the mindful), सम्पजानानं (of the clear-comprehending), अत्थं (destruction), गच्छन्ति (go), आसवा (mental taints)
meaning.overall: Those that have properly undertaken constant mindfulness of the body, don’t cultivate what should not be done, but always do what should be done. Mindful and aware, their defilements come to an end.

interpretation.traditional: In the Pakiṇṇakavagga (Chapter on Miscellaneous), the Buddha collects various teachings on delayed gratification, the danger of neglecting duty, and the power of mindfulness. Traditional Theravada commentary highlights that the disciples of the Buddha are 'happily awake' (suppabuddhaṁ) because they keep their minds constantly anchored in the Buddha, Dhamma, Sangha, non-harming, and meditation.
interpretation.core_principle: Every action has consequences; the wise purify their minds gradually and follow the path of truth.
interpretation.psychological: Constant redirection of attention to positive anchors (mindfulness of body, compassion, and teachings) trains the brain to ignore task-irrelevant distractions, reducing cognitive load and anxiety.

explanation.simple: Train your mind to be awake and alert. Think about what is good, practice kindness, and do not waste your time on things that don't help you grow. Focus on your duty.
explanation.deep: Chapter 21 is a mosaic of practical wisdom. It establishes that to find greater happiness, one must willingly surrender the lesser. It also warns against arrogance and neglecting what should be done. The core of the chapter is the description of the 'awake' ones who maintain continuous mindfulness, directing their attention away from worldly distractions and towards inner cultivation.

logic.premise: Moral conduct and mindfulness are the foundations of mental purification.
logic.observation: Taming anger, removing impurities, and weighing decisions impartially lead to peace.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, one must consistently practice self-restraint and walk the Eightfold Path.

reasoning.flow: Stimulus → Mindfulness → Restraint of Senses → Pure Intention → Liberation

tension.human_doubt: How do I stay committed when the world constantly tempts my greed and anger?
tension.resolution: Recognize that temptation and anger are just passing states. Guard your mind like a fortress and find the inner refuge that never changes.

contrast.ignorance: Giving in to anger, projecting faults onto others, and neglecting self-improvement.
contrast.wisdom: Dismantling ego-attachments, practicing loving-kindness, and seeking the light of wisdom.

verses.analogy: Like a silversmith patiently removing dross to make silver pure and brilliant.
verses.real_life: A person who receives criticism calmly, examines their own behavior, and continues to practice patience daily.

verse.original: लकुण्डकभद्दियवत्थु मातरं पितरं हन्त्वा, राजानो द्वे च खत्तिये; रट्ठं सानुचरं हन्त्वा, अनीघो याति ब्राह्मणो।
verse.transliteration: Lakuṇḍakabhaddiyavatthu Mātaraṁ pitaraṁ hantvā, rājāno dve ca khattiye; Raṭṭhaṁ sānucaraṁ hantvā, anīgho yāti brāhmaṇo.
verse.translation: Having slain mother and father, and two aristocratic kings, and having wiped out <j>the kingdom with its tax collector, the brahmin walks on untroubled.

meaning.overall: Having slain mother and father, and two aristocratic kings, and having wiped out <j>the kingdom with its tax collector, the brahmin walks on untroubled.

verse.original: मातरं पितरं हन्त्वा, राजानो द्वे च सोत्थिये; वेयग्घपञ्चमं हन्त्वा, अनीघो याति ब्राह्मणो।
verse.transliteration: Mātaraṁ pitaraṁ hantvā, rājāno dve ca sotthiye; Veyagghapañcamaṁ hantvā, anīgho yāti brāhmaṇo.
verse.translation: Having slain mother and father, and two prosperous kings, and a tiger as the fifth, the brahmin walks on untroubled.

meaning.overall: Having slain mother and father, and two prosperous kings, and a tiger as the fifth, the brahmin walks on untroubled.

verse.original: दारुसाकटिकपुत्तवत्थु सुप्पबुद्धं पबुज्झन्ति, सदा गोतमसावका; येसं दिवा च रत्तो च, निच्चं बुद्धगता सति।
verse.transliteration: Dārusākaṭikaputtavatthu Suppabuddhaṁ pabujjhanti, sadā gotamasāvakā; Yesaṁ divā ca ratto ca, niccaṁ buddhagatā sati.
verse.translation: The disciples of Gotama always wake up refreshed, who day and night constantly recollect the Buddha.

meaning.word_by_word: सुप्पबुद्धं (happily awake), पबुज्झन्ति (they awake), सदा (always), गोतमसावका (disciples of Gautama); येसं (whose), दिवा (by day), च (and), रत्तिञ्-च (and by night), निच्चं (always), बुद्धगता (centered on the Buddha), सति (mindfulness)
meaning.overall: The disciples of Gotama always wake up refreshed, who day and night constantly recollect the Buddha.

verse.original: सुप्पबुद्धं पबुज्झन्ति, सदा गोतमसावका; येसं दिवा च रत्तो च, अहिंसाय रतो मनो।
verse.transliteration: Suppabuddhaṁ pabujjhanti, sadā gotamasāvakā; Yesaṁ divā ca ratto ca, ahiṁsāya rato mano.
verse.translation: The disciples of Gotama always wake up refreshed, whose minds day and night delight in harmlessness.

meaning.word_by_word: सुप्पबुद्धं (happily awake), पबुज्झन्ति (they awake), सदा (always), गोतमसावका (disciples of Gautama); येसं (whose), दिवा (by day), च (and), रत्तिञ्-च (and by night), निच्चं (always), अहिंसाय (in non-harming), रमति (delights), मनो (mind)
meaning.overall: The disciples of Gotama always wake up refreshed, whose minds day and night delight in harmlessness.

interpretation.traditional: In the Pakiṇṇakavagga (Chapter on Miscellaneous), the Buddha collects various teachings on delayed gratification, the danger of neglecting duty, and the power of mindfulness. Traditional Theravada commentary highlights that the disciples of the Buddha are 'happily awake' (suppabuddhaṁ) because they keep their minds constantly anchored in the Buddha, Dhamma, Sangha, non-harming, and meditation.
interpretation.core_principle: Every action has consequences; the wise purify their minds gradually and follow the path of truth.
interpretation.psychological: Constant redirection of attention to positive anchors (mindfulness of body, compassion, and teachings) trains the brain to ignore task-irrelevant distractions, reducing cognitive load and anxiety.

explanation.simple: Train your mind to be awake and alert. Think about what is good, practice kindness, and do not waste your time on things that don't help you grow. Focus on your duty.
explanation.deep: Chapter 21 is a mosaic of practical wisdom. It establishes that to find greater happiness, one must willingly surrender the lesser. It also warns against arrogance and neglecting what should be done. The core of the chapter is the description of the 'awake' ones who maintain continuous mindfulness, directing their attention away from worldly distractions and towards inner cultivation.

logic.observation: Taming anger, removing impurities, and weighing decisions impartially lead to peace.
logic.conclusion: Therefore, one must consistently practice self-restraint and walk the Eightfold Path.

reasoning.flow: Stimulus → Mindfulness → Restraint of Senses → Pure Intention → Liberation

verse.original: वज्जिपुत्तकभिक्खुवत्थु दुप्पब्बज्जं दुरभिरमं, दुरावासा घरा दुखा; दुक्खोसमानसंवासो, दुक्खानुपतितद्धगू; तस्मा न चद्धगू सिया, न च दुक्खानुपतितो सिया।
verse.transliteration: Vajjiputtakabhikkhuvatthu Duppabbajjaṁ durabhiramaṁ, Durāvāsā gharā dukhā; Dukkhosamānasaṁvāso, Dukkhānupatitaddhagū; Tasmā na caddhagū siyā, Na ca dukkhānupatito siyā.
verse.translation: Going forth is hard, it’s hard to be happy; life at home is hard too, and painful, it’s painful to stay when you’ve nothing in common. A traveler is a prey to pain, so don’t be a traveler, don’t be prey to pain.

meaning.word_by_word: दुप्पब्बज्जं (difficult is the life of a monk/renunciation), दुरभिरमं (difficult to enjoy), दुरावासा (difficult and painful is home life), घरा (houses), दुक्खा (painful); दुक्खो (painful), असमान-संवासो (association with those who are not compatible), दुक्खानुपतितद्धिनो [दुक्खा-अनुपतित-अद्धिनो] (sorrow pursues the wanderer/traveler); तस्मा (therefore), न (not), चद्धी [च अद्धी] (be a wanderer), सिया (should be), न (not), च (and), दुक्खानुपतितो (pursued by sorrow), सिया (should be)
meaning.overall: Going forth is hard, it’s hard to be happy; life at home is hard too, and painful, it’s painful to stay when you’ve nothing in common. A traveler is a prey to pain, so don’t be a traveler, don’t be prey to pain.

verse.original: चित्तगहपतिवत्थु सद्धो सीलेन सम्पन्नो, यसोभोगसमप्पितो; यं यं पदेसं भजति, तत्थ तत्थेव पूजितो।
verse.transliteration: Cittagahapativatthu Saddho sīlena sampanno, yasobhogasamappito; Yaṁ yaṁ padesaṁ bhajati, tattha tattheva pūjito.
verse.translation: One who is faithful, accomplished in ethics, blessed with fame and wealth, is honored in whatever place they frequent.

meaning.word_by_word: सद्धो (endowed with faith), सीलेन (with virtue), सम्पन्नो (endowed), यसोभोगसमप्पितो [यसो-भोग-समप्पितो] (endowed with reputation and wealth); यं यं (whatever), देसं (country/place), भजति (he frequents), तत्थ तत्थेव [तत्थ तत्थ एव] (there indeed), पूजितो (he is honored)
meaning.overall: One who is faithful, accomplished in ethics, blessed with fame and wealth, is honored in whatever place they frequent.

verse.original: चूऌअसुभद्दावत्थु दूरे सन्तो पकासेन्ति, हिमवन्तोव पब्बतो; असन्तेत्थ न दिस्सन्ति, रत्तिं खित्ता यथा सरा।
verse.transliteration: Cūḷasubhaddāvatthu Dūre santo pakāsenti, himavantova pabbato; Asantettha na dissanti, rattiṁ khittā yathā sarā.
verse.translation: The good shine from afar, like the Himalayan peaks, but the wicked are not seen, like arrows scattered in the night.

meaning.overall: The good shine from afar, like the Himalayan peaks, but the wicked are not seen, like arrows scattered in the night.

verse.original: एकविहारित्थेरवत्थु एकासनं एकसेय्यं, एको चरमतन्दितो; एको दमयमत्तानं, वनन्ते रमितो सिया। पकिण्णकवग्गो एकवीसतिमो।
verse.transliteration: Ekavihārittheravatthu Ekāsanaṁ ekaseyyaṁ, eko caramatandito; Eko damayamattānaṁ, vanante ramito siyā. Pakiṇṇakavaggo ekavīsatimo.
verse.translation: Sitting alone, sleeping alone, tirelessly wandering alone; one who tames themselves alone would delight within a forest.

meaning.word_by_word: एकासनं (sitting alone), एकसेय्यं (sleeping alone), एकचरियं (wandering alone), अतन्दितो (unwearied); एको (alone), दमयम्-अत्तानं [दमयम् अत्तनो] (taming oneself), वनन्ते (in the edge of the forest), रमितो (delights), सिया (should be)
meaning.overall: Sitting alone, sleeping alone, tirelessly wandering alone; one who tames themselves alone would delight within a forest.
